credit: Google Arts & Culture — LwFch0SXuzBAIw Tate Images ( http://www.tate-images.com/results.asp?image=N00476&wwwflag=3&imagepos=1 )

title: Willows beside a Stream
description: A screen of willows leans over still water in an unforced piece of Thames-side observation. It belongs to the group of small oil sketches Turner kept in his studio rather than exhibiting.

title: Windsor Castle from the Thames
description: The royal castle rides above the river on its chalk bluff, painted from Turner's boat on one of his summer Thames campaigns. Sky and water are set down in long, wet sweeps that leave the paint visibly mobile.

title: Linlithgow Palace
description: The ruined Stewart royal palace above its loch in West Lothian, drawn from Turner's Scottish sketching tours undertaken for engraved topographical series. Soft watercolour washes blend the masonry into the surrounding landscape.
credit: SQGDHWt5XOVyOQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Newark Abbey
description: The Augustinian ruin on the Wey in Surrey, its broken walls reflected in still water. Turner mined such Gothic remains for the picturesque subjects favoured by Romantic patrons and the engraving trade.
credit: NwGAR5f7wgk8Pw at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Peter on the sea
description: Christ stretches out his hand to a sinking Peter on the Sea of Galilee, the figures reduced to clear, near-archaic outlines. Runge's interpretation of the Gospel episode reflects his ambition to fuse landscape, religion, and Romantic symbolism.

title: Shipping at the Mouth of the Thames
description: Working vessels labouring in the Thames estuary chop, sails braced against a low, weather-laden sky. The painting belongs to Turner's productive run of estuary subjects answering his rivalry with the marine tradition of van de Velde.
credit: Google Arts & Culture — qgGcWeWQCxwJMQ Tate Images ( http://www.tate-images.com/results.asp?image=N02702&wwwflag=3&imagepos=1 )

title: The Battle of Trafalgar
description: A close-quarters view of Nelson's flagship Victory at Trafalgar, taken from the mizen-shrouds rather than the more conventional broadside vantage. Turner had boarded Victory on her return to the Medway and used the sketches he made there to anchor the smoke-wreathed scene.

title: The Victory Returning from Trafalgar
description: Nelson's flagship shown in three rotated views entering Sheerness with the admiral's body aboard, after Turner sketched her at anchor. The triple study reflects his marine fascination and the national mourning that followed Trafalgar.
credit: PgH780ODwlK-ng at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Walton bridges
description: Cattle wade in the Thames below the timber arches of Walton Bridges, drawn from Turner's sketching campaigns along the river. The picture marks his transition from topographical watercolour into open-air oil painting.

title: Weir and Cattle
description: Cattle wade in the slow water below a country weir in one of Turner's pastoral Thames-valley sketches. The thin oil handling and silvery palette recall his admiration for Cuyp.

title: Cassiobury Park: Reaping
description: Harvesters work an open field on the Earl of Essex's estate at Cassiobury in Hertfordshire. The painting was a private commission produced alongside a series of watercolours of the park. It shows Turner extending his Claudean pastoral mode to a contemporary English country-house setting.

title: Cliveden on Thames
description: A Thames-side prospect of the wooded Cliveden cliffs in Buckinghamshire, the river curling beneath in pale silver. Turner repeatedly returned to the Thames as a quintessentially English subject, here softening topography into atmospheric haze.
credit: Google Arts & Culture — AAF7MnUg-TIdUQ Tate Images ( http://www.tate-images.com/results.asp?image=N01180&wwwflag=3&imagepos=1 )

title: Cross in the Mountains
description: A wayside crucifix stands atop a fir-clad alpine peak, gilded by rays of an unseen setting sun. Originally an altarpiece for the Tetschen chapel, the work provoked the so-called Ramdohr controversy by daring to treat pure landscape as a vehicle of religious revelation.
credit: 1. The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 . 2. nevsepic.com.ua

title: Newark Abbey on the River Wey
description: The ruined Augustinian priory of Newark stands above the still waters of the Wey, framed by trees and scattered cattle. Turner adopts the picturesque vocabulary of the topographical watercolorists, softened by an early painterly attention to reflected light.

title: The Junction of the Thames and the Medway
description: An estuary scene with merchantmen and a small boat tossed in choppy water where the two rivers meet. Turner builds the squally light from layered glazes, showing his early debt to Dutch marine painters.

title: The Thames near Windsor
description: A quiet reach of the upper Thames painted from the boat Turner had fitted out as a floating studio. The fluid handling belongs to the Thames oil sketches in which he loosened his manner before the public ever saw it.

title: Tree Tops and Sky
description: Most of the panel is given over to evening sky, with foliage massed at the lower edge and Guildford Castle barely indicated. The composition foreshadows Turner's later willingness to let weather dominate topography.

title: Vue du lac de Challes et du Mont Blanc
description: A Savoyard panorama of Lake Challes with Mont Blanc rising in the distance, unusual within the painter's output for being a pure landscape rather than a portrait. The view records her travels through the Alps during her long emigration from revolutionary France.
credit: Minneapolis Institute of Art, online collection

title: Oedipus and the Sphinx
description: Oedipus pauses on a rocky ledge to answer the riddle of the Sphinx, his polished nude profile set against deep shadow. Painted in Rome as part of Ingres's Prix de Rome obligations, it distils his Neoclassical doctrine of pure contour and idealised anatomy.

title: Portrait of Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
description: A sober three-quarter portrait of Goethe at fifty-nine, painted in Weimar by the Dresden academician Gerhard von Kügelgen. The poet wears civil dress against a plain dark ground, the smooth Neoclassical handling presenting him as statesman of letters rather than Romantic genius.

title: Purfleet and the Essex Shore as seen from Long Reach
description: Sailing barges and Thames hoys ride the broad estuary off the chalk cliffs of Purfleet, the low Essex shore stretching beyond. The watercolor belongs to Turner's early topographical phase, when accurate maritime detail was still the foundation of his atmospheric effects.

title: Sheerness as seen from the Nore
description: Naval shipping anchored at the Nore sandbank off the Kent dockyard, with rigging and bunting catching wet wind. Turner exhibited the canvas at his own gallery, courting comparison with Dutch marine masters in scale and atmosphere.

title: The Colossus
dateCreated: after 1808 Romanticism (second half of 18 th century –first half of 19 th century )
description: A naked giant rises behind a mountain ridge as a panicked crowd and herds flee across the plain below. Long attributed to Goya and tied to the Peninsular War, the picture stages collective terror with the bituminous darkness of his late, troubled imagination.

title: The Confluence of the Thames and Medway
description: Estuary shipping gathers where Thames and Medway meet, painted in the tradition of seventeenth-century Dutch sea pieces. Heavy cloud and a low horizon underline Turner's command of English coastal weather.

title: The Large Bather
description: Painted in Rome as one of Ingres's required envois to the Académie, the canvas presents a seated nude from behind, head wrapped in a striped turban. The smooth contour of the back, lifted to the surface as pure drawing, became a touchstone for the artist's later Turkish bath subjects.

provenance.collection: Department of Paintings of the Louvre

title: The Monk by the Sea
description: A solitary figure stands on a pale dune before an immense, near-empty expanse of sea and sky. Friedrich strips the composition to horizontal bands, dwarfing the human silhouette to evoke spiritual confrontation with the infinite — a foundational image of German Romantic landscape.

title: The painter and writer Friedrich August von Klinkowström
description: A portrait of the painter and writer Friedrich August von Klinkowström, Runge's close friend and a fellow figure in early German Romantic circles. The sitter is shown with a Nazarene-like clarity of contour and a steady, contemplative gaze.

title: The Painter Caspar David Friedrich
description: A bust-length likeness of Caspar David Friedrich painted by his close friend in Dresden. Kügelgen catches the Romantic landscapist with sharp side-lit features and inward gaze, fixing the public image of the painter at the height of his early career.
credit: 1. allposters.com 2. Bildindex der Kunst und Architektur : object 20146333 – image file XKH141499a.jpg 3. Bridgeman Art Library : Object 141499

title: The Siege of Cozel
description: A panoramic battle scene of the 1807 siege of Cosel in Silesia, Bavarian troops repulsing a Prussian sortie across an open foreground while the fortress smokes in the distance. Kobell, court painter in Munich, here works in the large-format Schlachtenbild tradition, organising hundreds of small figures within a clear, brightly lit landscape.
credit: 1. The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 . 2. Bildergipfel

title: The Third of May 1808
description: A Spanish patriot in a luminous white shirt flings out his arms before the firing squad of Napoleonic troops on Príncipe Pío hill. Commissioned after the French withdrawal, Goya treats martyrdom with the brutal lighting and crude paint of modern history painting.
credit: This file was derived from: El Tres de Mayo, by Francisco de Goya, from Prado in Google Earth.jpg :

title: Fishing upon the Blythe-Sand
description: Estuarine fishermen at low water on the Thames, the horizon line dropped to expose a vast, muted sky. The subdued grey-and-cream palette reflects Turner's study of seventeenth-century Dutch marine painters such as van de Velde.
credit: Google Arts & Culture — MQG2rOTbb5MaqQ Tate Images ( http://www.tate-images.com/results.asp?image=N00496&wwwflag=3&imagepos=1 )

title: London from Greenwich Park
description: A panoramic survey of the capital seen from the hill at Greenwich, with the Queen's House and Wren's Royal Hospital below and St Paul's hazed in coal-smoke distance. The painting belongs to a series celebrating the Thames as the artery of empire.
credit: Google Arts & Culture — IAHskvl8KuvdYw Tate Images ( http://www.tate-images.com/results.asp?image=N00483&wwwflag=3&imagepos=1 )

title: Philipp Otto Runge
description: A preparatory drawing in chalk and pencil for the unfinished Great Morning, mapping out the central goddess-figure rising amid lilies and infant spirits. The sheet shows Runge's diagrammatic approach to his cosmological Times of Day cycle.

title: The Abbey in the Oakwood
description: A procession of monks bears a coffin into the snow-rimed ruin of a Gothic abbey, the gnarled oaks around them silhouetted against a pale winter dusk. Friedrich exhibited the canvas in Berlin alongside Mönch am Meer as a Romantic meditation on death and resurrection.

title: The Garreteer’s Petition
description: A poet implores his muse in a cramped attic, an unusually literary genre subject for Turner. Exhibited at the Royal Academy with verses of his own composition, it shows him courting the hierarchy of history painting.

title: The Great Morning
description: A detail from the unfinished Great Morning, showing the radiant central figure Runge devised as the embodiment of dawn within his Times of Day cycle. The hard-edged drawing and saturated colour reveal the artist's mystical, almost emblematic Romanticism.
credit: Web Gallery of Art : Image Info about artwork wga QS:P11807,"r/runge/2morning"

title: The Sun Rising through Vapour
description: Turner's marine pairs working fishermen and beached fish with a low sun burning through coastal mist. Held by the Barber Institute, the picture exemplifies his early atmospheric naturalism, in which form is dissolved by light and humidity rather than drawn line.

title: The Trout Stream
description: An angler casts beside a wooded English river in a pastoral key drawn from Claude Lorrain. Painted in Turner's early Thames-and-Wales manner, the composition balances feathery foliage against a glassy reach of water.

title: Thomson's Aeolian Harp
description: A pastoral elegy for the poet James Thomson, with classical figures grouped on a hillside above the Thames at Richmond. Turner pairs the picture with verses of his own, blending Claudean idyll and literary tribute.

title: Grande Odalisque
dateCreated: 1810 Romanticism (second half of 18 th century –first half of 19 th century )
description: A reclining concubine turns her head over a famously elongated back -- Ingres lengthened her spine by several vertebrae in pursuit of an ideal sinuous line. Commissioned by Caroline Murat for the Naples court, the picture was scandalised at the 1819 Salon for its anatomical liberties.

title: Petworth House from the Lake: Dewy Morning
description: A view across the Earl of Egremont's lake, painted under the patronage that gave Turner the run of Petworth. Cool morning haze dissolves park, water and sky into the unified atmosphere typical of his Sussex landscapes.

title: Philipp Otto Runge
description: A black and white chalk drawing of the young Sophia Sieveking laid out in death, her features composed against a pale pillow. Runge made the sheet shortly before his own early death, lending its mortuary stillness an autobiographical weight.

title: Shipwreck of the Minotaur
description: A naval transport founders amid towering green-grey waves, lifeboats overloaded with survivors swept up the swells. Turner makes the wreck of HMS Minotaur an early demonstration of his lifelong subject of nature's indifferent force at sea.

title: The Fish Market at Hastings Beach
description: Sussex fisherfolk hauling their catch onto the shingle, with luggers and chalk cliffs receding behind. Turner repeatedly painted Hastings during the Napoleonic years when its beach was both a working port and a fashionable resort.
credit: rQED3TH4yBQtgg at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Aristotle
description: The Venetian Romantic Hayez imagines the philosopher in a sober half-length portrait, draped and meditative, painted with the polished historicism that made him the painter of Risorgimento-era Italy. The work hangs in the Gallerie dell'Accademia, the city where Hayez trained.

title: Saltash with the Water Ferry
credit: This file was donated to Wikimedia Commons as part of a project by the Metropolitan Museum of Art . See the Image and Data Resources Open Access Policy

title: Virgil reading The Aeneid before Augustus
description: An academic history piece showing Virgil reciting the Aeneid before the imperial family at the moment Octavia faints at the mention of her dead son Marcellus. The carefully balanced antique architecture and the chiselled outlines announce Ingres's lifelong allegiance to Davidian Neoclassicism.
credit: Photothèque Musée des Augustins, Toulouse Photo :Daniel Martin

title: Colonel Alastair Ranaldson Macdonell of Glengarry
description: The Highland chieftain stands armed in full Glengarry tartan against a heroic landscape, every weapon and badge of clan pride on display. Raeburn's broad, direct brushwork supplies the swagger that Macdonell himself cultivated as a self-styled embodiment of clan tradition.
credit: GgEDEPJ-LXa7Dg at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Teignmouth Harbour
description: Vessels lie at anchor under the red Devon cliffs of the Teign estuary, a coast Turner sketched repeatedly on his West Country tours. The watercolour links to his preparatory work for the Southern Coast engravings.

title: The Charging Chasseur
dateCreated: 1812 Romanticism (second half of 18 th century –first half of 19 th century )
description: A mounted officer of Napoleon's Imperial Guard wheels his rearing chestnut horse against a smoke-streaked sky. Géricault's Salon debut at twenty-one announced a new Romantic energy in French painting, with Rubensian colour and a kinetic diagonal that broke decisively from Davidian decorum.

title: Gothic Cathedral by the Water
description: A vast Gothic cathedral towers over a riverside town at sunset, its silhouette dwarfing the mortal traffic on the quay. The motif of the imagined medieval Dom appealed to Romantic painters as both architectural dream and patriotic emblem after the Napoleonic Wars.

title: Morning
description: Dawn light spreads across a placid river valley as a small boat puts out from the shore. Although the file references Schinkel, the work belongs to the meditative landscape mode of early-nineteenth-century German Romanticism, where the simple act of daybreak carries the weight of spiritual allegory.

title: View of Mont Blanc" Alternative title: " Blick auf den Mont Blanc aus einem Hochgebirgstal mit Bauernhof im Vordergrund " and " Landschaft im Charakter des Montblanc
description: Better remembered as Berlin's neoclassical architect, Schinkel was also a Romantic landscape painter. Here a high Alpine valley with a humble farmstead is dwarfed by the snow mass of Mont Blanc, the staffage and outbuildings supplying scale for the sublime geology beyond.

title: Apullia in Search of Appullus
description: An early classical landscape by J. M. W. Turner, exhibited at the British Institution and pointedly modelled on Claude Lorrain to challenge the older master's reputation. Sunlit foliage, distant temples and a literary Ovidian subject mark Turner's ambition to inherit the grand European pastoral tradition.

title: The Second of May 1808 or The Charge of the Mamelukes
description: Madrileños with knives drag Mamluk cavalrymen of the imperial guard from their rearing horses in the Puerta del Sol. Companion to The Third of May, Goya's painting reconstructs the chaos of the popular uprising against Napoleon's occupation in tumbling diagonals.

title: Gothic Church on a Rock by the Sea
description: A spired Gothic cathedral rises atop a cliff above the sea, more vision than topography. The motif - architecture as sacred outcropping - belongs to the shared Romantic imagination of Schinkel and Friedrich, where the medieval church stands in for a yearned-for unity of faith, nation and landscape.

title: Medieval City on a River
description: An imagined medieval city rises in tiers of Gothic spires above a river, lit by a low theatrical sun. Schinkel uses architecture as nationalist allegory in the wake of the Napoleonic wars, the cathedral silhouette standing in for a recovered German past.

title: The Eruption of the Soufrière Mountains in the Island of St Vincent
description: A Caribbean volcano detonates against the night, fountains of incandescent ash silhouetting palms and figures along the shore. Worked from eyewitness reports of the 1812 eruption, the picture exemplifies the early nineteenth-century taste for geological catastrophe.

title: Flatford Mill
description: A view from a footbridge on the Stour, with a barge being unhitched in the foreground beneath open Suffolk sky. Painted largely en plein air on Constable's family land, the picture exemplifies the truth-to-nature creed that underpinned his six large 'Stour' canvases.

title: Maria Bicknell
description: An intimate portrait of the painter's fiancée, completed shortly before their long-resisted marriage. Constable, primarily a landscapist, treats her face with a reserved tenderness, the muted palette and soft modelling reflecting the private nature of the commission.

title: The Temple of Jupiter Panellenius Restored
description: A reconstructed view of the Doric temple on Aegina, imagined intact above the Aegean, painted from sketches supplied by an architect-traveller as Turner himself never visited Greece. The canvas answered the British vogue for Hellenic antiquity stoked by the Elgin Marbles.

title: Wivenhoe Park
description: A panoramic estate view commissioned by Major-General Francis Slater-Rebow, balancing fishermen, swans, a grazing herd, and the distant house. Constable expanded the canvas at the patron's request to include a lawn and outbuildings, evidence of his pragmatic handling of the country-house genre.
credit: vAG0ovJU1U65Rw at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Raby Castle
description: A house portrait commissioned by the third Earl of Darlington, set in his County Durham deer park with a hunt in the foreground. Turner devotes the upper canvas to a churning, dramatic sky, signalling his shift from topography to Romantic landscape.
credit: Walters Art Museum : Home page Info about artwork

title: The hiker above the sea of fog
description: Seen from behind on a rocky outcrop, the wanderer surveys a swirling fog that swallows the peaks below. Friedrich's signature Rückenfigur device invites the viewer into the figure's vantage, making the landscape an interior as much as a topography of the Elbe Sandstone Mountains.
credit: The photographic reproduction was done by Cybershot800i . ( Diff )

title: Chalk Cliffs at Ruegen
description: Three figures peer over the dizzying white precipices of Rügen onto the Baltic far below, framed by an arch of beech branches. Painted shortly after Friedrich's honeymoon, the picture invests a real coastal panorama with Romantic allegories of marriage, faith, and mortality.
credit: The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 .

title: Chelsea Pensioners Reading the Waterloo Dispatch
description: Veterans cluster outside a Chelsea pub as a despatch announcing the victory at Waterloo is read aloud. Commissioned by the Duke of Wellington, Wilkie filled the genre tableau with regimental specificity, prompting crowd-control rails when it was shown at the Royal Academy in 1822.

title: Dort or Dordrecht: The Dort Packet-Boat from Rotterdam Becalmed
description: A glassy Dutch harbour bathed in golden morning light, paying open homage to Aelbert Cuyp, whose paintings Turner studied on his 1817 Low Countries tour. The becalmed packet boat anchors a luminous sky that occupies more than half the canvas.
credit: 4AFo7BatGeF12Q at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Evening: Landscape with an Aqueduct
description: A monumental Roman aqueduct strides across an imagined Italianate landscape at dusk, with bathers and travellers dwarfed by its arches. Painted for a Parisian patron's hôtel particulier, the picture shows Géricault adopting the heroic, Poussin-derived classical landscape mode at the threshold of Romanticism.

title: Gate in the Rocks
description: A natural rock arch frames a distant view, conceived as the Nordic pendant to a sunlit Italian landscape. The pairing stages a Romantic dialogue between the sublime German wilderness and the classical south, a polarity that runs through the era's painting and through Schinkel's own architectural imagination.
credit: Web Gallery of Art : Image Info about artwork wga QS:P11807,"/s/schinkel/gaterock"

title: Heroic Landscape with Fishermen
description: A Romantic landscape in the grand tradition, dwarfing fishermen beneath looming cliffs and gathering storm clouds. Gericault works the scene in dark earth tones, drawing on Poussin and Salvator Rosa to fuse classical composition with fervent natural drama.
credit: Web Gallery of Art : Image Info about artwork wga QS:P11807,"g/gericaul/1/104gerig"

title: The Field of Waterloo
description: Night falls on the battlefield as women search the heaped dead by torchlight, three years after the engagement. Turner had walked the ground himself; the picture quotes Byron's Childe Harold and refuses any patriotic triumph.

title: The Penny Wedding
description: A crowded Scottish wedding feast unfolds in a low-beamed interior, fiddler at the front and dancers, drinkers, and gossips crammed across the canvas. Wilkie's careful Dutch-inspired genre painting helped popularize sentimental rural Scottishness for a Regency audience.

title: The Raft of the Medusa
description: Survivors of the wrecked French frigate Méduse strain toward a distant ship, piling living and dead bodies into a single rising pyramid. Géricault interviewed actual survivors and modelled corpses in his studio for this Salon-shaking indictment of incompetent royalist command, now anchoring the Louvre's Romanticism galleries.

provenance.collection: Department of Paintings of the Louvre

title: Angelica
description: A study sheet from Ingres's mature years preserved at La Piscine museum in Roubaix, related to his Angelica compositions. The drawing shows his disciplined pencil contour and obsessive return to the same Orlando Furioso motif over decades.

title: Portrait Study of a Youth
description: A Romantic head study of a long-haired youth, painted in the years surrounding the Raft of the Medusa. Géricault works in rapid, sculptural brushstrokes against a darkened ground, using strong chiaroscuro to model the face with a directness drawn from his admiration of Caravaggio and the Old Masters.

title: The Violinist Niccolò Paganini
description: A pencil portrait of the virtuoso violinist captured during Ingres's years in Rome. The drawing distils Paganini to crisp contour and minimal shading, exemplifying the precise linear style for which Ingres's portrait sketches of musicians and patrons remain prized.
credit: Web Gallery of Art : Image Info about artwork wga QS:P11807,"i/ingres/08ingres"

title: The White Horse
description: The first of Constable's six-foot Stour valley canvases, showing a workhorse ferried across the river on a flat-bottomed barge. Constable elevates everyday East Anglian labor to grand-manner scale, with broken impasto highlights and a damp, atmospheric sky.

title: Castle by the River
description: A fortified castle commands the bend of a broad river under a luminous, theatrical sky. The composition treats architecture scenographically, the way Schinkel did in his stage designs for Berlin, while the contemplative figures and burnished light belong to the wider Romantic landscape current.

title: Cattle Market before a Large City on a Lake
description: Drovers, riders and townspeople negotiate over cattle on a rise overlooking a Bavarian lake, with a distant city silhouetted under bright clear air. Kobell's crisp draftsmanship and high-keyed Alpine light typify his Biedermeier landscapes.

title: Harwich: The Low Lighthouse and Beacon Hill
description: A breezy view of the Essex coast, with the squat Low Lighthouse anchoring a beach where small figures gather under a luminous, cloud-stacked sky. Constable's skies are scientifically observed, drawing on his sustained study of meteorology in oil sketches.
credit: yAGr9d4fNHBv5A at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Portrait of a Kleptomaniac
description: One of Gericault's series of portraits depicting inmates of a Paris asylum, each suffering from a specific monomania. The unflinching, dignified treatment of the sitter and the somber palette mark a pioneering encounter between Romantic painting and clinical psychiatry.
credit: Web Gallery of Art : Image Info about artwork wga QS:P11807,"g/gericaul/1/109geric"

title: Saturn Devouring His Son
dateCreated: between 1820 and 1823 Romanticism (second half of 18 th century –first half of 19 th century )
description: From the Black Paintings Goya applied directly to the walls of his Quinta del Sordo, the titan Cronus tears into one of his children, eyes blazing in a void. The savage handling, sooty palette, and private setting belong to the bleak, late vocabulary now reassembled in the Prado.

title: Susanna and the elders
description: A Neoclassical treatment of the biblical episode in which the chaste Susanna, bathing in her garden, is spied upon by two elders concealed in foliage. The smooth handling of the nude and theatrical contrast of light and shadow reflect the Flemish painter's training in the academic tradition of David.

title: The Spring
description: A standing nude pours water from an urn at her shoulder, an allegory of a freshwater spring. Ingres began the picture in Florence around 1820 and worked at it intermittently for decades; its porcelain-smooth contours epitomise his late Neoclassical ideal of feminine form.
credit: Google Art Project: Home – pic Maximum resolution.

title: The Epsom Derby
description: Four thoroughbreds stretched into the rocking-horse 'flying gallop' pose then conventional in sporting art, set against a low storm-bruised English sky. Géricault painted it in London, where his fascination with horseflesh and equestrian sport reshaped his late Romantic style.
credit: The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 .

title: Moonrise over the Sea
description: Two women and a man on a rocky shore watch sailing ships approach beneath a rising moon, the sky banded violet and gold. Friedrich repeated the moonlit-sea motif throughout his career as a Rückenfigur meditation on faith, return, and the threshold between visible and unseen.

title: Rocky ravine in the Elbe Sandstone Mountains
description: A study of the Neurathen rock formation in Saxon Switzerland, with weathered sandstone pillars rising against a pale sky. Friedrich drew repeatedly from this region near Dresden, translating direct observation into the meditative, vertical compositions characteristic of his mature Romantic landscapes.

title: Solitary Tree
description: A weathered oak rises at the centre of a Bohemian valley at dawn, its dead crown set against luminous distant mountains. Friedrich uses the broken tree as a quiet emblem of endurance, paired with the morning panorama as one half of a meditation on the cycle of day and life.

title: The Kiss
description: An intimate study of two lovers locked in an embrace, modelled with the vigorous, sculptural brushwork that characterises Gericault's late work. The intense physicality and shadowed flesh tones reflect his sustained interest in bodily expression.
credit: Web Gallery of Art : Image Info about artwork wga QS:P11807,"g/gericaul/2/25gerica"

title: First Sketch for ‘The Battle of Trafalgar’
description: A loose oil sketch toward the large Trafalgar canvas commissioned by George IV for St James's Palace. Turner stitched together multiple moments of the engagement onto a single mast-cluttered composition. The freedom of brushwork here, intended only as a working study, anticipates his later, looser exhibited manner.

title: Romeo and Juliet’s last kiss
description: Romeo, foot already on the balcony, embraces Juliet in an exchange of literary tenderness laced with foreboding. Hayez, the leading Italian Romantic painter and a quiet Risorgimento sympathiser, modelled the composition on theatrical tableaux to charge it with patriotic feeling.

title: Salisbury Cathedral from the Bishop's Grounds
description: The cathedral's spire rises through the screen of trees on a property owned by the painter's friend Bishop Fisher, who commissioned the picture. Constable renders the verdant grounds with characteristic flecks of white impasto — his 'snow' that animated foliage and water alike.
credit: The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 .

title: Orphan Girl at the Cemetery
dateCreated: 1824 Romanticism (second half of 18 th century –first half of 19 th century )
description: A young girl kneels beside a grave, her tear-streaked face turned skyward in mute appeal. Painted as a study for the Salon piece The Massacre at Chios, the work shows Delacroix's early Romantic instinct for raw psychological pathos rendered through loose brushwork and warm, earthen tones.

provenance.collection: Department of Paintings of the Louvre

title: Seascape Study with Rain Cloud
description: A small open-air oil sketch of the sea off Brighton, where Constable spent summers for his wife's health. He records a single squall in rapid, loaded brushwork, the sky's grey-violet rain veil treated with the empirical attention to weather he called the chiaroscuro of nature.

title: Self Portrait in a Group of Friend
description: Hayez places himself among the painters Giuseppe Molteni and Giovanni Migliara, the sculptor Pelagio Pelagi, and the writer Tommaso Grossi, an intimate Milanese circle gathered around a table. Now in the Museo Poldi Pezzoli, the conversation piece records the cultural milieu that shaped Lombard Romanticism.
credit: bwFUwn2Et3dOjQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: The Highland Family
credit: This file was donated to Wikimedia Commons as part of a project by the Metropolitan Museum of Art . See the Image and Data Resources Open Access Policy

title: The Massacre at Chios
description: A monumental Romantic response to the Ottoman massacre of Greeks on the island of Chios, exhibited at the 1824 Salon. Delacroix piles broken bodies and dazed survivors across a parched foreground, the distant smoking landscape echoing the philhellenic outrage of the moment.

provenance.collection: Department of Paintings of the Louvre

title: Death on a pale horse
description: An apocalyptic sketch from the Book of Revelation, the spectral rider looming from a vortex of dark pigment. Painted around the time of his wife's death, the canvas pushes Turner's late, near-abstract handling of paint and his fascination with sublime terror.
credit: Google Arts & Culture — VAEyZU0R3M3O5Q Tate images ( http://www.tate-images.com/results.asp?image=N05504&wwwflag=3&imagepos=1 )

title: Rocky Coast
description: An unpeopled stretch of cliff and surf treated as a study in elemental force. The thinly brushed sea and broken light show Turner's late shorthand for shoreline weather.

title: Shipping
description: A loose marine sketch from Turner's mature years, when his oils and watercolours of shipping abandoned topographic detail for weather and mood. Hulls and masts emerge from a smear of sea and sky, the kind of working study that fed his later exhibited storm and harbour pictures.

title: The Cemetery Entrance
description: A wrought-iron gate opens onto a hushed graveyard, its crosses and weathered stones receding into mist. Friedrich treats the cemetery as a threshold between visible world and the beyond, his characteristic Romantic meditation on mortality rendered in muted earth tones and silvery atmosphere.

title: Wounded Brigand
description: A bandit, slumped in a rocky landscape, drinks from a stream as his life ebbs away, the body twisted in a pose that recalls Delacroix's study of Géricault. The picture distils the young Romantic's taste for outsiders, suffering, and Byronic violence into a single charged moment.
credit: Web Gallery of Art : Image Info about artwork wga QS:P11807,"d/delacroi/1/109delac"

title: Cologne
description: Cologne's quayside gilds in evening light as a packet boat draws in beneath the unfinished cathedral. The vast canvas, now in the Frick Collection, pushes Rhineland topography toward Venetian-style golden atmosphere.

title: Port Ruysdael
description: An invented harbour named in homage to the Dutch marine master Jacob van Ruisdael, with squat fishing craft straining against a grey-green swell. Turner adopts the seventeenth-century tonal vocabulary while loosening the brushwork toward atmospheric haze.

title: The Harbor of Dieppe
description: A bustling Norman harbour bathed in unnaturally Italianate light, criticised at the Royal Academy for its sun-drenched palette. The painting forms part of Turner's continental port series following his post-war travels to France.

title: 1 Wild Turkey
description: The opening plate of Birds of America presents a male Meleagris gallopavo striding through cane, painted at Beechwoods Plantation in West Feliciana Parish. The towering double-elephant folio format permitted Audubon to render the bird at life size, with iridescent body plumage built up in layered aquatint.

title: 10 Brown Titlark
description: Plate 10 depicts the Brown Titlark, now known as the American Pipit, a slim ground-dwelling songbird of open country. Havell's aquatint records the streaked breast and slender bill against a simple botanical sprig.

title: 100 Marsh Wren
description: Plate 100 of The Birds of America shows the Marsh Wren clinging among reedy stems of its nesting habitat. Engraved and aquatinted by Robert Havell Jr. from Audubon's watercolor, the life-size composition pairs species portrait with carefully observed wetland setting.

title: 101 Raven
description: Plate 101 presents the Common Raven at full life size, a composition that pushed the limits of the double-elephant folio sheet. Glossy black plumage is rendered through the hand-coloring of Havell's aquatint, the bird shown with branch and prey in characteristic Audubon staging.

title: 102 Blue Jay
description: Plate 102 depicts three Blue Jays raiding eggs from another bird's nest, a tableau emphasizing Audubon's interest in behavior as well as form. Havell's aquatint preserves the iridescent barring of the wings and the dynamic diagonal grouping.

title: 103 Canada Warbler
description: Plate 103 shows the Canada Warbler among the foliage of a flowering plant, the yellow breast and necklace of streaks rendered with diagnostic precision. Audubon's habit of pairing each species with botanically accurate vegetation is on full display.

title: 104 Chipping Sparrow
description: Plate 104 depicts the Chipping Sparrow perched on a flowering branch, its rufous crown and clean breast carefully delineated. The aquatint preserves the soft gradations of the small songbird's plumage achieved in Audubon's original watercolor.

title: 105 Red-breasted Nuthatch
description: Plate 105 shows the Red-breasted Nuthatch in its characteristic head-down posture on a conifer trunk. Havell's aquatint renders the slate-blue back, rust underparts, and bold facial striping at life size.

title: 106 Black Vulture or Carrion Crow
description: Plate 106 presents two Black Vultures at a carcass, a subject that gave Audubon the chance to depict carrion-feeding behavior alongside the species' bare gray head and broad wings. The composition fills the double-elephant sheet with its scavenging pair.

title: 107 Canada Jay
description: Plate 107 depicts the Canada Jay (now Gray Jay) among coniferous branches of the northern forests it inhabits. Soft gray and white plumage is conveyed through subtle aquatint tones and selective hand-coloring.

title: 108 Fox-coloured Sparrow
description: Plate 108 shows the Fox Sparrow, its rich rufous tail and heavily streaked breast set against a sprig of ground vegetation. The aquatint captures the species' stout bunting-like build and warm coloration.

title: 109 Savannah Finch
description: Plate 109 depicts the Savannah Sparrow, the small grassland songbird Audubon named for the Georgia city where he first collected it. The bird is shown perched among slender grass stems appropriate to its open-country habitat.

title: 11 Bird of Washington
description: Audubon's so-called Bird of Washington, depicted as an immense brown eagle on a rocky perch. The species was claimed as a discovery by Audubon but is now generally regarded as a misidentified juvenile bald eagle, and the plate has become a touchstone in debates over his ornithological accuracy.

title: 110 Hooded Warbler
description: Plate 110 shows the Hooded Warbler, the male's black hood framing a vivid yellow face. Audubon places the bird among the foliage of an understory plant, in keeping with the species' preference for shady eastern woodlands.

title: 111 Pileated Woodpecker
description: Plate 111 fills the double-elephant sheet with a pair of Pileated Woodpeckers excavating a tree trunk, their flaming red crests a striking accent against the bark. The composition showcases Audubon's signature pairing of life-size scale with active behavior.

title: 112 Downy Woodpecker
description: Plate 112 depicts the Downy Woodpecker, North America's smallest woodpecker, clinging to a slender branch. Havell's aquatint preserves the crisp black-and-white pattern and the male's small red nape patch.

title: 113 Blue-bird
description: Plate 113 shows the Eastern Bluebird (Sialia sialis), a male, female, and juvenile arranged on a single branch to display the species' sexual and age variation. The vibrant blue back, achieved through hand-coloring over the aquatint, made the plate a perennial favorite.

title: 114 White-crowned Sparrow
description: Plate 114 depicts the White-crowned Sparrow, its boldly striped head crisply rendered above a clean gray breast. The bird is set among slender stems suited to the sparrow's brushy habitat.

title: 115 Wood Pewee
description: Plate 115 shows the Eastern Wood-Pewee perched among the blossoms of swamp azalea (then Azalea viscosa, now Rhododendron viscosum). The pairing of small flycatcher and flowering branch is typical of Audubon's union of ornithology and botanical record.

title: 116 Ferruginous Thrush
description: Plate 116 depicts the Ferruginous Thrush, now known as the Brown Thrasher, its long tail and rich rusty back rendered at life size. Audubon shows the bird in a dynamic pose against vegetation, capturing the secretive thicket-dweller in characteristic alertness.

title: 117 Mississippi Kite
description: An aquatint engraving of the Mississippi Kite, a slate-grey raptor of the southern wetlands. Audubon places the bird in characteristic mid-flight pose, fanned tail and pointed wings rendered with the precise feather work that defined the double-elephant folio.

title: 118 Warbling Flycatcher
description: A study of the small olive-toned songbird known today as the warbling vireo, perched amid foliage. The plate exemplifies Audubon's habit of pairing each species with a botanical setting drawn from direct field observation.

title: 119 Yellow-throated Vireo
description: The yellow-throated vireo is shown among leafy branches, its lemon breast and white spectacles picked out in fine line and watercolour wash. Engraved by Robert Havell Jr. from Audubon's life-size original.

title: 12 Baltimore Oriole
description: Baltimore orioles arrayed on a tulip tree branch, the pendulous woven nest the species is famed for visible among the leaves. The plate combines ornithological and botanical observation in a single, balanced composition.

title: 120 Pewit Flycatcher
description: Two eastern phoebes, here under their older common name, perched at a nest tucked into a rocky crevice. The composition reflects Audubon's interest in nesting behaviour and his early banding experiments with this species.

title: 121 Snowy Owl
description: A pair of snowy owls posed on a wind-swept outcrop, the male and female differentiated by the density of their dark barring. The aquatint exploits velvet blacks and reserved white paper to evoke arctic plumage against a low northern sky.

title: 122 Blue Grosbeak
description: Male and female blue grosbeaks set among grain stalks, the cobalt male contrasted with the warm cinnamon female. The plate shows Audubon's preference for placing songbirds within the agricultural margins where he encountered them.

title: 123 Black & Yellow Warblers
description: A study of magnolia warblers, formerly called black and yellow warblers, arranged on a flowering branch. Audubon's compositional habit of stacking multiple individuals across a single bough allowed him to display plumage variation within a species.

title: 124 Green Black-capt Flycatcher
description: The Wilson's warbler, here under an obsolete name, perched on a slender stem. The black-capped male and plainer female demonstrate the sexual dimorphism Audubon was careful to record.

title: 125 Brown-headed Nuthatch
description: A southern pine specialist shown clinging head-down to bark in the species' typical pose. The plate captures the small nuthatch's sharp bill and stubby tail, characters Audubon emphasised through tight, descriptive line work.

title: 126 White-headed Eagle
description: The bald eagle, here under its older descriptive name, gripping a freshly caught catfish on a riverside log. The plate is among the most dramatic in the folio, asserting the species as an emblem of American wilderness.

title: 127 Rose-breasted Grosbeak
description: Rose-breasted grosbeaks shown amid the foliage of a fruiting branch, the male's crimson bib set against bold black and white plumage. The composition pairs adult and juvenile to record seasonal change.

title: 128 Cat Bird
description: A pair of grey catbirds among bramble, named for their mewing call. Audubon situates the birds within thorny undergrowth typical of their breeding habitat, a recurring strategy for grounding species in ecological context.

title: 129 Great Crested Flycatcher
description: The great crested flycatcher poised on a forked branch, its rufous tail and lemon belly delicately laid in. Audubon notes the species' habit of weaving shed snake skins into its nest, an ethological detail typical of his accompanying writings.

title: 13 Snow Bird
description: The dark-eyed junco, then called the snow bird, depicted in slate and white plumage on a sprig of greenery. Audubon's terse staging suits the species' winter ubiquity at northern doorsteps.

title: 130 Yellow-winged Sparrow
description: Grasshopper sparrows, under an older name, posed low among grass stems. Audubon's spare composition reflects the cryptic, ground-dwelling nature of the bird, whose flat-headed silhouette he renders with characteristic precision.

title: 131 American Robin
description: American robins shown on a fruiting branch, an adult feeding alongside speckled juveniles. The plate documents the species across age classes, a comparative approach Audubon employed for common North American thrushes.

title: 132 Three-toed Woodpecker
description: Black-backed and American three-toed woodpeckers gathered on a beetle-bored conifer trunk, their yellow caps clearly distinguished. The plate combines several northern boreal species into one ecological tableau.

title: 133 Black-poll Warbler
description: Blackpoll warblers perched on a flowering branch, the male's black cap and white cheek set against streaked flanks. Audubon's tight stippling captures the fine plumage detail of this long-distance migrant.

title: 134 Hemlock Warbler
description: Now considered a plumage variant of the yellow-rumped or Townsend's warbler, the hemlock warbler appears here on a coniferous spray. The plate reflects the unsettled state of warbler taxonomy in the early nineteenth century.

title: 135 Blackburnian Warbler
description: Blackburnian warblers, the male's flame-orange throat ablaze against black and white, set within hemlock foliage. Audubon's controlled palette concentrates colour at the throat to mirror the bird's own visual emphasis.

title: 136 Meadow Lark
description: Eastern meadowlarks posed in tall prairie grass, the yellow breast and black V boldly displayed. The composition links the species firmly to the open agricultural country in which Audubon most often observed it.

title: 137 Yellow-breasted Chat
description: Yellow-breasted chats among tangled vines and blooming trumpet flowers. Audubon plays the chat's lemon breast against the vivid orange-red of the blossoms, integrating bird and habitat in a single decorative scheme.

title: 138 Connecticut Warbler
description: A pair of Connecticut warblers posed low among foliage, the male's grey hood and bold eye-ring carefully delineated. The species was first described by Alexander Wilson in Connecticut, hence the name retained by Audubon.

title: 139 Field Sparrow
description: Field sparrows on a low briar, the pink bill and rusty crown rendered against a soft palette. The plate's modest scale and quiet pose suit the unassuming character of this old-field songbird.

title: 14 Prairie Warbler
description: Two prairie warblers perch among flowering branches, their yellow underparts and side streaking carefully delineated. The plate is typical of the early Havell numbers, in which Audubon paired songbirds with the specific vegetation in which he had observed them feeding.

title: 140 Pine Creeping Warbler
description: Pine warblers, under an older common name, posed on a pine bough heavy with cones. Audubon ties the species inseparably to its host tree, reflecting the strict habitat fidelity that gives the warbler its name.

title: 141 I. Goshawk - 2. Stanley Hawk
description: A composite plate showing the northern goshawk together with the Stanley hawk, now identified as the Cooper's hawk. The grouping allowed Audubon to compare two large accipiters of similar markings within a single sheet.

title: 142 American Sparrow Hawk
description: American kestrels, formerly called sparrow hawks, shown with prey at a rocky perch. The plate captures the falcon's distinctive facial pattern and slate-blue wings of the male, the rufous back of the female.

title: 143 Golden-crowned Thrush
description: The ovenbird, under its older name, walking the forest floor among fallen leaves. Audubon registers the bird's olive back and bold black-bordered crown stripe with the close observation typical of ground-dwelling warblers in the folio.

title: 144 Small Green Crested Flycatcher
description: A small olive-toned flycatcher poses among slender branches in this hand-coloured aquatint from the double-elephant folio Birds of America. Audubon presents the bird life-size and in characteristic alert posture, his foliage rendered with the botanical precision that distinguished the project from earlier ornithological prints.

title: 145 Yellow Red-poll Warbler
description: A pair of warblers, their crowns flushed with rufous, perch among delicate leafing twigs. The plate exemplifies Audubon's working method of arranging freshly collected specimens on a wire grid to capture lifelike attitudes for the engraver.

title: 146 Fish Crow
description: The Fish Crow is shown perched among coastal vegetation, its glossy black plumage rendered through layered aquatint tones. Audubon distinguished this species from the larger American Crow chiefly by voice and habitat, observations recorded alongside the plate in his Ornithological Biography.

title: 147 Night Hawk
description: Nighthawks wheel and rest in this composition, their long pointed wings and cryptic mottled plumage shown in multiple attitudes. Audubon characteristically combined several individuals on a single sheet to display sex differences and flight posture in one image.

title: 148 Pine Swamp Warbler
description: A small warbler clings to a budding branch in this hand-coloured aquatint engraving. The plate forms part of the Havell edition of Birds of America, etched and aquatinted in London by Robert Havell Jr. after Audubon's watercolour studies.

title: 149 Sharp-tailed Finch
description: A sharp-tailed sparrow is shown among marsh grasses, its streaked breast and ochre face pattern emphasised against muted reeds. Audubon associated the species with Atlantic salt-meadows, a habitat he sketched on field expeditions along the eastern seaboard.

title: 15 Blue Yellow-backed Warbler
description: The diminutive Northern Parula is shown working through lichen and blossom, its blue-grey back and yellow throat picked out in delicate washes. Audubon's botanical setting reflects the bird's preference for Spanish-moss-laden southern woodlands.

title: 150 Red-eyed Vireo
description: A red-eyed vireo is shown gleaning among broad green leaves, the diagnostic eye-stripe and pale ruby iris picked out by the colourist. Audubon depicted the bird in its preferred deciduous canopy, where its persistent song was familiar throughout eastern American summers.

title: 151 Turkey Buzzard
description: The Turkey Vulture stands with wings half-spread, its bare red head and ragged plumage rendered in close anatomical detail. Audubon's notes on this species fuelled his celebrated dispute with Charles Waterton over whether vultures locate carrion by sight or smell.

title: 152 White-breasted Black-capped Nuthatch
description: Nuthatches descend a tree trunk head-first in their characteristic posture, the bark texture engraved with fine aquatint grain. The composition records the bird's habitual foraging behaviour rather than presenting it as a static museum specimen.

title: 153 Yellow-crown Warbler
description: Small warblers with bright yellow crown-patches are arranged on a flowering branch, the colourist's washes giving warmth to the breast streaking. Audubon often grouped several individuals to economise on plates while showing plumage variation.

title: 154 Tennessee Warbler
description: A Tennessee Warbler perches among slender twigs, its olive-and-grey palette modelled by selectively applied aquatint. The species was named by Alexander Wilson from a migrant collected on the Cumberland River, and Audubon retained the regional epithet.

title: 155 Black-throated Blue Warbler
description: Male and female Black-throated Blue Warblers appear together, the marked sexual dimorphism between slate-blue male and olive female deliberately contrasted on a single branch. The pairing reflects Audubon's pedagogical impulse to depict both sexes for naturalists' reference.

title: 156 American Crow
description: An American Crow grasps an ear of maize, the glossy black plumage built from layered aquatint and selective burnishing. The image, like much of Audubon's corvid imagery, registers the crow's reputation as an agricultural pest while affording it a dignified portrait.

title: 157 Rusty Grakle
description: Rusty Blackbirds are shown in autumn plumage edged with cinnamon, the colourist working warm washes over the matte black underdrawing. Audubon observed the species in flooded woodland on its migration south through the Mississippi valley.

title: 158 American Swift
description: Chimney Swifts are caught in rapid flight, their stiff bowed wings and short tails sketched in several positions. The plate departs from Audubon's usual perched compositions, attempting to render an aerial bird that almost never alights in plain view.

title: 159 Cardinal Grosbeak
description: A pair of Northern Cardinals are arranged on a fruiting branch, the male's vermilion plumage carried by a saturated red wash applied over the engraved ground. The plate's vivid colouring relies on the brilliant cochineal- and madder-based reds available to the Havell workshop.

title: 16 Great-footed Hawk
description: A Peregrine Falcon stands over a duck prey-item, blood-flecked feathers strewn around its talons. The plate, here under Audubon's preferred name of Great-footed Hawk, is among the most dramatic predator images in Birds of America.

title: 160 Black-capped Titmouse
description: A Black-capped Chickadee hangs from a slender twig, its bold cap and bib reduced to crisp shapes against the understated background. Audubon often illustrated tits and chickadees in acrobatic feeding postures observed at close range.

title: 161 Brasilian Caracara Eagle
description: The Crested Caracara is shown standing over carrion, its bare facial skin and barred breast carefully delineated. Audubon encountered the species in Florida and Texas, expanding the geographic scope of Birds of America to the subtropical edge of the continent.

title: 162 Zenaida Dove
description: A pair of Zenaida Doves perch on a fruiting branch, their warm cinnamon plumage and iridescent neck patches finely modelled. Audubon studied this West Indian species on his 1832 expedition to the Florida Keys, the principal source for several plates in this section.

title: 163 Palm Warbler
description: Palm Warblers are placed among low foliage with their characteristic tail-pumping posture suggested by the bird's downward-curved attitude. The plate reflects Audubon's habit of incorporating diagnostic behaviour into otherwise still compositions.

title: 164 Tawny Thrush
description: A Veery is shown among understorey foliage, the warm cinnamon back and faintly spotted breast realised through restrained washes. Audubon followed nineteenth-century usage in calling this woodland thrush the Tawny Thrush.

title: 165 Bachmans Finch
description: Bachman's Sparrow is shown among grass stems in a sparse composition, the streaked back and plain breast typical of the genus. The species honours Audubon's friend and collaborator John Bachman, with whom he later co-authored the Viviparous Quadrupeds of North America.

title: 166 Rough-legged Falcon
description: The Rough-legged Hawk is depicted in dark and light morphs, the feathered tarsi that give the species its name carefully engraved. Audubon's plate documents the broad plumage variation within a single Arctic-breeding raptor.

title: 167 Key-west Dove
description: The Key West Quail-Dove is shown amid tropical foliage, its iridescent nape and rufous flanks rendered in unusually saturated hues. Audubon collected the species on the Florida Keys, where it has since become a vanishingly rare visitor.

title: 168 Fork-tailed Flycatcher
description: A Fork-tailed Flycatcher is shown with its dramatically elongated outer tail-feathers fanned across the page, the design exploiting the double-elephant folio's exceptional sheet size. Audubon included this South American vagrant on the strength of stray records reaching the eastern United States.

title: 169 Mangrove Cuckoo
description: The Mangrove Cuckoo perches in dense coastal foliage, its buff underparts and black ear-patch picked out against tropical leaves. Audubon recorded the species during his Florida Keys expedition, where he distinguished it from the more familiar Yellow-billed Cuckoo.

title: 17 Carolina Turtle Dove
description: Carolina (mourning) doves grouped on a flowering branch, their pinkish-buff plumage set against delicate blossoms. The composition uses overlapping figures to create the multi-bird tableaux Audubon favoured for sociable species.

title: 170 Gray Tyrant
description: A Gray Kingbird perches alertly on a slender stem, its pale grey upperparts and stout black bill rendered with restrained tonal washes. Audubon associated the species with the Florida coast, where it occupies the same exposed perches as its more northerly relative the Eastern Kingbird.

title: 171 Barn Owl
description: A pair of barn owls perched in a darkened nocturnal scene, one grasping a captured ground squirrel in its talons. Audubon's hand-coloured aquatint emphasises the species' heart-shaped facial disc and softly mottled plumage, rendered with the dramatic lighting typical of his Havell-engraved double elephant folio.

title: 172 Blue-headed Pigeon
description: Plate from Birds of America showing the blue-headed quail-dove, a ground-feeding species native to Cuba. The bird is set against tropical foliage, its iridescent crown and white throat-stripe rendered through layered aquatint and watercolour finishing.

title: 173 Barn Swallow
description: Barn swallows shown in flight and at rest, their forked tails and rust-throated faces sharply observed. The composition typifies Audubon's preference for dynamic poses over the stiff specimen-style of earlier ornithological illustration.

title: 174 Olive sided Flycatcher
description: An olive-sided flycatcher perched on a slender branch, captured mid-watch in the alert posture characteristic of aerial insectivores. The plate combines etching, aquatint and hand colouring produced for the double elephant folio.

title: 175 Nuttall's lesser-marsh Wren
description: Marsh wrens perched among reedy stalks, named here for the naturalist Thomas Nuttall. The plate places the small passerines within a habitat vignette, a hallmark of Audubon's life-size approach to ornithological portraiture.

title: 176 Spotted Grous
description: A spotted (or Canada) grouse rendered in profile with closely observed plumage barring and crimson eye comb. The aquatint engraving reflects Audubon's collaboration with Robert Havell Jr., whose London workshop translated the watercolours into print.

title: 177 White-crowned Pigeon
description: White-crowned pigeons depicted among glossy fruiting branches of a tropical tree, evoking the species' Caribbean and Floridian range. The white cap and slate-grey body are set against rich foliage typical of Audubon's habitat backgrounds.

title: 178 Orange-crowned Warbler
description: An orange-crowned warbler poised on a flowering twig, its olive plumage and faint head-stripe carefully delineated. The plate is engraved, etched and aquatinted, with watercolour applied by hand in Havell's London studio.

title: 179 Wood Wren
description: A wood wren among woodland branches, rendered at life-size as required by Audubon's double elephant folio format. The aquatint preserves the soft barring of the plumage and the alert tail-cocked posture typical of the family.

title: 18 Bewick's Wren
description: A pair of Bewick's wrens with cocked tails and finely barred plumage, named for the English wood engraver Thomas Bewick. The plate emphasises their habit of foraging through dense thickets, suggested here by an entwined branch.

title: 180 Pine Finch
description: Pine finches (now pine siskins) perched among conifer foliage, with streaked plumage and yellow wing-flashes precisely registered. The plate exemplifies Audubon's dual interest in scientific accuracy and decorative botanical setting.

title: 181 Goldon Eagle
description: A golden eagle in full predatory descent, gripping a white hare against a mountainous backdrop. One of Audubon's most theatrical compositions, the plate showcases the raptor's broad wingspan and golden nape feathers.

title: 182 Ground Dove
description: A pair of common ground-doves on a flowering branch, the species' diminutive scale and scaled breast accurately rendered. The plate balances ornithological detail with Audubon's characteristic floral staging.

title: 183 Goldon-crested-Wren
description: Golden-crested wrens (kinglets) shown among coniferous sprigs, their flame-coloured crown stripes carefully highlighted. The miniature scale of the species allowed Audubon to compose multiple birds within a single intimate vignette.

title: 184 Mangrove Humming Bird
description: A mangrove hummingbird hovering at tropical blossoms, its iridescence captured through the layered aquatint process. The plate reflects the broader nineteenth-century fascination with the dazzling plumage of New World hummingbirds.

title: 185 Bachman's Warbler
description: Bachman's warbler depicted on a flowering branch, named after Audubon's friend and collaborator the Rev. John Bachman. Once a southern wetland species, the bird is now feared extinct, lending the plate added documentary weight.

title: 186 Pinnated Grous
description: A male pinnated grouse (greater prairie chicken) in courtship display, neck-sacs inflated and pinnae raised. The plate documents a once-abundant prairie species whose lekking behaviour Audubon observed firsthand.

title: 187 Boat-tailed Grackle
description: Boat-tailed grackles displayed in pairs, the long keel-shaped tail of the male contrasted with the smaller browner female. The composition captures the species' coastal marsh habitat through suggestive vegetation.

title: 188 Tree Sparrow
description: American tree sparrows arrayed on a slender bare branch, their rufous caps and distinctive breast-spot precisely drawn. The simplicity of the staging foregrounds the careful study of plumage that underpins the entire Birds of America project.

title: 189 Snow Bunting
description: Snow buntings in winter plumage, rendered against a sparse Arctic-suggestive ground. The black-and-white males and warmer-toned females are positioned to record both seasonal phases of this circumpolar species.

title: 19 Louisiana Water Thrush
description: Parkesia motacilla shown probing along a streamside branch, rendered with the characteristic streaked underparts and bold supercilium. Engraved by Robert Havell Jr. from Audubon's double-elephant folio watercolour, with delicate aquatint tonal washes evoking damp woodland habitat.

title: 190 Yellow bellied Woodpecker
description: Yellow-bellied sapsuckers shown clinging to a tree trunk, their characteristic horizontal sap-well rows implied beneath them. The plate combines etching and aquatint to capture the strongly patterned head and red crown.

title: 191 Willow Grous or Large Ptarmigan
description: Willow grouse, or willow ptarmigan, depicted in transitional plumage between summer rufous and winter white. The image documents the species' Arctic and sub-Arctic adaptation through carefully observed feathered legs and feet.

title: 192 Great American Shrike or Butcher Bird
description: A northern shrike perched with prey impaled on a thorn, illustrating the larder behaviour that earned the species its common name. The plate combines naturalistic observation with the narrative drama Audubon favoured.

title: 193 Lincoln Finch
description: Lincoln's sparrow depicted on a flowering branch, named for Audubon's young Labrador-expedition companion Thomas Lincoln. Subtly streaked plumage and a buff malar stripe distinguish the species from related sparrows.

title: 194 Canadian Titmouse
description: Boreal chickadees, then known as Canadian titmice, perched among coniferous twigs. The plate records a northern forest species whose brown cap distinguishes it from its more familiar black-capped relative.

title: 195 Ruby crowned Wren
description: Ruby-crowned kinglets shown among slender sprigs, the male displaying the scarlet crown-patch usually concealed beneath olive feathers. The miniature scale required especially fine aquatint work in the Havell engraving.

title: 196 Labrador Falcon
description: A Labrador (gyr) falcon perched in profile, the species' powerful build and barred plumage rendered with raptorial gravitas. The plate reflects Audubon's 1833 Labrador expedition and its Arctic ornithological discoveries.

title: 197 American Crossbill
description: Red crossbills feeding among conifer cones, the unique mandible crossing essential to their seed-extraction technique clearly visible. The composition unites diet, habitat and anatomy in a single life-size study.

title: 198 Brown headed Worm eating Warbler
description: A worm-eating warbler perched on slender vegetation, the bold dark head-stripes that identify the species precisely registered. The plate shows the typical aquatint-and-watercolour layering of the Havell-engraved double elephant folio.

title: 199 Little Owl
description: A small Athene noctua perched in alert profile, rendered in hand-coloured aquatint after Audubon's life-size watercolour. Sharp etched feather striations across the crown and mantle convey the species' compact, ground-hunting build, while the dark eye-mask anchors the composition.

title: 2 Yellow-billed Cuckoo
description: A slender, long-tailed cuckoo with a curved yellow bill, perched among the foliage of a flowering pawpaw. Audubon's botanical accuracy and elongated S-curve composition became signatures of the early Birds of America plates engraved by Robert Havell Jr.

title: 20 Blue-winged Yellow Warbler
description: Vermivora cyanoptera arranged on a leafy branch to display the lemon underparts and slate wing edging. The pose reflects Audubon's practice of wiring fresh specimens into life-like attitudes for direct watercolour study.

title: 200 Shore Lark
description: Eremophila alpestris depicted on tundra-like ground, the small black facial pattern and yellow throat carefully etched. The plate captures the species' open-country posture, with sparse plant detail emphasising the bird's preference for bare, gravelly terrain.

title: 201 Canada Goose
description: A pair of Branta canadensis arranged across the sheet to fit the folio dimensions, exhibiting Audubon's habit of bending necks and limbs to maintain life-size proportions. Aquatint grain models the soft grey body plumage against the crisply etched black neck and white chinstrap.

title: 202 Red-Throated Diver
description: Gavia stellata in breeding plumage, the rust-red throat patch contrasting with finely engraved silver-grey neck stripes. The low waterline composition and distant horizon situate the diver in its northern coastal habitat.

title: 203 Fresh Water Marsh Hen
description: King rail, Rallus elegans, drawn skulking among reeds with neck extended in characteristic cautious gait. Fine etched lines build the barred flank pattern, while aquatint shading establishes the dim, vegetated marsh interior.

title: 204 Salt Water Marsh Hen
description: Rallus crepitans set within tidal cordgrass, the long bill and laterally compressed body conveying its furtive habits in salt marshes. Subtle tonal aquatint distinguishes the muted ochre and grey plumage suited to coastal camouflage.

title: 205 Virginia Rail
description: Rallus limicola depicted in a low, alert stance with the long reddish bill prominent. The plate's restrained palette and tightly etched feather barring reflect Audubon's effort to render small marsh species with the same dignity afforded to larger birds.

title: 206 Summer or Wood Duck
dateCreated: 1827-1838 (publication; from a painting made in Louisiana in 1821)
description: Aix sponsa shown in arboreal habit suggested by overhanging foliage, the drake's iridescent crest and bold facial striping rendered in saturated hand-applied watercolour over aquatint. A favoured Audubon subject for its colour and behaviour.

title: 207 Booby Gannet
description: Sula leucogaster perched in profile, the wedge-shaped bill and dark mantle contrasted against pale underparts. The composition reflects observations from Audubon's Florida and Gulf coast voyages aboard the Marion in the 1830s.

title: 208 Esquimaux Curlew
description: Numenius borealis shown probing tundra ground, the slender decurved bill carefully traced. The species was already in steep decline during Audubon's lifetime and is now likely extinct, lending the plate documentary value.

title: 209 Wilson's Plover
description: Charadrius wilsonia rendered on a beach margin, the heavy black bill and single breast band etched with precision. The sparse coastal setting and shell fragments locate the bird in its preferred Atlantic shoreline habitat.

title: 21 Mocking Bird
description: Mimus polyglottos engaged with a coiled rattlesnake amid yellow jessamine vines, one of Audubon's most narrative compositions. The dramatic encounter drew scientific criticism over snake-climbing accuracy but established the plate's enduring fame.

title: 210 Least Bittern
description: Ixobrychus exilis shown in classic vertical reed-grasping pose, body elongated and bill pointed skyward in the species' camouflage stance. Etched striations on the breast and crown convey the bird's cryptic plumage.

title: 211 Great blue Heron
description: Ardea herodias rendered at full life size by bending the long neck and legs to fit the double-elephant folio sheet. Aquatint and watercolour build the slate-grey body and shaggy plumes, with marshland horizon barely indicated.

title: 212 Common Gull
description: Larus canus depicted in flight or standing profile, the soft grey mantle and yellow bill described in muted aquatint washes. A relatively restrained composition typical of plates devoted to widespread coastal species.

title: 213 Puffin
description: Atlantic puffin, Fratercula arctica, shown with breeding-season bill ornament and orange feet. The plate likely draws on observations from Audubon's 1833 expedition to Labrador, where he studied seabird colonies first-hand.

title: 214 Razor Bill
description: Alca torda set on a rocky ledge, the deep laterally flattened bill with its white vertical stripe carefully etched. Aquatint tonality conveys the velvety black mantle against the cliff-side seabird habitat.

title: 215 Hyperborean phalarope
description: Red-necked phalarope, Phalaropus lobatus, shown swimming with the species' lobed feet and slender needle bill. The compositional emphasis on aquatic habit reflects the bird's distinctive pelagic life cycle.

title: 216 Wood Ibiss
description: Wood stork, Mycteria americana, given full folio scale with bare scaly head and heavy decurved bill. The towering pose and southern wetland setting reflect Audubon's Florida fieldwork in the 1830s.

title: 217 Louisiana Heron
description: Tricolored heron, Egretta tricolor, captured in a sinuous wading pose with white belly stripe set against slate body. The dynamic curve of the neck typifies Audubon's preference for active, narrative postures over static specimen views.

title: 218 Foolish Guillemot
description: Common murre, Uria aalge, shown in profile with the pointed bill and dark upperparts of breeding plumage. The plate reflects observations from Audubon's Labrador voyage among dense seabird cliffs.

title: 219 Black Guillemot
description: Cepphus grylle in breeding plumage, the sooty body relieved by a striking white wing patch and red feet rendered in saturated watercolour. The cliff-perched composition evokes North Atlantic colony habitat.

title: 22 Purple Martin
description: Several purple martins gather around a hollow gourd nest, the glossy blue-black males and duller females shown in the cooperative groupings typical of the colony-nesting swallow. The plate documents an early form of artificial nest box used by southern planters, embedding ethnography within ornithological observation.

title: 220 Piping Plover
description: Charadrius melodus on pale sand, the bird's sandy-buff back and partial neck band described with minimal etched line. The sparse beach setting reflects the species' preference for open Atlantic shoreline.

title: 221 Mallard Duck
description: Anas platyrhynchos drake and hen arranged within marsh vegetation, the male's bottle-green head built up in iridescent layered washes. A characteristic Audubon pairing of sexes within a single life-size plate.

title: 222 White Ibis
description: Eudocimus albus shown probing with the long red decurved bill, white plumage punctuated by black wingtips. The composition reflects Audubon's southern Florida expeditions among coastal mangrove and estuarine habitats.

title: 223 Pied oyster-catcher
description: American oystercatcher, Haematopus palliatus, on a shell-strewn shore with the long red bill specialised for prying bivalves. Bold tonal contrast between black hood and white belly suits the bird's striking field appearance.

title: 224 Kittiwake Gull
description: Rissa tridactyla rendered with the species' clean grey mantle and black wingtips, perched on rocks suggestive of ocean cliffs. The plate's restrained palette emphasises the kittiwake's pelagic, colony-nesting life.

title: 225 Kildeer Plover
description: A pair of killdeer arranged in alert postures along a stretch of bare sand, their double black breast bands rendered through Havell's hand-coloured aquatint. The plate showcases the species' upright stance and the distinctive russet rump that flashes in flight, presented life-size on double elephant folio paper.

title: 226 Hooping Crane
description: A whooping crane bows its long neck to seize a young alligator from a marshy Louisiana shoreline, with additional hatchlings scattering through the grass. Composed in stages between 1821 and 1822, the plate combines on-the-spot field study with a later background, and depicts the bird at full life-size by twisting its neck downward.

title: 227 Pin tailed Duck
description: A drake northern pintail and his mate are paired against a watery margin, the male's chocolate head and slender tail-spike contrasted with the hen's softer scalloping. The aquatint records the species' elegant body line and the fine vermiculation across the flanks that Audubon studied closely from skinned specimens.

title: 228 American Green winged Teal
description: Drake and hen green-winged teal cluster on a marshy bank, the male's chestnut head crossed by an iridescent green eye-mask and the speculum picked out in metallic green ink. Aquatint and stipple together register the dense barring across the flanks of North America's smallest dabbling duck.

title: 229 Scaup Duck
description: A drake and hen lesser scaup float on rippled water, the male's blue-grey bill and finely vermiculated back set against the female's brown plumage and white facial patch. The plate compresses two views of the species into a single composition, a device Audubon often used to convey sexual dimorphism.

title: 23 Yellow-breasted Warbler
description: A small yellow-breasted warbler perches among slim foliage, its citron underparts and olive back rendered with fine stipple. The plate reflects Audubon's effort to document the still-unsettled nomenclature of North American wood-warblers, several of which were known by names that have since been revised.

title: 230 Ruddy Plover
description: A trio of sanderlings, here called Ruddy Plover, are shown along a shoreline in transitional plumage, their reddish summer wash giving way to the pale winter dress. The aquatint captures the species' restless run-and-stop foraging at the surf line through tightly composed silhouettes.

title: 231 Long-billed Curlew
description: A long-billed curlew stands above a panoramic view of Charleston harbour, its enormous decurved bill nearly touching the cinnamon underwing. Audubon's choice of city skyline as backdrop is unusual for the series, situating the shorebird within a specific Atlantic seaport context.

title: 232 Hooded Merganser
description: A drake hooded merganser raises his fan-shaped white crest while the hen swims behind, her shaggier rust-coloured tuft trailing in the water. The aquatint emphasises the saw-edged bill of this fish-eating diving duck and the bold pied pattern of the breeding male.

title: 233 Sora or Rail
description: A sora picks its way through a tangle of marsh stems, the short yellow bill and barred flanks rendered against the vertical reeds. Audubon used the dense vegetation both as habitat documentation and as a compositional device to suggest the secretive bird's preference for dense cover.

title: 234 Tufted Duck
description: A tufted duck paddles on open water, the drake's drooping black crest and golden eye contrasted with the brown hen alongside. The plate is among the rarer Eurasian vagrants in the series, included after Audubon obtained specimens from the European edge of the species' range.

title: 235 Sooty Tern
description: A sooty tern banks above a tropical sea, its dark upperparts and white forehead resolved against pale sky. Audubon studied the species during his 1832 voyage to the Florida Keys, where vast breeding colonies on the Tortugas supplied much of his field data.

title: 236 Night Heron or Qua bird
description: Adult and immature black-crowned night herons share a single branch, the sleek slate-and-white adult above and the streaky brown juvenile below. The plate compresses the species' dramatic age-related plumage change into one frame, a strategy Havell's aquatint handled through tonal gradation.

title: 237 Great Esquimaux Curlew
