description: An Eskimo curlew stands amid coastal vegetation, its slender decurved bill and cinnamon underwing visible at full life-size. The plate has acquired posthumous weight as a record of a species that was abundant in Audubon's day and is now feared extinct following relentless market hunting.

title: 238 Great Marbled Godwit
description: A marbled godwit probes a tidal flat with its long, slightly upturned, bicoloured bill. The aquatint emphasises the cinnamon wash across the underparts and the heavy barring on the back that gives the species its name, presented against a pared-back shoreline.

title: 239 American Coot
description: An American coot swims among aquatic vegetation, the white frontal shield and chalky bill standing out against slate-black plumage. Audubon shows the lobed toes splayed beneath the water, a structural detail that distinguishes coots from the superficially similar ducks.

title: 24 Roscoe's Yellow-throat
description: A small yellow-throated warbler perches on a slender twig, the throat and breast a saturated lemon yellow against an olive back. The name Roscoe's Yellow-throat reflects nineteenth-century nomenclatural confusion that ornithology later resolved by folding the bird into established species.

title: 240 Roseate Tern
description: A roseate tern hovers with deeply forked tail streaming behind, its pale grey mantle and faintly pink-flushed breast captured through Havell's careful stipple. The plate distinguishes this slender species from the commoner common and Arctic terns by the long, almost wholly black bill.

title: 241 Black Backed Gull
description: A great black-backed gull stands at full life-size on a rocky shore, the slate-black mantle contrasted with a bright pink leg and a heavy yellow bill marked by a red gonys spot. The plate registers the formidable scale of the world's largest gull, a predator of seabird colonies along the North Atlantic.

title: 243 American Snipe
description: A Wilson's snipe crouches in marsh tussocks, its cryptic striped head pattern and long flexible bill visible above the grasses. Audubon shows the bird in the freezing posture used to evade detection, illustrating the camouflage that makes the species so difficult to flush.

title: 244 Common Gallinule
description: A common gallinule walks across floating vegetation, the bright red frontal shield and yellow-tipped bill catching the eye against slate plumage. The aquatint records the long, splayed toes adapted for distributing the bird's weight across lily pads.

title: 245 Thick-billed Murre
description: A thick-billed murre rides choppy water in upright posture, the heavy black bill with its pale tomial line set against a glossy black hood. Audubon studied the species during his 1833 expedition to Labrador, where breeding colonies on the cliffs supplied specimens for the plate.

title: 246 Eider Duck
description: A drake common eider swims with the brown hen behind, his bold black-and-white plumage and pastel green nape divided sharply along the flank. The plate documents the marine duck whose down has been harvested for centuries from nests in northern colonies.

title: 247 Velvet Duck
description: A white-winged scoter, here called Velvet Duck, floats on coastal water, the drake's velvety black plumage broken by a white wing patch and a knobbed orange bill. Aquatint reproduces the matte quality of the breeding plumage that gave the species its old name.

title: 248 American Pied-bill Dobchick
description: A pied-billed grebe sits on calm water, the short dark-banded ivory bill and stub-tailed silhouette giving the species its archaic name. The plate emphasises the lobed feet barely visible below the surface, the same structural feature shared with coots and other grebes.

title: 249 Tufted Auk
description: A crested auklet rests on a rocky ledge with its forward-curving black crest and bright orange bill plate prominent. Audubon worked from specimens collected in the Bering Sea region, an area he never visited, making this plate one of his more reconstructive compositions.

title: 25 Song Sparrow
description: Song sparrows perched on a flowering branch, their streaked breasts and warm brown backs picked out in fine engraved line. Audubon places the birds in active poses that suggest the territorial singing for which the species is named across North America.

title: 250 Arctic Tern
description: An Arctic tern hovers with deeply forked tail and short red legs trailing, the blood-red bill held downward as it scans for fish. The plate captures the proportions of the famously long-distance migrant, whose annual journey between polar oceans Audubon could only have suspected.

title: 251 Brown Pelican
description: A brown pelican perches on a mangrove branch, its huge gular pouch slack and the chestnut-and-cream nape of breeding plumage clearly registered. Audubon studied the species at close range during his 1832 Florida voyage, allowing the life-size composition to bend the bird into a natural resting posture.

title: 252 Florida Cormorant
description: A Florida cormorant rendered in hand-coloured aquatint, its glossy black plumage and hooked bill set against a coastal backdrop. The plate exemplifies Audubon's life-size convention, with the bird's posture suggesting a moment of rest between dives along the southern shore.

title: 253 Jager
description: A jaeger captured mid-flight, the slender wings and elongated central tail feathers stretched across the double-elephant sheet. Audubon pairs anatomical precision with a windswept marine setting, evoking the bird's piratical habit of harassing terns and gulls for food.

title: 254 Wilson's Phalarope
description: Wilson's phalarope shown in breeding plumage, with the chestnut neck stripe and needle-fine bill carefully detailed. The composition includes a wading pose typical of the species, which spins on shallow water to stir up invertebrates from the muddy bottom.

title: 255 Red Phalarope
description: Red phalaropes depicted in their rust-coloured summer dress, slender bills probing at the water's edge. Hand-coloured aquatint by Robert Havell Jr. brings out the contrast between the rich plumage and the muted tones of the Arctic-tinged habitat.

title: 256 Purple Heron
description: A long-necked heron poised among reed-fringed waters, its slate and chestnut plumage rendered through layered aquatint washes. The bird is shown in the patient, statuesque stance characteristic of ambush-feeding herons in marshland environments.

title: 257 Double-crested Cormorant
description: A double-crested cormorant displaying the wispy nuptial tufts that give the species its name, perched with throat sac visible. The aquatint plate emphasises the iridescent black plumage and the sharp, hooked bill used to seize fish underwater.

title: 258 Hudsonian Godwit
description: A Hudsonian godwit shown with its long, slightly upturned bill and cinnamon underparts of the breeding season. The shorebird is set against a sparse shoreline, a nod to its tundra-to-pampas migration that takes it across the entire length of the Americas.

title: 259 Horned Grebe
description: A horned grebe in breeding plumage, the namesake golden ear tufts flared against a chestnut neck. The composition conveys the bird's compact, low-floating posture, supported by lobed feet that propel it on diving forays for small fish.

title: 26 Carolina Parrot
description: Carolina parakeets clustered on a cocklebur branch, their green bodies and yellow-and-orange heads brilliantly hand-coloured. Audubon's plate is now bittersweet documentation of the only parrot native to the eastern United States, driven to extinction by the early twentieth century.

title: 260 Fork-tail Petrel
description: A fork-tailed petrel skimming low over the open ocean, the deeply notched tail and dark plumage rendered in tonal aquatint. The plate captures the seabird's pelagic life, only briefly touching down on water to pick at planktonic prey.

title: 261 Hooping Crane
description: A whooping crane bent into the folio's outsized format, its tall white frame and crimson crown filling the page. Audubon shows the bird in a feeding pose along a marsh edge, a record of an animal already growing scarce in his lifetime.

title: 262 Tropic Bird
description: A tropicbird rendered with elongated tail streamers trailing behind a stout white body. The plate evokes the species' offshore wanderings across warm seas, where it plunge-dives for squid and flying fish far from any coast.

title: 263 Pigmy Curlew
description: A pygmy curlew, today known as the curlew sandpiper, depicted with its decurved bill probing at the tide line. Audubon's aquatint records a Eurasian visitor to North American shores, in transitional plumage between the rufous breeding dress and pale winter coat.

title: 264 Fulmar Petrel
description: A fulmar shown in stiff-winged glide, the tube-nosed bill and pale grey plumage finely etched. The composition reflects this seabird's life on Atlantic swells, where it feeds on fish offal and was long associated with whaling fleets.

title: 265 Buff breasted Sandpiper
description: A buff-breasted sandpiper rendered in soft ochre tones, the upright stance typical of this dry-grassland shorebird. The plate emphasises the plain, dove-like face and yellow legs that distinguish it from the wetter-loving members of its family.

title: 266 Common Cormorant
description: A great cormorant standing with wings half-spread to dry, its glossy black plumage offset by a yellow gular patch. Audubon's hand-coloured aquatint captures the heavy, prehistoric silhouette familiar from rocky coasts on both sides of the Atlantic.

title: 267 Arctic Yager
description: A long-tailed jaeger shown over open water, the slender central rectrices streaming behind a sharply patterned body. The plate documents a high-Arctic breeder that follows seabird colonies south, robbing them of their catch in piratical chases.

title: 268 American Woodcock
description: An American woodcock half-hidden in leaf litter, the long bill and large eyes set high on the head rendered with anatomical care. Audubon's woodland setting reflects the bird's crepuscular habits and probing search for earthworms in damp forest floors.

title: 269 Greenshank
description: A greenshank standing tall on its namesake olive-green legs, slightly upturned bill held alert. The aquatint records a Eurasian wader that occasionally appears on American shores, with the soft grey-and-white winter plumage carefully picked out.

title: 27 Red headed Woodpecker
description: A boldly patterned woodpecker with a crimson head, white belly, and ink-black wings, shown clinging to bark in characteristic vertical poise. Audubon's hand-coloured aquatint captures the bird's habit of caching acorns in tree crevices across the open oak woodlands of eastern North America.

title: 270 Stormy Petrel
description: A storm petrel skimming low over the waves, its small dark form and white rump patch finely engraved. The plate captures the swallow-like flight by which these tiny seabirds patter across the surface, picking at oily morsels in open water.

title: 271 Frigate Pelican
description: A magnificent frigatebird with deeply forked tail and angular wings, rendered against an oceanic sky. Audubon emphasises the long hooked bill that the species uses to harry boobies and tropicbirds into giving up their catch in mid-air.

title: 272 Richardson's Jager
description: A parasitic jaeger, here under its older name for Sir John Richardson, shown in the dark morph that occurs across the species. The plate captures the falcon-like wings and pointed central tail feathers used in agile aerial pursuits of terns and small gulls.

title: 273 Cayenne Tern
description: A Cayenne tern shown with its yellow-tipped bill and shaggy black crest, perched along a sandy shore. The aquatint records a southern subspecies of the Sandwich tern, a slender seabird that hovers and plunges for small fish in coastal shallows.

title: 274 Semipalmated Snipe
description: A willet in breeding plumage, the long straight bill and grey-brown body opening to reveal the striking black-and-white wing pattern in flight. Audubon's plate shows both standing and flying postures to record the field marks that emerge only when the wings unfold.

title: 275 Noddy Tern
description: A brown noddy with its sooty body and pale cap, perched on a tropical branch. The plate reflects the species' colonial nesting on warm-water islands, where birds build flat platforms in low trees rather than scraping in the sand like most terns.

title: 276 King Duck
description: A king eider drake displaying its extraordinary head, with slate-blue crown, pearly cheeks and bulbous orange forehead shield rendered in detail. Audubon pairs the male with a more soberly mottled female, contrasting the species' dramatic sexual dimorphism on Arctic seas.

title: 277 Hutchins's Barnacle Goose
description: A small Canada goose form, then named for naval officer Thomas Hutchins, depicted standing alert on tundra ground. The plate captures the compact body and short neck that distinguish this northern subspecies, now generally treated as a cackling goose by ornithologists.
credit: This file comes from the Biodiversity Heritage Library , and is available online at biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40392741 .

title: 278 Schinz's Sandpiper restored
description: A small sandpiper attributed to Schinz, with the streaked breast and decurved bill that fit it equally to a dunlin or white-rumped sandpiper. The plate stands at the centre of a long taxonomic confusion stretching from Brehm's 1822 description through Bonaparte and Coues.

title: 279 Cabot's Tern
description: A Cabot's tern with shaggy black crest and yellow bill tip, rendered in clean profile against the page. The aquatint marks this American population of what was long considered a Sandwich tern, whose taxonomic status has shifted between subspecies and full species over time.

title: 28 Solitary Flycatcher or Vireo
description: A small olive-and-white songbird with bold white spectacles, perched among foliage. Now known as the Blue-headed Vireo, the plate captures the deliberate, gleaning movements that distinguish vireos from the more aerial true flycatchers.

title: 280 Black Tern
description: A small marsh tern in sooty breeding plumage, depicted in mid-flight over inland wetlands. The aquatint emphasises the bird's buoyant, dipping foraging style as it plucks insects from the water surface, a behaviour distinguishing it from its coastal relatives.

title: 281 Great White Heron
description: A towering all-white wading bird with a heavy yellow bill, stalking shallows of the Florida Keys. Audubon treated the form as a separate species, and the plate's life-size composition required folding the long neck to fit the double-elephant folio sheet.

title: 282 White-winged silvery Gull
description: A pale-mantled gull rendered in soft greys against a marine horizon. The plate belongs to Audubon's later seabird sequence, drawn during voyages along the Labrador coast where he documented species new to North American ornithology.

title: 283 Wandering Shearwater
description: A long-winged pelagic seabird shown gliding low across an Atlantic swell. The aquatint conveys the stiff-winged shearing flight that gives the family its name, with subtle tonal washes describing the dark upperparts and pale underbody.

title: 284 Purple Sandpiper
description: A stocky shorebird in winter plumage, picking through wave-washed rocks. Audubon's etched line and watercolour wash describe the bird's slate-grey back and yellow legs, and place it in the rugged tidal habitat it favours along North Atlantic coasts.

title: 285 Fork-tailed Gull
description: A graceful gull with a deeply forked tail and dark hood, shown banking over open ocean. The plate documents a high-Arctic pelagic species rarely encountered ashore, drawn from specimens collected during the northern expeditions of Audubon's contemporaries.

title: 286 White-fronted Goose
description: A grey-brown goose with a distinctive white facial blaze and barred belly, set in a marshland vignette. Audubon's composition pairs adult and juvenile to show plumage development, a comparative device he used throughout the waterfowl plates.

title: 287 Ivory Gull
description: An all-white high-Arctic gull with jet-black legs, perched on pack ice. The aquatint relies on minimal tonal modelling to render the pure plumage, a technical challenge Robert Havell Jr. solved with the lightest of aquatint grounds.

title: 288 Yellow Shank
description: A slender wading sandpiper with bright yellow legs, shown in a brackish pool. The plate corresponds to what is now called the Lesser Yellowlegs, captured mid-stride to show the bird's restless, high-stepping foraging gait.

title: 289 Solitary Sandpiper
description: A dark-backed inland sandpiper bobbing along a wooded stream edge. Unlike its flock-forming relatives, the species nests alone in old songbird nests in northern conifer forests, a life history Audubon noted in the accompanying Ornithological Biography.

title: 29 Towhe Bunting
description: A long-tailed ground-dwelling sparrow with black hood, rufous flanks, and white belly, scratching among leaf litter. Audubon's vignette shows the species' two-footed backward kick foraging behaviour, a habit unusual among North American passerines.

title: 290 Red backed Sandpiper
description: A small shorebird in rich breeding plumage with a black belly patch, the form now called Dunlin. Audubon's aquatint shows the species probing wet mudflats, the long down-curved bill rendered with delicate engraved line.

title: 291 Herring Gull
description: A large grey-mantled gull with a yellow bill and pink legs, shown standing on a rocky shore. The plate pairs adult and mottled juvenile to show the four-year plumage progression typical of the larger gulls.

title: 292 Crested Grebe
description: A diving waterbird with elaborate rufous and black head plumes raised in display. The composition places the bird low on a glassy lake surface, emphasising the lobed feet and almost tail-less profile that adapt the species for underwater pursuit.

title: 293 Large billed Puffin
description: A black-and-white seabird with a deep, brightly banded breeding bill, perched on a sea cliff. The plate documents what Audubon proposed as a distinct species; later ornithologists reclassified the form within the Atlantic Puffin's variation.

title: 294 Pectoral Sandpiper
description: A medium-sized sandpiper with a sharply demarcated streaked breast, foraging in wet grass. Aquatint stippling describes the cryptic plumage that camouflages the species on the Arctic tundra where it breeds.

title: 295 Manks Shearwater
description: A black-above, white-below pelagic seabird, depicted skimming the trough of a wave. Audubon based the plate on specimens taken offshore of New England, documenting a transatlantic species whose nocturnal burrow-nesting habits were then poorly understood.

title: 296 Barnacle Goose
description: A boldly patterned goose with a white face, black neck, and silvery barred back, shown in a coastal marsh. Medieval naturalists believed the species hatched from goose-barnacles, a folk-tale Audubon's text in the Ornithological Biography quietly displaces.

title: 297 Harlequin Duck
description: A small sea duck with intricate slate-blue, chestnut, and white markings on the male, paired with a soberly mottled female. Audubon places the pair on rocky water to evoke the fast mountain streams and surf zones the species inhabits.

title: 298 Red-necked Grebe
description: A diving waterbird with a chestnut neck and pale cheek patches in breeding dress, set on still water. The plate's low waterline and reflected plumage show Havell's skill at adapting Audubon's watercolours into atmospheric aquatint.

title: 299 Dusky Petrel
description: A small dark seabird with a slim bill, shown pattering across the ocean surface. The aquatint depicts a tropical-Atlantic shearwater rare in northern waters, drawn from specimens collected during Gulf Stream voyages.

title: 3 Prothonotary Warbler restored
description: Brilliant golden-yellow warblers move through a tangle of cane and creeper at the edge of a southern swamp. Audubon drew the species in the bottomland habitat where it nests in flooded tree cavities, a detail he stressed in the accompanying Ornithological Biography.

title: 30 Vigor's Warbler
description: A small wood warbler, named by Audubon for the British zoologist Nicholas Aylward Vigors, perches among slender twigs. The species is now considered a probable juvenile or hybrid, making the plate an artefact of early American ornithological taxonomy.

title: 300 Golden Plover
description: A medium plover in dark-bellied breeding plumage spangled with gold, shown on open ground. Audubon documents a long-distance migrant whose autumn flocks once darkened American skies before market hunting reduced the population.

title: 301 Canvas backed Duck
description: A large diving duck with a chestnut head and pale grey back, paired with a brown female on still water. The plate references the species' association with wild celery on Chesapeake Bay, the staple food that gave its flesh a celebrated flavour in nineteenth-century markets.

title: 302 Dusky Duck
description: A dark, mottled-brown dabbling duck with a paler head, set among emergent marsh plants. Now known as the American Black Duck, the plate captures the cryptic plumage that distinguishes the species from its closest relative, the Mallard.

title: 303 Bartram Sandpiper
description: A long-necked grassland shorebird with a small dove-like head, shown standing tall in open prairie. The species, now called Upland Sandpiper, was named by Audubon in honour of the Philadelphia naturalist William Bartram.

title: 304 Turn-stone
description: A short-legged shorebird with a harlequin orange, black, and white pattern, shown levering a pebble with its wedge-shaped bill. The plate illustrates the species' eponymous foraging method of flipping stones and shells along the tideline.

title: 305 Purple Gallinule
description: A vivid waterbird with iridescent violet-blue plumage, a red-and-yellow bill, and yellow legs, walking across floating leaves. The plate places the species in southern swamp vegetation where its disproportionately long toes spread its weight over lily pads.

title: 306 Great Northern Diver or Loon
description: A Common Loon glides low across glassy water, the checkerboard of white spots on its black back rendered in the precise hand-coloured aquatint that defined the double-elephant folio. Audubon arranged the bird life-size, the long body stretching nearly the full sheet to convey its diving build.

title: 307 Blue Crane or Heron
description: A Little Blue Heron stands in a hunched stalking pose, slate plumage washed with the soft purple cast of its breeding crest. The composition crops marsh grasses tightly around the legs, a typical Havell-engraved aquatint with watercolour finish.

title: 308 Tell-tale Godwit or Snipe
description: The Greater Yellowlegs, then called Tell-tale Godwit for its alarm calls that betrayed hunters, is shown mid-stride on a tidal flat. Fine etched stippling defines the speckled back while flat washes carry the pale belly and yellow legs.

title: 309 Great Tern
description: A Common Tern banks against an open sky, wings spread to display the dark primary wedge and forked tail. Audubon favoured these dynamic flight studies for seabirds, drawn from freshly shot specimens wired into lifelike postures.

title: 31 White-headed Eagle
description: A Bald Eagle stands over a freshly killed catfish, gripping the pale belly of the prey with one taloned foot. The grim, unsentimental treatment of carrion and predation is characteristic of Audubon's approach to North American raptors.

title: 310 Spotted Sandpiper
description: Two Spotted Sandpipers occupy a rocky shoreline, their breeding plumage carrying the heavy round spots that vanish in winter. Audubon often paired adults to compress life history into a single sheet of the Birds of America.

title: 311 American White Pelican
description: An American White Pelican fills the folio sheet, its vast bill resting along the breast and the breeding-season horn rising from the upper mandible. The compressed pose was Audubon's standard solution for fitting outsized waterbirds within the double-elephant format.

title: 312 Long-tailed Duck
description: Long-tailed Ducks, then called Old-squaws, are shown in winter pied plumage with the drake's slender tail trailing behind. Audubon studied them on the Atlantic coast where they wintered in noisy rafts.

title: 313 Blue-Winged Teal
description: A pair of Blue-winged Teal rest on a quiet bank, the drake's white facial crescent crisply lifted from the cinnamon head. The pale blue forewing patch that names the species is folded just visible along the flank.

title: 314 Black-headed Gull
description: A Black-headed Gull, a chiefly Old World species recorded as a North American vagrant, hovers above water with wings cocked. The aquatint captures the dark chocolate hood that gives the bird its name only in summer dress.

title: 315 Red-breasted Sandpiper
description: The Red Knot, here under its older common name, is shown in the rust-coloured breeding plumage that earns the species its alternative title. Audubon paired it with a winter-plumaged bird to record the dramatic seasonal change.

title: 316 Black-bellied Darter
description: An Anhinga perches with wings spread to dry, the long serpentine neck and silver-streaked scapulars defining the cormorant-like swimmer. Audubon worked the bird from southern swamp habitat, then a frontier subject for natural history.

title: 317 Black
description: Surf Scoters ride a low swell, the drake's piebald head with its multicoloured bill rendered in dense aquatint stipple. The plate represents Audubon's interest in coastal sea ducks gathered along the New England shore.

title: 318 American Avocet
description: An American Avocet wades on stilt-like blue legs, the upturned bill sweeping water for invertebrates. The cinnamon head and neck mark breeding plumage; the plate uses a low horizon to emphasise the bird's improbable proportions.

title: 319 Lesser Tern
description: Least Terns hover and dive over a stretch of sand, their black-capped, yellow-billed forms cropped close to the waterline. The colony-nesting seabird remains one of the smallest terns in the Americas.

title: 32 Black-billed Cuckoo
description: A Black-billed Cuckoo perches among magnolia leaves and large pink blooms, its long graduated tail and slender bill set against the foliage. Audubon's botanical backgrounds were typically painted by his assistants Joseph Mason or George Lehman.

title: 320 Little Sandpiper
description: The Least Sandpiper, North America's smallest shorebird, is shown probing wet mud with its short downcurved bill. Audubon recorded the yellowish legs that distinguish it from the otherwise similar Semipalmated Sandpiper.

title: 321 Roseate Spoonbill
description: A Roseate Spoonbill bends its spatulate bill to feed, the deep pink wing coverts and orange tail set against a Florida backwater. The plate is among the most flamboyant in Birds of America and helped fix the species in the public imagination.

title: 322 Red-headed Duck
description: A Redhead drake floats with its chestnut head and pale grey back distinct from the closely related Canvasback. Audubon's diving ducks were drawn from market-gunned specimens shot in Chesapeake Bay.

title: 323 Black Skimmer or Shearwater
description: A Black Skimmer cuts low over calm water, its outsized lower mandible slicing the surface for small fish. The asymmetrical bill, longer below than above, is unique among birds and was carefully measured in Audubon's drawing.

title: 324 Bonapartian Gull
description: A Bonaparte's Gull, named by Audubon for the ornithologist Charles Lucien Bonaparte, hovers in light tern-like flight. The slate hood of breeding plumage and clean white wedge along the primaries are precisely registered in aquatint.

title: 325 Buffel-headed Duck
description: A Bufflehead drake displays the puffy white-and-iridescent head pattern that gives the species its old name. Audubon paired the small diver with a more sober female to document the strong sexual dimorphism.

title: 326 Gannet
description: A Northern Gannet stands on a rocky ledge, the cream-washed head and pale eye marking adult plumage. Audubon visited Bird Rock in the Gulf of St Lawrence in 1833 and based later studies on observations from that breeding colony.

title: 327 Shoveller Duck
description: Northern Shovelers swim with the drake's iridescent green head leading and the broad spatulate bill held just above the water. The bill's lamellae, used to filter feed, are detailed in the aquatint as fine parallel hatching.

title: 328 Long-legged Avocet
description: The Black-necked Stilt, here under an older common name, balances on improbably long pink legs, its black-and-white plumage held against a thin marsh horizon. Audubon emphasises the slender form by giving the figure almost the entire vertical sheet.

title: 329 Yellow-breasted Rail
description: A Yellow Rail crouches in dense sedges, the buff-and-black striping providing camouflage that made the bird notoriously hard to flush. Audubon's plate is among the earliest detailed depictions of this secretive marsh species.

title: 33 American Goldfinch
description: Goldfinches in summer yellow perch among thistle, the seedhead Audubon repeatedly used to characterise this species' diet. The plate is among the earlier engravings in the series, executed initially by W. H. Lizars before Havell took over production.

title: 330 Ring Plover
description: The Semipalmated Plover, formerly grouped with the Eurasian Ringed Plover, runs along wet sand in its breeding necklace of black. Audubon worked from coastal birds and recorded the partial webbing between the toes that gives the species its modern name.

title: 331 Goosander
description: A Common Merganser, called Goosander in Britain, swims with the drake's dark green head and white flanks stretched along the surface. The serrated bill used to grasp slippery fish is drawn in fine etched detail.

title: 332 Pied Duck
description: The Labrador Duck, extinct since around 1878, appears here in piebald drake plumage and brown female dress. Audubon's plate is now a historical document of one of the first North American birds lost to human pressure.

title: 333 Green Heron
description: A small wading heron shown in profile beside reedy water, rendered in the hand-coloured aquatint technique that defines the double-elephant folio. The compressed pose accommodates the life-size format Audubon insisted upon for every species in the project.

title: 334 Black-bellied Plover
description: A coastal shorebird depicted in breeding plumage, its black underparts and silvery dappled back picked out in fine engraved line and aquatint wash. The low marshland setting reflects Audubon's habit of placing each species in its working habitat.

title: 335 Red-breasted Snipe
description: Long-billed dowitchers, then known as red-breasted snipe, probe a shoreline in postures observed during Audubon's wetland field studies. The plate uses Robert Havell Jr.'s aquatint and engraving to render the streaked, cinnamon-toned breeding dress.

title: 336 Yellow-Crowned Heron
description: A yellow-crowned night heron stalks a crab among coastal vegetation, the recurved plumes and slate body tones built up through layered aquatint. The composition uses the bend of the heron's neck to fit the bird at full scale within the folio sheet.

title: 337 American Bittern
description: The cryptic marsh heron is shown in its characteristic upright freeze pose, neck extended skyward to mimic surrounding reeds. Audubon's plate captures the streaked brown camouflage in the fine tonal gradations possible through aquatint.

title: 338 Bemaculated Duck
description: A diving duck of the goldeneye group, the so-called bemaculated duck is now considered a variant rather than a distinct species. The plate pairs a swimming bird with a preening companion to record both head pattern and scapular detail.

title: 339 Little Auk
description: The dovekie, a stub-billed pelagic auk of the North Atlantic, appears swimming and in flight against an open sea. The black-and-white plumage is articulated through dense aquatint stippling rather than line, a Havell speciality.

title: 34 Worm eating Warbler
description: A buff-toned warbler with bold black head stripes clings to woodland foliage, holding the larvae from which it takes its name. The plate is from the early Lizars-engraved portion of the folio, later reissued by Havell.

title: 340 Least Stormy-Petrel
description: A tiny pelagic petrel skims a wave trough, wings raised in the fluttering flight that earned the family its name. Audubon shows the species over open ocean, a setting rare in the folio and demanding extensive aquatint tone for the swell.

title: 341 Great Auk
description: The flightless North Atlantic auk is shown swimming with a fish, drawn from specimens since the bird was already vanishing toward its 1844 extinction. The plate is now treated as a key visual record of a species Audubon never observed alive.

title: 342 Golden-Eye Duck
description: A drake and hen common goldeneye are arranged on water, the drake's white cheek-spot and bottle-green head set against finely aquatinted ripples. The pair format allowed Audubon to register sexual dimorphism within a single plate.

title: 343 Ruddy Duck
description: A stiff-tailed diving duck shown with its tail cocked vertically, a courtship posture characteristic of the species. The chestnut body and bright blue bill of the breeding male required carefully registered hand colouring over the aquatint base.

title: 344 Long-legged Sandpiper
description: The stilt sandpiper, here under its older name, stands in shallow water in worn breeding plumage. Havell's aquatint renders the barred underparts with a dotted texture distinct from the smoother washes used for the back.

title: 345 American Widgeon
description: A drake American wigeon displays the white forecrown and green eye-stripe that distinguish it from its Eurasian cousin. The bird is set on still water, with reflections built up by selective biting of the aquatint plate.

title: 346 Black-Throated Diver
description: The black-throated loon, called diver in British usage, is shown in breeding plumage with its striped neck patch. The vermiculated back pattern is achieved by very fine engraved cross-hatching beneath the aquatint tone.

title: 347 Smew or White Nun
description: A drake smew, a small Eurasian sawbill that strays to North America, is rendered in his crisp white plumage with black eye-mask. The plate's negative-space approach leaves the paper itself to carry most of the bird's body.

title: 348 Gadwall Duck
description: A pair of gadwall, a quietly patterned dabbling duck, are placed in shallow marsh water. The intricate vermiculation of the drake's flanks is one of Havell's more demanding aquatint passages in the waterfowl series.

title: 349 Least Water-hen
description: The diminutive black rail, here as least water-hen, is shown in dense marsh vegetation that nearly conceals the bird. The plate reflects how rarely Audubon could observe a species that remains among North America's most secretive rails.

title: 35 Children's Warbler
description: A small wood-warbler named after John George Children of the British Museum, now considered a juvenile yellow warbler rather than a distinct species. The plate is a record of the taxonomic uncertainty that surrounded many small migrants in Audubon's day.

title: 350 Rocky Mountain Plover
description: The mountain plover stands on dry shortgrass terrain rather than the shoreline typical of its family. Audubon emphasised the unmarked breast and pale legs that distinguish this prairie species from its coastal relatives.

title: 351 Great Cinereous Owl
description: A great grey owl perches on a broken branch, the concentric facial discs rendered through circular sweeps of aquatint tone. The plate captures the disproportion between the species' enormous head and its relatively slight body.

title: 352 Black-Winged Hawk
description: The white-tailed kite, then called black-winged hawk, hovers with raised wings above grassland. Audubon often drew raptors in active flight rather than at rest, requiring Havell to model the underwing through graduated aquatint.

title: 353 I. Chesnut-backed Titmouse - 2. Black-capt Titmouse - 3. Chesnut-crowned Titmouse
description: Three small chickadees and titmice are arranged on a single branch as a comparative study, a format Audubon used for closely related songbirds. The plate sets chestnut-backed and boreal forms beside a more familiar black-capped relative.

title: 354 I. Louisiana Tanager - 2. Scarlet Tanager
description: A western tanager, then called Louisiana tanager, shares foliage with a scarlet tanager, contrasting two of North America's most saturated red plumages. The double subject required careful registration of two distinct red mixtures in the hand colouring.

title: 355 MacGillivray's Finch
description: A coastal seaside sparrow named for the Scottish naturalist William MacGillivray, Audubon's London editor. The streaked olive plumage and yellow lore are placed against salt marsh grasses to reflect the species' restricted habitat.

title: 356 Marsh Hawk
description: The northern harrier, here as marsh hawk, glides low with wings held in the species' characteristic shallow dihedral. The plate emphasises the white rump patch that remains the field mark used to identify the bird in flight.

title: 357 American Magpie
description: A black-billed magpie perches with its long graduated tail extended, the iridescent blue-green wing and tail tones laid in over a black aquatint base. Audubon encountered this corvid during his western travels along the upper Missouri.

title: 358 Pine Grosbeak
description: A male pine grosbeak in rosy breeding plumage feeds among conifer needles, the heavy bill characteristic of the boreal finches. The plate uses a vertical pine sprig to anchor a relatively quiet, single-bird composition.

title: 359 I. Arkansaw Flycatcher - 2. Swallow-Tailed Flycatcher - 3. Says Flycatcher
description: Three western tyrant flycatchers are grouped on a single branch: western kingbird, scissor-tailed flycatcher and Say's phoebe. The arrangement compares tail shape and underpart colour across species Audubon studied during his expeditions west of the Mississippi.

title: 36 Stanley Hawk
description: A Cooper's Hawk, here under its old vernacular Stanley Hawk, drops on a covey of Northern Bobwhite, talons spread. The dynamic prey-strike composition is one of the recurring set-pieces Audubon used to differentiate accipiters from buteos.

title: 360 I. Winter Wren - 2. Rock Wren
description: Two diminutive North American wrens share a single sheet, the Winter Wren perched among forest debris while the Rock Wren clings to broken stone. Hand-coloured aquatint after watercolour, with double-strike etched lines giving the plumage its fine barred texture.

title: 361 Long-tailed or Dusky Grous
description: A male Dusky Grouse displayed on a Rocky Mountain branch, its long tail spread to show the slate barring and yellow comb above the eye. The plate relies on Robert Havell Jr.'s aquatint ground to render the soft, smoke-grey body feathers in graduated tone.

title: 362 I. Yellow billed Magpie - 2. Stellers Jay - 3. Ultramarine Jay - 4. Clark's Crow
description: A composite plate of four western corvids gathered on a single oak branch, including the California-endemic Yellow-billed Magpie and the crested Steller's Jay. Such grouped plates condensed Audubon's later Pacific-coast specimens, drawn from skins forwarded by Townsend and Nuttall.

title: 363 Bohemian Chatterer
description: A pair of Bohemian Waxwings perched on a fruiting branch, their silky cinnamon plumage punctuated by red sealing-wax tips on the secondaries. The aquatint tone captures the smooth, almost powdered quality of waxwing feathers.

title: 364 White-winged Crossbill
description: Several White-winged Crossbills work cones on a spruce sprig, the crossed mandibles drawn in profile to show their seed-extracting function. Crimson males and olive females are arranged to display sexual dimorphism on a single page.

title: 365 Lapland Long-spur
description: Lapland Longspurs in breeding and winter plumage on a tussock of grass, illustrating the seasonal change between a black-faced summer male and the streaked, sparrow-like winter bird. Drawn from specimens taken on Audubon's Labrador expedition.

title: 366 Iceland or Jer Falcon
description: Two Gyrfalcons stand over prey, one in the white Greenland morph and the other in darker plumage, emphasising the wide colour range of this circumpolar raptor. The white bird is rendered almost in reserve against the paper, with aquatint shadow giving the body modelling.

title: 367 Band-tailed Pigeon
description: A western Band-tailed Pigeon perched among oak foliage, the broad terminal tail-band and iridescent neck patch carefully delineated. The species was newly described to eastern audiences when this plate appeared in the double elephant folio.

title: 368 Rock Grous
description: A Rock Ptarmigan on tundra rubble, shown in the mottled summer plumage that camouflages it among lichen-covered stones. Audubon worked from skins collected on the Arctic coast, since the species does not occur in the lower United States.

title: 369 I. Mountain Mocking bird - 2. Varied Thrush
description: A Sage Thrasher and a Varied Thrush share a sheet of Pacific Northwest specimens, both birds based on study skins from the Wyeth-Townsend expedition. The plate pairs a desert-sagebrush songbird with a humid coniferous-forest thrush.

title: 37 Golden-winged Woodpecker
description: A family group of Northern Flickers, then known as Golden-winged Woodpeckers, arranged on a branch to show the bright yellow shafts of the wing and tail feathers. The composition presents male, female and juveniles together in the manner of a comparative life history.

title: 370 American Water Ouzel
description: An American Dipper perched on a wet stone in a fast stream, the stocky grey body and short cocked tail noted for its underwater foraging habit. Audubon arranged the bird against splashing water to allude to the species' diving behaviour.

title: 371 Cock of the Plains
description: A male Greater Sage-Grouse in lek display, air sacs inflated over a white ruff while the spike-tailed female crouches nearby. The plate documents a sagebrush species Audubon never saw alive, drawn from Townsend specimens.

title: 372 Common Buzzard
description: A Common Buzzard perched on a stump with a captured snake, included in the American series on the basis of a single questionable specimen. The bird is Eurasian and the record is now considered erroneous, but the figure remains in the folio sequence.

title: 373 I. Evening Grosbeak - 2. Spotted Grosbeak
description: Evening Grosbeak and Black-headed Grosbeak share a flowering branch, contrasting the heavy yellow western finch with the cinnamon-breasted Black-headed. The composition stresses the disproportionately massive seed-cracking bills of both species.

title: 374 Sharp-shinned Hawk
description: A pair of Sharp-shinned Hawks attacks smaller songbirds in mid-air, the slate-backed adult and brown juvenile shown together. The plate exemplifies Audubon's preference for raptors caught in the act of predation rather than at static rest.

title: 375 Lesser Red-Poll
description: Common Redpolls feed on a slender branch of birch, the small streaked finches with rosy crowns rendered in delicate engraved line. Their fondness for catkin seeds, suggested in the foliage choice, is a hallmark of Audubon's habitat-aware staging.

title: 376 Trumpeter Swan
description: An adult Trumpeter Swan arches its neck to drink from a marsh pool, the long body filling almost the full sheet of the double elephant folio. The plate is one of the largest single-bird compositions in the series, exploiting the format for North America's heaviest waterfowl.

title: 377 Scolopaceus Courlan
description: A Limpkin wades among aquatic vegetation, the long decurved bill specialised for extracting apple snails from their shells. Audubon observed the species in Florida and rendered the shaggy brown plumage with white drop-shaped streaks.

title: 378 Hawk Owl
description: A Northern Hawk Owl perches upright on a dead conifer stub, the long tail and falcon-like silhouette distinguishing it from typical owls. The diurnal hunting habit is suggested by the open daylight setting, unusual for owl plates.

title: 379 Ruff-necked Humming-bird
description: Several Rufous Hummingbirds hover at a flowering branch, the male's iridescent gorget shifting from copper to flame depending on angle. The plate gathers multiple postures to compensate for the difficulty of catching the bird at rest.

title: 38 Kentucky Warbler
description: Two olive-and-yellow warblers with bold black sideburns perch on a flowering magnolia branch. The composition pairs an eastern songbird with a southern bloom, a habit Audubon used to compress habitat and season into one engraved sheet.

title: 380 Tengmalm's Owl
description: A Boreal Owl perched against dark conifer foliage, the squarish facial disc and white-spotted forehead drawn from a northern specimen. Its scarcity in the lower latitudes made the species a rarity in the folio.

title: 381 Snow Goose
description: An adult white-morph Snow Goose stands beside a darker first-year bird, contrasting the species' plumage stages. The black wing tips on the white adult are a recurring identification cue Audubon emphasised through aquatint shading.

title: 382 Sharp-tailed Grous
description: Sharp-tailed Grouse perch on a prairie shrub, the pointed central tail feathers and pale spotted underparts characterising the species. Drawn from upper Missouri specimens, the plate represents the open-country grouse of the northern plains.

title: 383 Long-eared Owl
description: A Long-eared Owl turns its tufted head from a perch among dense branches, its mottled brown plumage breaking up against the bark. The composition shows the cryptic roosting posture by which the bird disappears into wooded cover during the day.

title: 384 Black-Throated Bunting
description: A pair of Dickcissels on grain stalks, the male's yellow breast crossed by a black bib reminiscent of a meadowlark in miniature. Audubon set them in cultivated grasses to allude to the species' preference for grain fields and old prairie.

title: 385 I. Bank Swallow - 2. Violet-green Swallow
description: A Bank Swallow at a sandy burrow entrance is paired with the western Violet-green Swallow, contrasting brown plumage against iridescent green and purple. The grouping aligns nesting habit with geographical range across one sheet.

title: 386 White Heron
description: A Great Egret in breeding plumage stands amid coastal vegetation, long aigrette plumes cascading down its back. The plate documents the species before late nineteenth-century plume hunting nearly drove it to extinction.

title: 387 Glossy Ibis
description: A Glossy Ibis probes a wetland margin, the curved bill and mahogany body washed with green-bronze iridescence rendered through layered aquatint and watercolour. Audubon based the figure on a Florida specimen of this then-uncommon coastal wader.

title: 388 I. Nuttall's Starling - 2. Yellow-headed Troopial - 3. Bullock's Oriole
description: Three western blackbirds and an oriole share a slender branch, their black, yellow, and orange plumages set off against bare paper. The aquatint and engraved line, hand-coloured under Audubon's direction in Robert Havell's London workshop, register every barb of the wing feathers.

title: 389 Red-Cockaded Woodpecker
description: A pair of pine-dwelling woodpeckers cling to a longleaf trunk, the male's tiny scarlet cockade barely visible behind the eye. Audubon's double elephant folio fits the birds at life size, and the etched bark conveys the resin-streaked pines of the southern coastal plain.

title: 39 Crested Titmouse
description: A pair of tufted titmice work over an oak twig hung with acorns, the upright crest and dark eye giving each a sharp profile. The plate compresses the bird's preferred habitat — eastern hardwood mast — into a single engraved branch.

title: 390 I. Lark Finch - 2. Prairie Finch - 3. Brown Song Sparrow
description: Three sparrow-like birds of the Great Plains and Pacific slope are arranged on a low rock with a sprig of grass. The plate gathers species Audubon worked up from western specimens supplied by Townsend and Nuttall, drawn at life size for the engraved folio.

title: 391 Brant Goose
description: A small dark sea goose stands on a shoreline strewn with eelgrass, its head and neck inky against a pale tidal sky. Audubon shows the bird in the low, ground-hugging pose typical of brants on the Atlantic coast, where they overwinter on the grass beds they feed on.

title: 392 Louisiana Hawk
description: A broad-shouldered raptor of the southern marshes grips its prey on a low perch, dark wings half-mantled. The plate, etched and aquatinted by Havell, preserves Audubon's habit of staging hawks mid-action rather than as static specimens.

title: 393 I. Townsend's Warbler - 2. Arctic Blue-bird - 3. Western Blue-bird
description: Three small western birds, recently described from Pacific Northwest collections, share a flowering branch. Audubon assembled the plate from skins forwarded by John Kirk Townsend, allowing him to add Pacific species to a work otherwise built on his own field studies.

title: 394 I. Chestnut-coloured Finch - 2. Black-headed Siskin - 3. Black crown Bunting - 4. Arctic Ground Finch
description: Four small seed-eaters from the western interior are stacked on twigs and a low stone, their ruddy, black, and streaked plumages contrasted side by side. Combination plates like this allowed Audubon to keep the folio's species count climbing as new western specimens reached him.

title: 395 I. Audubon's Warbler - 2. Hermit Warbler - 3. Black-throated gray Warbler
description: Three western wood-warblers, all named from Townsend and Nuttall's expedition skins, gather on a single conifer spray. The plate is one of several near the end of the folio that introduced Pacific species to European subscribers for the first time.

title: 396 Burgomaster Gull
description: A heavy pale-mantled gull stands on a rocky shore, its hooked yellow bill and cold pink legs picked out against the engraved water. Now called the glaucous gull, the bird was given its old Dutch nickname for the way it lords over Arctic colonies, robbing eggs and chicks.

title: 397 Scarlet Ibis
description: A vivid red wading bird probes a coastal shallow, its curved bill and long neck tracing an S against pale sky. Audubon based the plate on birds taken in the Gulf of Mexico, the northernmost edge of a species otherwise tied to South American mangroves.

title: 398 I. Lazuli Finch - 2. Clay-coloured Finch - 3. Oregon Snow Finch
description: Three small western buntings and sparrows share a sprig of seeding grass, the lazuli's blue head set against the duller browns of its neighbours. The grouping is one of Audubon's late composite plates assembled from skins rather than live observation.

title: 399 I. Black-throated green Warbler - 2. Blackburnian - 3. Mourning Warbler
description: Three eastern wood-warblers, including the flame-throated Blackburnian, perch among hemlock needles and a curling vine. Audubon worked the species into a single branch to pack his northeastern songbirds into the folio's later plates.

title: 4 Purple Finch
description: A pair of finches, the male flushed raspberry-red and the streaked brown female, cling to a flowering branch in mirrored poses. The composition follows the field-observed habit of pairing male and female on a single botanical specimen, a hallmark of the Birds of America project drawn from skins and live study.

title: 40 American Redstart
description: Black-and-orange flycatching warblers spread their tails through a tangled branch, flashing the pigment patches that give the species its name. Audubon shows the male in the fan-tailed display posture used to startle insects from leaves.

title: 400 I. Arkansaw Siskin - 2. Mealy Red-poll - 3. Louisiana Tanager - 4.Townsend's Finch - 5. Buff-breasted Finch
description: Five small finches and a tanager are stacked across a pine spray, the western tanager's red head burning above the duller seed-eaters. The plate marks Audubon's centenary number and shows his late reliance on study skins for species he never observed alive.

title: 401 Red-breasted Merganser
description: A drake and hen sawbill ride a low Atlantic swell, the male's ragged green crest and rust collar laid against a pale wash of horizon. Audubon paired the sexes to show the merganser's marked dimorphism in a single image.

title: 402 I. Black-throated Guillemot - 2. Nobbed-billed Auk - 3. Curled-crested Auk - 4. Horned-billed Guillemot
description: Four small North Pacific auks and auklets are arranged on bare rock, their stub bills and ornamented faces shown in profile. The plate, drawn from skins collected on the Northwest Coast, brought species new to Atlantic ornithology into the folio.

title: 403 Golden-eye Duck
description: A drake and hen common goldeneye dive in a winter bay, the male's white cheek-spot bright against his green-glossed head. Audubon's plate captures the abrupt forward thrust of a diving duck just before it submerges.

title: 404 Eared Grebe
description: A slender black-necked grebe in summer dress rides a shaded pool, its golden ear-tufts fanned behind a red eye. The bird's long neck and lobed feet are drawn with the wiry precision Havell's aquatint allowed for water birds.

title: 405 Semipalmated Sandpiper
description: A small grey-and-white sandpiper, one of the abundant peeps of the Atlantic flats, picks across wet sand. Audubon includes the partly webbed toes that give the species its name, a detail more often described than drawn.

title: 406 Trumpeter Swan
description: A massive white swan arches its neck along the full height of the double elephant folio sheet, snapping at a small moth. Audubon bent the bird's neck into a tight curl so it could stand at life size on a page just over a metre tall — one of his most-discussed compositional solutions.

title: 407 Dusky Albatros
description: A long-winged southern seabird banks over an open ocean horizon, its sooty plumage dark against a pale wash. Audubon worked the bird up from a specimen rather than field encounter, and the plate is among the few in the folio devoted to a Southern Hemisphere species.

title: 408 American Scoter Duck
description: A jet-black sea duck with an orange-knobbed bill rides a low swell, a hen tucked behind. The black scoter winters in rafts along the North American coasts, and Audubon's etching keeps the matte plumage from going flat by varnishing the highlight on the bill.

title: 409 I. Havell's Tern - 2. Trudeau's Tern
description: Two slender terns wheel across a coastal sky, their forked tails and slate-tipped wings set against bare paper. Audubon named one of the birds for his London engraver Robert Havell, the first such naming in the folio.

title: 41 Ruffed Grouse
description: A male Ruffed Grouse fans his black-banded tail and erects the dark neck ruff in courtship display, while females and a chick feed in autumn underbrush. Audubon shows the bird amid grapevine and dogwood, signalling the eastern hardwood habitat he knew from Pennsylvania.

title: 410 Marsh Tern
description: A pale tern with black cap and forked tail courses low over a salt marsh, snapping at insects above the cordgrass. Now identified with the gull-billed tern, the species hawks for prey rather than plunge-diving like most of its relatives.

title: 411 Common American Swan
description: A tundra swan stands on a quiet shore, the small yellow lore-spot at the base of the bill picked out in colour. Audubon distinguished the species from the larger trumpeter on the basis of voice and bill markings, an early American contribution to swan taxonomy.

title: 412 I. Violet-green Cormorant - 2. Townsend's Cormorant
description: Two Pacific cormorants perch on a sea stack, their iridescent throats and slim crests catching a low light. The plate draws on skins gathered by John Kirk Townsend on the Columbia River, where these species nest on coastal cliffs.

title: 413 California Partridge
description: A covey of crested California quail forages through low chaparral, the male's curling topknot tipped forward. Audubon worked the plate from skins sent east from the Pacific coast, introducing one of the West's most distinctive game birds to his subscribers.

title: 414 I. Golden-winged Warbler - 2. Cape May Warbler
description: Two small wood-warblers are arranged on slender branches, the Golden-winged showing its black throat patch and yellow wing bars beside the streaked, chestnut-cheeked Cape May. Engraved by Robert Havell Jr. from Audubon's life-size watercolour, then hand-coloured in aquatint for the double-elephant folio.

title: 415 I. Brown Creeper - 2. Californian Nuthatch
description: A Brown Creeper spirals up a furrowed trunk while the small pygmy nuthatch (here labelled Californian) perches head-down nearby, contrasting two bark-foraging strategies. The plate uses Havell's combined etching, engraving and aquatint, with watercolour applied by hand to the printed sheet.

title: 416 I. Hairy Woodpecker - 2. Red-bellied Woodpecker - 3. Red-shafted Woodpecker - 4. Lewis' Woodpecker - 5. Red-breasted Woodpecker
description: A composite study of five woodpecker species posed on dead snags, comparing crown patterns, barred backs and tail bracing across eastern and western North America. Such grouped plates appeared in the later supplementary numbers of the folio, accommodating species Audubon received from western expeditions.

title: 417 I. Maria's Woodpecker - 2. Three-toed Woodpecker - 3. Phillips' Woodpecker - 4. Canadian Woodpecker - 5. Harris's Woodpecker - 6. Audubon's Woodpecker
description: Six woodpecker forms are stacked on a single trunk, several of them now treated as regional races of the Hairy and American Three-toed Woodpeckers. The plate reflects the taxonomic optimism of 1830s ornithology, when subtle plumage variants were routinely described as full species.

title: 418 I. American Ptarmigan - 2. White-tailed Grous
description: A pair of high-latitude grouse pose against tundra plants, the Rock Ptarmigan in mottled summer plumage beside the alpine White-tailed Ptarmigan. Audubon worked from skins supplied by the Hudson's Bay Company and from Thomas Nuttall's western specimens.

title: 419 I. Little Tawny Thrush - 2. Ptiliogony's Townsendi - 3. Canada Jay
description: Three forest birds share a sprig of foliage: the russet Hermit Thrush form, Townsend's Solitaire with its pale eye-ring, and the soft grey Canada Jay. The grouping pulls together species new to science from John Kirk Townsend's Columbia River collections.

title: 42 Orchard Oriole
description: Several Orchard Orioles, including a yellow-green female and a brick-red adult male, animate a flowering locust branch with a pendulous woven nest. Audubon emphasises the species' close association with farmsteads and orchard trees of the eastern states.

title: 420 Prairie Starling
description: The Yellow-headed Blackbird, here under an obsolete name, is shown on a stalk of prairie grass, the male's golden hood set against jet plumage. Audubon based the figure on specimens from the upper Missouri, a region he reached in 1843.

title: 421 Brown Pelican
description: A Brown Pelican stands on a mangrove branch, the long pouched bill folded against the breast and the breeding-season chestnut nape clearly rendered. Audubon studied the species along the Florida Keys in 1832, and the plate is among the largest aquatints in the folio.

title: 422 Rough-legged Falcon
description: A Rough-legged Hawk grips prey on a low perch, feathered tarsi and dark belly band visible against a pale, lightly washed sky. The species winters on open North American country after breeding in the Arctic, a range Audubon noted from northern correspondents.

title: 423 I. Plumed--Partridge - 2. Thick--legged Partridge
description: Two western quail are figured together: the Mountain Quail with its long straight head plume, and the Scaled Quail showing its distinctive crescent-edged feathering. The plate draws on specimens from Townsend and Nuttall's overland expedition to the Pacific.

title: 424 I. Lazuli Finch - 2. Crimson-necked Bull-Finch - 3. Gray-crowned Linnet - 4. Cow-pen Bird - 5. Evening Grosbeak - 6. Brown Longspur
description: Six finch-like passerines crowd a single branch, ranging from the turquoise Lazuli Bunting to the heavy-billed Evening Grosbeak. Such crowded composite sheets close the folio, making efficient use of the costly copper plates as Audubon hurried to incorporate western discoveries.

title: 425 Columbian Humming Bird
description: A pair of Rufous Hummingbirds, named for the Columbia River where Townsend collected them, hover at trumpet-shaped flowers. Audubon renders the male's coppery gorget with the metallic shimmer the aquatint medium handles unusually well.

title: 426 Californian Vulture
description: An immense California Condor is shown perched, its bare orange head and white underwing linings displayed at near life size. Audubon never saw the bird alive, working from a skin in the museum at Fort Vancouver brought by David Douglas.

title: 427 I. White-legged Oyster-catcher - 2. Slender-billed Oyster-catcher
description: Two oystercatcher forms stand on a rocky shore with bright orange-red bills lowered to bivalves, one pied and one nearly all-black. Both names are now subsumed into the American Oystercatcher and Black Oystercatcher of the Pacific coast.

title: 428 Townsend's Sandpiper
description: A small shorebird in autumn plumage probes a wet shoreline, the species first described from a single specimen collected by John Kirk Townsend. Modern ornithologists treat it as an aberrant or hybrid bird rather than a valid taxon, leaving the plate a record of a vanished name.

title: 429 Western Duck
description: The Steller's Eider, called Western Duck here, drifts on calm water in the male's striking black, white and chestnut breeding pattern. Audubon worked from Bering Sea specimens supplied by Russian-American Company contacts.

title: 43 Cedar Bird
description: A flock of Cedar Waxwings perches in a fruiting branch, the silky fawn plumage, black mask and red wing tips carefully differentiated. Audubon notes their fondness for berries by surrounding them with the very fruit on which they feed.

title: 430 Slender-billed Guillemot
description: A pair of Pigeon Guillemots in summer dress sit on a sea-cliff ledge, the slim red feet and wing patches set against sooty plumage. The plate depends on specimens from Townsend's North Pacific collecting along the Oregon coast.

title: 431 American Flamingo
description: A single flamingo bows its long neck almost to the ground, its body filling the double-elephant sheet vertically so the entire bird could be shown life-size. Audubon studied the species in the Florida Keys in 1832 and the plate is among the most graphically daring of the folio.

title: 432 I. Burrowing Owl - 2. Large-headed Burrowing Owl - 3. Little night Owl - 4. Columbian Owl - 5. Short-eared Owl
description: Five small owls are arrayed against a sparse landscape, including the long-legged Burrowing Owl at its prairie-dog hole and the buoyant Short-eared Owl quartering open ground. The composition compares head shape, leg length and wing form across diurnal and nocturnal hunters.

title: 433 I. Bullock's Oriole - 2. Baltimore Oriole - 3. Mexican Goldfinch - 4. Varied Thrush - 5. Common Water Thrush
description: An assortment of bright passerines crowds a leafy branch: two oriole species, a Lesser Goldfinch, a Varied Thrush and a Northern Waterthrush. Such mixed plates were a practical way to publish western specimens that reached Audubon late in the project.

title: 434 I. Little Tyrant Fly-catcher - 2. Small-headed Fly-catcher - 3. Blue Mountain Warbler - 4. Bartran's Vireo - 5. Short-legged Pewee - 6. Rocky Mountain Fly-catcher
description: Six small insect-eaters share a single twig, several of them species known only from a handful of skins and never reliably observed since. The plate has long fascinated ornithologists as a possible record of birds now extinct or never validly described.

title: 435 I. Columbian Water Ouzel - 2. Arctic Water Ouzel
description: Two American Dippers cling to mossy stones in a fast stream, one upright and one diving for aquatic insects. The plate documents the only North American songbird that hunts under flowing water, with western specimens supplied by Townsend.

title: 44 Summer Red Bird
description: A male Summer Tanager in saturated rose-red and a duller olive female pose in a flowering branch with insect prey in the male's bill. Audubon's text emphasises the species' fondness for hornets and bees, behaviour the figures hint at directly.

title: 45 Traill's Flycatcher
description: A small olive-brown Empidonax flycatcher named after the Edinburgh naturalist Thomas Stewart Traill perches alertly on a sprig. Modern taxonomy splits the original Traill's into the Alder and Willow Flycatchers, two near-identical species separable mainly by song.

title: 46 Barred Owl
description: A Barred Owl stares forward with dark liquid eyes, vertical breast streaks contrasting with horizontal barring across the throat, gripping a captured grey squirrel. Audubon's typically narrative composition presents predator and prey on a single branch.

title: 47 Ruby-throated Humming Bird
description: Multiple Ruby-throated Hummingbirds hover and perch around trumpet-creeper blossoms, the iridescent throats catching light at different angles. Audubon used the plate to advertise the species' status as the only hummingbird breeding east of the Mississippi.

title: 48 Azure Warbler
description: A small blue-and-white warbler, now usually identified with the Cerulean Warbler, perches in tender spring foliage. Audubon worked from limited material for this species and his figure remained the standard reference for decades.

title: 49 Blue-green Warbler
description: A small olive-green songbird perches among the slender shoots and ripening fruit of a wild plant, drawn life-size in the format that defined the double-elephant folio. Hand-coloured aquatint over copper engraving renders the warbler's pale underparts with the soft tonal washes characteristic of the Havell workshop in London.

title: 5 Bonaparte's Flycatcher
description: A small flycatcher named for the ornithologist Charles Lucien Bonaparte perches among slender branches. The bird is no longer recognised as a distinct species and is generally considered a misidentified or aberrant specimen, surviving only in this plate.

title: 50 Black & Yellow Warbler
description: Now identified as the magnolia warbler, the bird is shown twisting through foliage with its yellow underparts and bold black streaks turned toward the viewer. The aquatint plate combines etched contour lines with hand-applied watercolour to capture the warbler's distinctive tail-spot pattern.

title: 51 Red-tailed Hawk
description: Two hawks grapple over a hare in a dramatic mid-air struggle, talons interlocked and wings braced against one another. The double-elephant folio's full sheet is exploited to show the raptors at life size, a scale unprecedented in ornithological publishing of the period.

title: 52 Chuck-will's Widow
description: A nightjar pair confronts a coiled snake among oak foliage, the birds' camouflaged plumage rendered in finely stippled aquatint. The narrative encounter reflects the artist's preference for staging behaviour rather than presenting specimens against blank ground.

title: 53 Painted Finch
description: The painted bunting, called the Nonpareil for its blue, green and crimson plumage, is shown with several individuals on a sprig of wild almond. The plate demonstrates the colourists' ability to register multiple unmixed pigments through the aquatint ground without muddying the saturated hues.

title: 54 Rice Bird
description: Bobolinks gather among the seed-heads of a cultivated rice plant, the breeding males in their inverted black-and-buff plumage contrasted with the streaked females. The plate documents the species under the agricultural name applied to migrating flocks that fed on Carolina rice fields.

title: 55 Cuvier's Regulus
description: A tiny crested kinglet sits on a leafy branch, its identity now considered a probable hybrid or unrepeatable observation. The plate honours Georges Cuvier and exemplifies the speculative species occasionally included from single field encounters.

title: 56 Red-shouldered Hawk
dateCreated: 1827-1838 (publication; from painting made in Louisiana c. 1826)
description: A red-shouldered hawk stoops with talons spread, banded tail flared as it descends on prey. The flight pose required the engraver Robert Havell Jr. to translate rapid wash drawing into the layered tonal bitings of aquatint without losing the gesture.

title: 57 Loggerhead Shrike
description: A pair of shrikes occupies the spiny branches of a green-briar, the predatory songbird's hooked bill and black mask carefully delineated. The botanical setting accurately records a thicket habitat where shrikes impale prey on thorns.

title: 58 Hermit Thrush
description: A hermit thrush stands on the forest floor among fallen leaves and a sprig of partridgeberry, the rufous tail set against cool grey-brown upperparts. The understated composition reflects the bird's retiring habits in eastern woodland.

title: 59 Chestnut-sided Warbler
description: Two warblers move through a flowering shrub, their yellow crowns and chestnut flanks picked out against fresh green leaves. The aquatint records a species that became more common as nineteenth-century clearing created the second-growth scrub it favours.

title: 6 Wild Turkey
description: A male wild turkey strides across the ground, iridescent body feathers and bare blue head turned in profile. The bird occupies almost the full sheet of the double-elephant folio, a deliberate scaling that opens the publication's prospectus and signals American subject matter.

title: 60 Carbonated Warbler
description: Two yellow-and-black warblers occupy a branch of a flowering tree, depicting a species known only from the artist's own report. No other naturalist has documented it, and the plate stands as the sole record of a bird that may have been a hybrid or extinct form.

title: 61 Great Horned Owl
description: A pair of great horned owls perches in heavy darkness, ear tufts raised and yellow eyes facing the viewer. The deep blacks were achieved through repeated aquatint bitings, a technique that gave the Havell workshop its reputation for nocturnal subjects.

title: 62 Passenger Pigeon
description: A male and female passenger pigeon exchange a beakful of food on a branch, the male's slate-blue head turned to the rosy female. The plate documents flock behaviour of a species then numbered in billions but driven to extinction by 1914.

title: 63 White-eyed Flycatcher or Vireo
description: A white-eyed vireo sits on a sprig with prominent fruit, the pale iris that gives the bird its name carefully picked out. The plate reflects the unsettled early-nineteenth-century classification that grouped vireos with flycatchers.

title: 64 Swamp Sparrow
description: A swamp sparrow clings to a stalk of marsh vegetation, its rusty cap and grey breast set against the muted greens of wetland reed. The composition keeps the bird at eye level among emergent stems rather than placing it in open view.

title: 65 Rathbone Warbler
description: A small warbler named in honour of the Rathbone family of Liverpool, patrons during the artist's English subscription drive. Now considered an immature or variant yellow warbler rather than a distinct species, the plate preserves a name otherwise lost from ornithology.

title: 66 Ivory-billed Woodpecker
description: Three ivory-billed woodpeckers cling to a dead trunk, the male's vermilion crest and the species' pale chisel-bill rendered at life size. The aquatint preserves a bird widely considered extinct, with the social group depicted reflecting the family parties once seen in southern bottomland forest.
credit: University of Pittsburgh, Audubon's Birds of America, Darlington Digital Library , Image

title: 67 Red-winged Starling or Marsh Blackbird
description: Red-winged blackbirds in graded plumages occupy a wild oat plant, the male's scarlet-and-yellow epaulet flashed in display. The plate uses the older common names current before the species was universally classed among American blackbirds.

title: 68 Republican or Cliff Swallow
description: Cliff swallows are shown alongside their gourd-shaped mud nests built under a rocky overhang, several birds entering and leaving the colony. The plate documents the colonial nesting biology that earned the bird its alternative name of Republican Swallow.

title: 69 Bay-breasted Warbler
description: Two bay-breasted warblers move through the foliage of a flowering branch, the male's chestnut throat and crown contrasted with creamy neck patches. The plate captures spring breeding plumage rarely encountered south of the boreal forest.

title: 7 Purple Grakle or Common Crow Blackbird
description: Common Grackles cluster on a maize stalk, ears split open to expose the kernels they raid. The composition links the species directly to its reputation as an agricultural pest, with iridescent plumage rendered through layered hand-colouring over an aquatint base.

title: 70 Henslow's Bunting
description: Henslow's sparrow, then placed among the buntings, rests on a stalk in a grassy field setting. The plate honours the Cambridge botanist John Stevens Henslow, who supported the artist during his English campaign for subscribers.

title: 71 Winter Hawk
description: A perched raptor identified as the Winter Hawk, now recognised as the immature plumage of the red-shouldered hawk. The plate records a separate species name once thought to apply, before juvenile and adult plumages were correctly correlated.

title: 72 Swallow-tailed Hawk
description: A swallow-tailed kite banks on long pointed wings, its forked tail spread to steer in flight. The aerial pose, set against open sky, exploits the format of the folio sheet to display a raptor that hunts insects on the wing over southern wetlands.

title: 73 Wood Thrush
description: A wood thrush stands on a branch of flowering dogwood, its bold round breast spots set against rich brown upperparts. The pairing of the bird with this characteristic spring-flowering tree of eastern deciduous forest follows the project's commitment to ecological context.

title: 74 Indigo Bird
description: An indigo bunting sings from a sprig of wild grape, the male's saturated blue plumage achieved through layered washes over the aquatint ground. The plate uses the common name in circulation before the species was reclassified among the cardinal-grosbeak family.

title: 75 Le Petit Caporal
description: A small falcon known under a French common name and now generally identified as an immature merlin or American kestrel variant. The plate preserves a vernacular term used by Louisiana hunters that did not enter standard ornithological nomenclature.

title: 76 Virginian Partridge
description: A covey of Virginian (Northern Bobwhite) Partridges scatters in panic as a hawk strikes from above, the dynamic composition exemplifying the dramatic narrative staging that distinguishes Birds of America from earlier ornithological plates. Hand-coloured aquatint after a watercolour drawn from life-size specimens.

title: 77 Belted Kingfisher
description: A pair of Belted Kingfishers perches on a riverside branch, the female distinguished by her chestnut breast band. The hand-coloured aquatint captures the slate-blue plumage and shaggy crest, with a subtle aquatic background hinting at the species' habitat along North American waterways.

title: 78 Great Carolina Wren
description: Two Carolina Wrens animate a tangled vine, rendered with the close botanical observation typical of the double-elephant folio. The hand-coloured aquatint emphasizes the warm cinnamon plumage and prominent white eye-stripe, presenting the birds at life-size on the unusually large 39-inch sheet.

title: 79 Tyrant Fly-catcher
description: An Eastern Kingbird is shown defending its territory, the species' aggressive behaviour toward larger raptors having earned it the name Tyrant Fly-catcher. Hand-coloured aquatint engraving by Robert Havell Jr. of London, working from the original watercolour study.

title: 8 White throated Sparrow
description: White-throated Sparrows perch among the broad green leaves and dangling pods of a dogwood, with the boldly striped head and bright white throat patch rendered through layered watercolour over engraving. Aquatint hand-coloured under Robert Havell Jr.'s London supervision.

title: 80 Prairie Titlark
description: Two American Pipits, then called Prairie Titlarks, are shown on the ground among low vegetation, reflecting their terrestrial foraging habits. Hand-coloured aquatint engraving from the double-elephant folio, with the streaked buff plumage carefully differentiated to aid field identification.

title: 81 Fish Hawk or Osprey
description: An Osprey clutches a freshly caught weakfish in its talons, mid-flight against a coastal sky. The plate emphasizes the raptor's specialized fishing anatomy, including the reversible outer toe, and is among the more dramatic action compositions in the folio.

title: 82 Whip-poor-will
description: Three Eastern Whip-poor-wills perch on an oak branch among red-flowered foliage, the cryptic plumage demonstrating the bird's reliance on camouflage during daylight roosting. Hand-coloured aquatint conveying the nocturnal nightjar's enormous gape and bristled rictus.

title: 83 House Wren
description: House Wrens animate a glass bottle and old hat repurposed as nesting cavities, a domestic vignette underscoring the species' adaptability to human structures. Hand-coloured aquatint engraved by Robert Havell Jr., with characteristically lively posing of the small brown birds.

title: 84 Blue-Grey Fly-catcher
description: A pair of Blue-grey Gnatcatchers tends a lichen-covered cup nest, the delicate construction rendered in fine detail to show the spiderweb binding. Hand-coloured aquatint demonstrating the natural history precision that supplemented Audubon's more theatrical bird studies.

title: 85 Yellow Throated Warbler
description: Yellow-throated Warblers explore pine boughs in a composition reflecting the species' preferred coniferous habitat in the southern United States. Hand-coloured aquatint with the bold yellow throat patch and bold facial striping rendered from Audubon's life-size watercolour study.

title: 86 Black Warrior
