description: A bustling Damascus thoroughfare rendered with the German Orientalist's exacting eye for tilework, lattice and crowd. Bauernfeind, trained as an architect, treated the city's facades almost archaeologically, balancing photographic detail with the strong overhead light of the Levant.

title: At Eternity's Gate
description: At Eternity's Gate - Vincent Van Gogh

title: Bank of the Oise at Auvers
Cesar Cui
description: A bust-length portrait of the composer and music critic César Cui, a member of The Mighty Handful. Repin captures the sitter in a relaxed, contemplative pose with the loose brushwork and warm psychological focus characteristic of his portraits of Russian cultural figures.

title: Flowering Meadow with Trees and Dandelions
description: Flowering meadow with trees and dandelions - Vincent Van Gogh

title: Gerome venus
description: Venus emerges from the sea trailing the morning star, her body lit with the pale, enamel finish Gérôme cultivated late in life. The composition revisits the Anadyomene tradition while flirting with the polychrome sculpture project that preoccupied his final decades.

title: Girl in a Japanese Costume
description: A young woman lies on cushions in a kimono, surrounded by fans and lacquered objects. The picture taps the Aesthetic-era taste for Japonisme, deploying its props in a frankly studio-arranged tableau of textures.
credit: zQFJS956MheMiw at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Glen Birnam
description: A view of the Perthshire glen near Dunkeld where Millais routinely summered and painted. The wet leaf-strewn ground and bare birches are handled with the freer, broader brushwork of his maturity, far from the meticulous botanical inventories of his Pre-Raphaelite youth.

title: Green Wheat Fields
description: Made in the final months at Auvers-sur-Oise, this study of a young wheat crop uses short, parallel strokes to register the wind moving over the field. The horizon is pushed high, leaving the green expanse to dominate as a meditation on growth and impermanence.
credit: 1. Copied from an art book 2. National Gallery of Art, Washington, D. C., online collection

title: Guanyin riding the dragon
description: The Buddhist bodhisattva of compassion rides a coiling dragon through cloudbanks, the figure modelled with the volumetric solidity of European academic painting. A Munich-trained pioneer of yōga (Western-style oil painting), Harada provoked debate by giving a Japanese religious subject the visual language of Counter-Reformation altarpieces.

provenance.collection: The National Museum of Modern Art, Tokyo
provenance.location: The National Museum of Modern Art, Tokyo

title: House at Auvers
description: A thatched cottage screened by trees in Auvers-sur-Oise, where Van Gogh spent his last seventy days under the care of Dr Paul Gachet. The brisk diagonal strokes and pale, cool palette belong to the prolific summer 1890 burst that produced more than seventy canvases.
credit: RwFvYmUb77JK5g at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Houses at Auvers / Houses in Auvers
description: A cluster of village rooftops in Auvers-sur-Oise, painted during the artist's final months in the small town north of Paris. Thatched and tiled roofs press together in interlocking diagonals, the brushwork rhythmic and ribbon-like across walls, foliage, and a streaked summer sky.
credit: tQGRZEZ7Pn6KQA at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Irises
description: Painted at Saint-Rémy in the final year of his life, the canvas crops in tightly on a bed of irises set against a pale background. Van Gogh organises the scene as a near-flat tapestry of curling green leaves and violet petals, the heavy impasto and decorative outlines reflecting his interest in Japanese prints.

title: Irises
description: A bouquet of cut irises set in a simple vase, painted at the asylum of Saint-Paul-de-Mausole in the spring before the artist's death. Violet petals are built from short, kinetic strokes against a yellow ground, the complementary contrast deliberately heightening the flowers' chromatic intensity.
credit: Metropolitan Museum of Art, online collection (accession number 58.187)

title: Irises
description: A still life of irises in a vase against a saturated yellow ground, executed at Saint-Rémy. Stems curve upward in calligraphic lines while the petals are massed in cool violets and blues, an overt complementary pairing that the artist used repeatedly during his late flower studies.
credit: ZQH2h7PBY47yXQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Landscape with a Carriage and a Train
description: A wide Auvers landscape with a horse-drawn carriage on a country road and a steam train cutting across the middle distance. The composition layers green fields, yellow wheat, and a band of poplars, registering the encroachment of modern travel on the rural plain.
credit: The Museum of Modern Western Art (NWM)

title: Montagne Saint-Victoire
description: Cézanne returned obsessively to the limestone ridge near his native Aix-en-Provence, structuring its slopes through patches of constructive brushwork. The painting treats landscape as an architecture of colour planes, foundational for early modernism.
credit: FgH8RRkGP4EUfg at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Motherhood
description: A bare-shouldered mother cradles her child against her, the two faces close in quiet intimacy. Cassatt returned to the maternité theme repeatedly after 1880, treating the secular subject with the formal gravity of a Renaissance Madonna while keeping her brushwork bright and contemporary.

title: Noon – Rest from Work
description: Two harvesters nap in the shade of a haystack at noon, freely copied from a Millet print Van Gogh kept in his Saint-Rémy studio. He translated Millet's grey original into a pairing of complementary blue shadows and golden yellow stubble.
credit: LwFmpmDARcicFQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Prisoners Exercising
description: Convicts circling a stone yard under guard, copied at Saint-Rémy after a print by Gustave Doré of Newgate Prison. The artist's choice of source from his asylum bed gives the trudging round of figures a transparent autobiographical charge.
credit: The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 .

title: Pygmalion and Galatea
description: The Cypriot sculptor embraces his ivory creation as she turns from cold stone to warm flesh from the hips upward. Gérôme also modelled the subject in polychrome sculpture in these years; the painting plays on his own dual practice as painter and sculptor.

title: Road with Cypress and Star
description: A country road at twilight with two figures, a horse-drawn cart, a flame-like cypress, and a crescent moon and evening star above. The composition compresses earth and sky into spiraling, rhythmic strokes; it was among the last canvases finished at Saint-Rémy before the move north.

title: Roses
description: A dense bouquet of pale pink roses tumbling from a green vase, painted at the asylum of Saint-Rémy weeks before the artist left for Auvers. Van Gogh's swirling impasto and rhythmic brushstrokes turn a quiet still life into a study of vitality and renewal.

title: Snow-Covered Field with a Harrow
description: A bleak field at Auvers with a discarded harrow under fresh snow, freely after a black-and-white print by Jean-François Millet. Van Gogh translated Millet's earthbound peasant subjects into his own thicker, colour-saturated impasto throughout his last year.

title: Spring in Giverny
description: Fruit trees in blossom rise above a meadow of high grass at the artist's Giverny estate, the pink froth picked out with rapid stippled touches. Spring orchards became one of Monet's most consistent local motifs after he settled in the village in 1883.

title: Sunlight on the Coast
description: Surf detonates against a Maine ledge under hard noon light, with no human figure to soften the shock. Homer's Prouts Neck marines reduced the genre to elemental confrontations of rock, water and atmosphere.

title: The Church at Auvers
description: The medieval parish church of Auvers seen from the apse, its stonework leaning under a deep cobalt sky split by branching lanes. The masonry and lead roofs are worked in thick, undulating strokes that twist the Gothic structure into a single restless form.

title: The Gate Of The Great Umayyad Mosque
description: Figures cluster at the threshold of one of Islam's oldest monuments, the painter dwelling on the carved stone, polychrome marble inlay and worn thresholds of the Bab al-Barid. The composition is typical of Bauernfeind's Damascene pictures, framing dense human activity within rigorously drawn architecture.

title: The Pyrrhic Dance
description: Greek warriors in plumed helmets perform the ancient pyrrhic war dance before a seated audience, swords and shields raised in unison. The carefully staged classical reconstruction is typical of Gérôme's late academic manner, in which archaeological detail is mobilised to bring antiquity to vivid life.

title: View of Vessenots Near Auvers
description: A view of the Vessenots hamlet at Auvers-sur-Oise, with thatched cottages set among fields of long grass and slender trees. Painted in the artist's final weeks, the surface is densely worked in horizontal greens and yellows, the buildings reduced to compact accents within the landscape.

title: Vineyards at Auvers / Vineyards with a View of Auvers
description: A walled vineyard outside Auvers descends toward red-roofed cottages and the church tower beyond. Painted weeks before Van Gogh's death, the canvas balances his late, agitated brushwork with an unusually orderly Northern French motif.
credit: Saint Louis Art Museum, online collection

title: Wheat Fields after the Rain
description: Furrowed wheat fields on the plain of Auvers, the soil glistening after a storm, painted in the final weeks of Van Gogh's life. The high horizon, ribbon-like brushwork, and rain-washed greens belong to the productive Auvers months that ended in July 1890.
credit: Photo I took of Vincent Van Goghs' Wheat Fields after the Rain (The Plain of Auvers) from 1890. Carnegie Museum of Art

title: Wheatfield under thunderclouds
description: A wide double-square wheat field under a lowering blue-grey sky, painted in the Auvers fields during the artist's final weeks. The horizon is set high, leaving a vast expanse of ripe grain whose horizontal strokes resist the weight of the gathering storm.
credit: kAErTfh0dORNwQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Wheatfield under Thunderclouds
description: A double-square canvas painted at Auvers-sur-Oise in the last weeks of the artist's life, the wheat below worked in horizontal strokes and the heavy clouds above in slate and lilac scumbles. It belongs to a small group of panoramic Auvers wheatfields that pushed his format toward the cinematic.

title: Wheatfield With Cornflowers
credit: BwFKPHmzvqHGJw at Google Cultural Institute , zoom level maximum

title: Wheatfield with crows
description: A double-square panorama of a wheat field crossed by three diverging paths, with a flock of black crows lifting against a turbulent blue sky. Painted in the Auvers fields shortly before the artist's death, the work compresses storm, harvest, and flight into a single agitated surface.
credit: dwFdD5AMQfpSew at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: A huntsman and dogs
description: An Adirondack hunter shoulders a deer hide while his hounds press against him on a windswept ridge. Homer's late realism strips the scene to silhouette and weather, registering the rougher economy of post-frontier wilderness.
credit: fgGa0e8ULzplRw at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Before Moscow waiting for boyars
description: Перед Москвой в ожидании депутации бояр

title: Duse by Repin
description: A charcoal portrait of the Italian tragedienne Eleonora Duse, capturing the famously expressive actress in a moment of inward concentration. Repin exploits the chalky drag of charcoal on canvas to model her face while leaving the gown in summary, atmospheric strokes.

title: Grainstack
description: A single haystack rises against a flushed sky from one of Monet's most influential serial campaigns. Working from his Giverny window over the winter of 1890–91, he painted twenty-five Grainstack canvases tracking the same motif through every shift of weather and hour.

title: Leo Tolstoy02
description: A studio portrait of Tolstoy seated in the vaulted room of his estate at Yasnaya Polyana. Repin, the leading realist of the Peredvizhniki, painted the writer repeatedly over decades, capturing here the ascetic peasant dress and inward gaze of Tolstoy's late religious phase.

title: Life Study
description: A standing nude rendered with academic precision during Sargent's travels in North Africa, the body modeled in warm earth tones against a neutral ground. The study reflects his sustained interest in the figure outside commissioned portraiture.

title: Mink Pond
description: A trout, frog and lily pads share a single watercolor surface in this Adirondack pond study. Homer dissolves traditional figure-ground hierarchy, letting reflections and refraction flatten the underwater and overwater worlds into one decorative plane.

title: Poplars on the Epte
description: From the Poplars series, painted from a flat-bottomed boat moored in the river Epte near Giverny. The slender trunks form a rhythmic vertical lattice, doubled by reflection, that anticipates the decorative serial logic of the later Rouen Cathedrals.
credit: tAF_YbFvVelS9Q at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: The Circus
description: Seurat's last, unfinished canvas captures a circus performance with a bareback rider, ringmaster and tiered audience. The arabesque rhythms and yellow-blue contrasts apply Charles Henry's theories of expressive line and colour, pushing pointillism toward decorative stylisation.
credit: The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 .

title: Tiger in a Tropical Storm
description: A tiger crouches through whipping grasses as rain slashes a tropical thicket lit by lightning. Rousseau's first jungle picture, it stitched together motifs from botanical gardens and prints into the imagined wilderness that would occupy him for the rest of his life.

title: Warden of the Mosque
description: An elderly warden stands sentinel in the courtyard of the Umayyad Mosque, his white robe set against patterned stone. Bauernfeind treats the single figure with quiet dignity, the surrounding architecture rendered with the meticulous draftsmanship of his architectural training.

title: Watching the Breakers
description: Figures stand at the edge of a rocky shore as heavy Atlantic surf detonates in spray against the ledges. The picture belongs to Homer's Prouts Neck years, when he reduced his palette to slate, foam, and granite and treated the meeting of land and sea as the subject in itself.

title: A Sunny Day at Shinnecock Bay
description: Sand, scrub, and pale sky meet under bright Long Island light at Shinnecock, where Chase ran his pioneering open-air summer school from 1891. The painting's broad horizontal bands and feathery brushwork mark his absorption of French Impressionism into an American plein-air idiom.

title: At the Seaside
credit: This file was donated to Wikimedia Commons as part of a project by the Metropolitan Museum of Art . See the Image and Data Resources Open Access Policy

title: Blow Blow Thou Winter Wind
description: Titled from a song in Shakespeare's As You Like It, the painting shows a young woman trudging through a bleak snowbound landscape. By the 1890s Millais had moved away from his early Pre-Raphaelite tightness toward a broader, more mood-driven late style.
credit: KAFjkUpsnAJxZQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

provenance.collection: Auckland Art Gallery Toi o Tāmaki
provenance.location: Auckland Art Gallery Toi o Tāmaki

title: Cassia-tree moon
description: Wu Gang, the legendary Chinese woodcutter condemned to chop forever at the cassia tree on the moon, swings his axe against silvered foliage. Yoshitoshi adapts the Daoist tale into the One Hundred Aspects of the Moon series, paring the design to the figure's strained pose against an empty sky.

title: Curiosity
description: Two young women lean in conspiratorially, one whispering while the other listens with an expression caught between amusement and disbelief. The intimate framing and high finish are typical of de Blaas's Venetian genre painting, in which gossip itself becomes the subject.

title: Nasturces
description: A close, plunging view of nasturtium leaves and blossoms painted in Caillebotte's Petit Gennevilliers garden. The unusual cropping and high vantage echo the steep perspectives he favoured in his urban scenes, applied here to a horticultural subject he cultivated himself.

title: Portrait of Lady Agnew of Lochnaw
description: Gertrude, Lady Agnew of Lochnaw lounges in a bergère, her white satin gown and lilac sash dashed in with bravura wet-into-wet brushwork. The portrait's frank gaze and easy elegance made Sargent's reputation when shown at the Royal Academy in 1893.

title: The Card Players
description: Two Provençal labourers face off across a wine bottle in Cézanne's most distilled treatment of the card-players theme. The Courtauld version reduces the cast to its essential pair, the figures built from the planar, faceted strokes that prefigured Cubism.

title: The moon on Musashi Plain
description: A pale full moon rises over the windswept grasses of Musashino, where a fox glances back through the autumn pampas. The plate from One Hundred Aspects of the Moon distills a literary motif into spare silhouette and graded sky.
credit: jAHH0A04avBLOg at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: The Red Fan
description: A young woman lifts a vivid red fan to half-shield her smile, the single warm accent ignites the otherwise restrained tonal harmony. De Blaas exploits such props to focus attention and showcase his command of silk, lace, and the answering blush of skin.

title: Fox Hunt
description: A red fox flounders through deep snow as a flock of crows descends, the predator suddenly become prey. Painted at Prouts Neck on the Maine coast, this is the largest canvas of Homer's late career, its compressed Japonist composition and bleak palette signalling the harshness of the northern winter.
credit: 2. The Phialdelphia Inquirer 1. The Athenaeum: Home - info - pic

provenance.collection: Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts
provenance.location: Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts

title: Ice Floes
credit: This file was donated to Wikimedia Commons as part of a project by the Metropolitan Museum of Art . See the Image and Data Resources Open Access Policy

title: The Antique Pottery Painter: Sculpturæ vitam insufflat pictura
description: An ancient Greek artisan paints a terracotta Tanagra figurine, the Latin title declaring that 'painting breathes life into sculpture'. The picture is Gérôme's manifesto for polychromy, arguing that the white marbles museums revered were originally as colored as this little figure.

title: The Bathing Posts
description: A small panel of bathing markers driven into the Breton sand under a soft, overcast sky. Whistler reduces the seaside to a few horizontal bands of muted colour, in keeping with the tonal restraint and Aestheticist economy of his late oils.
credit: Hunterian Museum and Art Gallery, Glasgow

title: The Child’s Bath
description: Seen from a steep, raised viewpoint, a woman bathes a small child in a striped basin, their bodies pressed together in tender absorption. Cassatt borrows the high vantage and patterned surfaces from Japanese woodblock prints, which she had studied closely in the Paris exhibition of 1890.

title: The Elopement
description: A pair of lovers stealing away by torchlight through a Tudor courtyard, the woman lifting her brocade skirts as her companion checks the way ahead. Blair Leighton wraps the runaway plot in crisp costume detail and stage-managed late-Victorian sentiment.

title: The scream of nature
description: A skeletal figure clutches its head beneath a blood-red sky on a fjord-side bridge, mouth agape in soundless anguish. Munch combined oil, tempera and pastel on cardboard to externalise an attack of existential dread, anchoring the visual vocabulary of European Expressionism.
credit: National Museum of Art, Architecture and Design

provenance.collection: National Museum of Art, Architecture and Design

title: The Sin
description: A nude woman emerges from shadow with an enormous serpent coiled across her shoulders, its head reared at her ear. The picture, replicated in several versions, distils von Stuck's Symbolist creed: a frontal nude, deep gilded frame, and femme-fatale eroticism rendered in glazed, almost lacquered pigment.
credit: The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 .

title: Beardsley-peacockskirt
description: Beardsley's ink illustration for Oscar Wilde's Salome shows the princess in a sweeping skirt patterned with peacock eyes, confronting Herodias. The economy of black silhouette and decorative line, drawn from Japanese prints, became a touchstone of Art Nouveau graphic design.

title: Day of the God
description: Mahana no atua, painted in Paris between Gauguin's two Tahitian sojourns, presents an idol of the moon goddess Hina flanked by ritual bathers. The lower foreground dissolves into pools of pure, mirror-bright color, an experiment in flat decorative abstraction that would influence Nabi and Fauve painters.
credit: 1. The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 . 2. Google Art Project works from the Art Institute of Chicago

title: Despair
description: This is a painting featuring a figure standing on a bridge, gazing down, seemingly lost in thought. The background consists of a dramatic, swirling sky with striking red, yellow, and blue colors. The figure is dressed in dark clothing, and their posture and the expression on their face convey a sense of melancholy or introspection.

title: Monet w1370
description: From the mid-1890s, when Monet was consolidating his serial method and refining his Giverny motifs. The handling is patient and tonal, with closely keyed colours building atmosphere through accumulation rather than contrast.

title: My Next-Door Neighbour
description: A young woman leaning over a garden wall to chat with the unseen occupant beyond, a typical genre-romance vignette of late-Victorian England. Blair Leighton sets up the gentle social comedy with crisp drawing and a sunlit, almost photographic clarity of detail.

title: Portrait of a Neapolitan woman
description: A bust-length portrait of a young Neapolitan woman, painted during Repin's travels in southern Italy. The Russian realist favours direct observation over picturesque costume drama, registering complexion and gaze with a swift, loaded brush.

title: The Adirondack Guide
description: A solitary woodsman paddles his canoe across a still mountain lake, the watercolor reserving the white of the paper for highlights on water and shirt. The picture distills Homer's late respect for the working backwoodsman into a quietly monumental profile.

title: The Garden at Petit Gennevilliers in Winter
description: A snow-blanketed view of the painter's own riverside garden across the Seine from Argenteuil, where Caillebotte spent his final years tending plants and painting boats. The muted whites and bare branches show the late, looser brushwork he developed after retreating from Paris's Impressionist circle.

title: Young Girl Combing Her Hair
description: An intimate domestic scene of a young woman combing her hair, a recurring subject in Renoir's later years. The soft, feathery handling of flesh and fabric reflects his shift away from strict Impressionist plein-air practice toward a warmer, more classicizing manner influenced by Renaissance art.

title: A Friendly Call
description: Chase records a sunlit afternoon visit in his Shinnecock summer studio, the parlour a parade of mirrors, framed prints, and white-painted wicker. A leading American Impressionist and influential teacher, he handles light and reflective surfaces with bravura brushwork indebted to Sargent and Whistler.

title: An Afternoon Stroll
description: Chase paints his wife and one of their children walking through the dunes at Shinnecock, Long Island, where he ran his summer school of plein-air painting. Loose, sun-bleached brushwork shows his absorption of French Impressionism into an American idiom.

title: Chinese Warship Ting Yuang Visiting Japan
description: The German-built ironclad Tingyuan, then the largest warship in the Far East, lies at anchor in a Japanese port flying her flags. Kōitsu records the ship's Meiji-era courtesy visit in a colour woodblock print that combines ukiyo-e tradition with the new Western subject matter of steam and steel.

title: Daydreaming by the shore
description: A young Venetian girl rests on the lagoon's edge, her gaze drifting beyond the picture plane while sunlight catches her loose hair and starched blouse. De Blaas pairs his trademark textile precision with a softer, more reflective mood than his lively flirtation scenes.

title: Frederick Law Olmsted
description: Sargent painted the landscape architect of Central Park during work on the Biltmore Estate grounds, posing him among the rhododendrons he had laid out. Loose, plein-air handling of the foliage gives way to a tighter likeness of the sitter, knitting Realist portraiture to Impressionist surface.

title: Leda and the Swan
description: Gérôme treats the Ovidian myth in his characteristic polished academic manner, isolating the pale figure of Leda against a dim ground for sculptural emphasis. Trained as both painter and sculptor, he often returned to mythological nudes that doubled as exercises in modelled, near-tactile flesh.

title: Northeaster
description: A lone outcrop of the Maine coast pummeled by a winter storm, rendered with the muscular realism Homer developed at Prout's Neck. The painting strips the marine subject to surf, rock, and spray, a meditation on raw natural force.
credit: Metropolitan Museum of Art, online collection (The Met object ID 20011487)

title: Study of a Girl in Japanese Dress
description: Chase, the leading American Impressionist teacher, captures a young model in a borrowed kimono with rapid, fluid brushwork. Such japoniste studio subjects were fashionable in Gilded Age New York and reveal his admiration for Whistler and Sargent.
credit: Online Collection of Brooklyn Museum ; Photo: Brooklyn Museum, 51.60_SL1.jpg

title: Summer Afternoon on a Lake
description: A rare unpopulated landscape from Gérôme: still water, a wooded shore and a single skiff under hazy summer light. Stripped of his usual ethnographic figures, the picture shows the careful tonal control underlying his more theatrical work.

title: The Artist and His Model
description: A late self-reflexive subject: Gérôme depicts himself in his studio carving a tinted marble figure, addressing his lifelong campaign for polychrome sculpture. Smooth academic finish gives way to studio clutter of mallets, plaster casts and pigment pots, declaring painting and sculpture as kindred crafts.

title: Vampire
description: A red-haired woman bends over a man, her lips at his neck while her hair cascades like flowing blood. Originally titled Love and Pain, the picture was rechristened by Munch's Symbolist circle and became a key motif of his Frieze of Life.

title: Wedding of Nicholas II and Grand Princess Alexandra Fyodorovna
description: An imperial ceremonial scene staged inside a Russian Orthodox chapel, with Tsar Nicholas II and Alexandra Fyodorovna at the centre of a glittering crowd of courtiers, clergy and grand dukes. Tuxen, official painter to several European royal houses, marshals the assembly into a measured, document-like composition.

provenance.location: Grand Church of the Winter Palace

title: A Pensive Moment
description: A solitary young woman pauses at a stone window, chin propped in hand, eyes turned inward. De Blaas reduces his usual courtyard chatter to a single note of quiet introspection, the polished academic finish lending the everyday subject a hint of portrait gravity.

title: Annecy Lake
description: Painted during a summer stay at Talloires, the canvas reduces the Savoyard lake and the Château de Duingt to interlocking planes of blue, green and ochre. Cézanne's structural brushwork and tilted perspective treat water and stone as equivalent geometric facets, prefiguring Cubist analysis of form.
credit: The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 .

title: Autumn
description: From the four-panel decorative series The Seasons, this allegorical figure stands among ripening fruit and ochre foliage. Mucha frames her in the slender vertical format and circular halo motif that defined his Parisian poster style.

title: Night Bivouac of Great Army
description: Soldiers of Napoleon's Grande Armée huddle around guttering fires in a frozen Russian field during the catastrophic 1812 retreat. Vereshchagin, a battle painter known for unsparing realism, treats the scene with documentary cold rather than heroic rhetoric.

title: Nikolay II of Russia by I.Repin
description: A formal portrait of Tsar Nicholas II, the last emperor of Russia, painted shortly after his coronation. Repin balances state-portrait dignity with the loose, vigorous brushwork that distinguished him among the Peredvizhniki realists.

title: Spring
description: Part of Mucha's first Seasons cycle, the figure plays a lyre fashioned from a branch, surrounded by fresh blossoms and songbirds. The pale palette and arabesque hair are signatures of his early Champenois lithographs.

title: Summer
description: From the Seasons quartet issued by Champenois, a languid woman trails her feet in water beneath ripe poppies. Mucha sets her in the elongated whiplash silhouette that became shorthand for fin-de-siècle Paris.

title: The Chess Game
description: Two men hunched over a chessboard in a tiled Cairene interior, a favourite subject of the Austrian Orientalist Ludwig Deutsch. The picture displays his trademark microscopic finish, with carpets, brass trays and mashrabiya screens recorded in jewel-like detail under cool, even daylight.

title: Truth Coming Out of Her Well
description: An allegory of Truth, nude and enraged, climbing from the well into which liars have cast her — painted at the height of the Dreyfus Affair and widely read as Gérôme's bitter comment on public deceit. The polished academic surface gives the mythic figure unsettling photographic immediacy.

title: Winter
description: Closing the Seasons set, a fur-cloaked woman warms a small bird against a snow-laden branch. The cool blue-grey palette and stylized snowfall demonstrate Mucha's command of decorative colour lithography.

title: Entry of the Christ in Jerusalem
description: Christ on the donkey descends a road white with Judean dust, the procession trailing behind him under a sky bleached by glare. Gérôme treats the Gospel scene as travel reportage from his Holy Land journeys, scattering the crowd into a long, frieze-like recession.

title: Henri Meunier04
description: A poster, L'Heure du Silence, by the Belgian Art Nouveau designer Henri Meunier, lithographed in the flat planes and sinuous contour the Brussels school adopted from Japanese prints. Meunier worked extensively in advertising, treating commercial commissions with the same graphic refinement as his fine-art prints.

title: In Time of Peril
credit: 9gF4Die6xd2HeQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

provenance.collection: Auckland Art Gallery Toi o Tāmaki
provenance.location: Auckland Art Gallery Toi o Tāmaki

title: Lakeside
description: Lakeside, painted at Hakone, shows the artist's wife seated by the water in a yukata, a paper fan in her lap. Kuroda, who trained in Paris, brought plein-air Impressionist colour and the high-key palette of yōga oil painting back to Meiji Japan.

title: Monet w1897
description: From the long water-lily campaign begun once Monet diverted a branch of the Epte to feed his pond. The composition omits sky and bank entirely, training the eye on the drift of pads and the cool glaze of reflection.

title: Morning on the Seine
description: Morning on the Seine, near Giverny

title: Mucha seasons 1897 spring
description: Spring from Mucha's second Four Seasons set, personified as a young woman tuning a lyre strung between blossoming branches. The decorative panel format, sinuous contour and floral framing typify the Art Nouveau idiom Mucha helped to define in Paris.

title: Napoleon near Borodino
description: Part of an extensive cycle the painter devoted to the 1812 French invasion of Russia, the canvas shows the emperor on the battlefield reduced to an ordinary, weary commander. The series was researched on site and built around demystifying the Napoleonic legend.

title: Sappho
description: The Swiss painter imagines the Greek poet on a Lesbos cliff, lyre in hand, gazing seaward in the conventional pose of impending suicide. Stückelberg works in the late academic idiom, smoothing antique legend into a moralized salon image.
credit: "Von Anker bis Zünd, Die Kunst im jungen Bundesstaat 1848 – 1900", Kunsthaus Zürich, 1998, upload by Adrian Michael

title: The Hermitage
description: Cover lithograph for the Symbolist literary review L'Ermitage, depicting a pensive woman wreathed in stylised foliage. Berthon, a pupil of Eugène Grasset, worked in soft chalky tones rather than Mucha's saturated colour, giving his Art Nouveau posters a distinctive fresco-like flatness.

title: The Sleeping Gypsy
description: A lion sniffs at a sleeping Black musician beside a moonlit river, lute and water jar at her side. Rousseau's flattened forms, dreamlike disjunctions and meticulous storybook clarity show the self-taught douanier inventing a modern visionary mode admired by the Parisian avant-garde.

title: Aphonse Mucha - Rose 1898
description: A young woman wreathed in roses stands within a decorative arch, her flowing hair and gown merging with the surrounding blooms. Part of Mucha's Flowers series of four panel decorations, the design exemplifies the Art Nouveau idiom he refined in Paris in the late 1890s.

title: Dance
description: Part of The Arts panel series, an entranced female personification floats through swirling drapery and autumnal leaves. Mucha treats movement itself as ornament, threading line through the figure in continuous arabesques.

title: First Touch of Autumn
description: A wooded path glows with the first yellow leaves of fall, painted in long, vibrant strokes. Chase's plein-air technique here owes as much to French Impressionism as to the Long Island summer school he led at Shinnecock.
credit: twFSmkC99OhD0g at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Painting
description: Part of Mucha's four-panel allegorical suite The Arts, this lithograph personifies Painting as a young woman in profile contemplating a flower at twilight. The slender format, ornamented halo and arabesque border are classic vocabulary of Mucha's Paris-period Art Nouveau.

title: Series The Flowers
description: From the four-panel Flowers series, a virginal figure rises among towering white lilies in a symmetrical decorative niche. Mucha equates the woman with the bloom, fusing botanical study and Art Nouveau allegory.

title: The Hookah Lighter
description: An Orientalist genre scene of a turbaned attendant kindling the coals of a water pipe in a tiled interior. Gérôme's polished academic finish renders brass, silk and ceramic with photographic precision.
credit: Scanned from Smoke: a global history of smoking (2004) ISBN 1-86189-200-4

title: The Old Adam and Eve
description: A pen-and-ink illustration prepared for an edition of Carlyle's Sartor Resartus, picturing the first couple as worn, clothed elders rather than as youthful nudes. Sullivan's nervous cross-hatched line carries the late-Victorian taste for Symbolist book ornament.

title: Two Venetian Women
description: Two figures stand framed by a worn brick wall, one balancing a copper water vessel, the other looking out beneath a lace-edged kerchief. The painting belongs to de Blaas's prolific Venetian street type, where ethnographic observation meets the polish of academic Salon painting.

title: After the Hurricane
description: A black sailor lies cast up beside his shattered sloop on a Bahamian beach, the sea behind still heavy from the storm. Homer's late watercolor technique uses transparent washes and reserved white paper to convey both tropical glare and human vulnerability.
credit: TAG7VpnWDiMTDQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Charing Cross Bridge
description: Monet painted the railway bridge across the Thames repeatedly during winter stays at the Savoy Hotel, working on dozens of canvases at once as the light shifted. The composition dissolves bridge, smoke, and water into veils of mauve and pale orange characteristic of his London series.

title: Charing Cross Bridge
description: One of nearly forty variants Monet made of Charing Cross Bridge, focused here on the trembling reflections beneath the iron span. The series, painted between 1899 and 1904, was the artist's sustained study of London fog as a coloured atmospheric medium rather than a mere obscurant.

title: Jean-Léon Gérôme
description: First panel of a late narrative pair drawn from an ode attributed to Anacreon, in which a drenched Cupid knocks at the poet's door on a stormy night. Gérôme's tightly drawn classical interior sets up the comic mishap that follows in the second canvas.

title: Jean-Léon Gérôme
description: The companion to the rainstorm scene: Anacreon has taken the shivering Cupid in and warms him by the hearth, just before the boy will draw his bow and pierce the host's heart. Gérôme stages the moral fable with the archaeological props and porcelain finish of his late manner.

title: Jean-Léon Gérôme
description: Part of a late illustrative cycle Gérôme devoted to the Greek lyric poet Anacreon, the scene shows the mischievous Cupid escaping after warming himself at the poet's hearth. Drawn in Gérôme's tight academic line, it preserves the anecdotal charm of the original Anacreontic ode.

title: Jean-Léon Gérôme
description: A late narrative drawing in Gérôme's Anacreon series, picturing the aged poet dozing by the fire as Cupid hovers nearby. The composition leans on Gérôme's signature precision of contour and his appetite for ancient literary conceits rendered in tableau form.

title: The Gulf Stream
description: A Black sailor lies on the deck of a dismasted sloop adrift on rough seas, encircled by sharks while a waterspout darkens the horizon. Homer's late marine paintings turn man-against-nature subjects into terse moral allegories, the figure's resigned posture sharpened by the painter's economical handling of pigment and salt-bleached light.
credit: 1. The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 . 2. art database

title: the-water-lily-pond
description: A view across the Japanese footbridge spanning the lily pond at Monet's Giverny garden, the green arch dissolving into shimmering reflections. The painting belongs to the celebrated bridge series in which Monet examined a single subject under shifting conditions of light.

title: the-water-lily-pond
description: The arched footbridge of Monet's Giverny garden hangs above a pond crowded with floating blossoms, framed by curtains of willow and iris. Painted in tightly woven greens and rose-tinted whites, the canvas turns a private water garden into an enclosed world of reflection.

title: Waterloo Bridge. Effect of Fog
description: The bridge's arches dissolve into Thames fog while the smoke-stained palette of the south bank glows behind. Monet worked on the Waterloo and Charing Cross views from a balcony at the Savoy Hotel, juggling many canvases as the light shifted.

title: Wrestlers
description: Two locked grapplers studied with the anatomical exactness Eakins demanded of his Philadelphia students. The earthy palette, low viewpoint and frieze-like composition reflect his admiration for ancient athletic sculpture filtered through photographic study of the male body.

provenance.collection: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
provenance.location: Los Angeles County Museum of Art

title: At the Dome of the Rock and the Fountain of Qayt Bay
description: Pilgrims and worshippers gather between the Mamluk-era Sabil of Qaytbay and the gleaming Dome of the Rock on the Haram al-Sharif. Bauernfeind, who eventually settled in Jerusalem, painted the site repeatedly, prizing the geometry of its mosaic and stone over picturesque incident.

title: Blue Irises
description: A cluster of blue irises rises against a plain ground in a tall, narrow format suited to the Japanese hashira-e pillar print. Designed by the shin-hanga artist Ohara Koson, the colour woodcut combines the close natural observation of kachō-e bird-and-flower prints with early twentieth-century printmaking technique.

title: Monet w1900
description: A turn-of-the-century pond view, made when Monet first showed the water lilies as a unified series at Durand-Ruel. The Japanese footbridge or its absence anchors a study of green-on-green reflection.

title: Nitta Yoshisada
description: A historical woodblock print showing the medieval samurai Nitta Yoshisada at the Battle of Ikuta Forest, where his vassal Oyamada Takaie sacrificed himself in his lord's place. The composition follows the dramatic narrative conventions of late Meiji musha-e warrior prints.

title: The Artist’s Garden in Giverny
description: Rows of irises beneath espaliered fruit trees in the artist's own garden at Giverny, which he designed and tended as obsessively as he painted it. The diagonal furrows of mauve and violet flowers organise the canvas as a near-abstract chromatic grid.

title: Train Smoke
description: A coastal landscape pierced by the white plume of a passing locomotive, its trail snaking across a russet shoreline. Munch cropped the scene around the rhythm of the smoke itself, turning industrial intrusion into a curling, almost serpentine compositional device.

title: Houses of Parliament
description: From the London series begun in 1899 from a balcony at the Savoy, the Palace of Westminster looms as a violet silhouette through Thames fog. Monet pursued the same view across hours and weathers, finishing many canvases later in the Giverny studio.
credit: Unknown Unknown (image is in the public domain; see below)

title: Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives at Sunrise
description: The walled Old City catches first light from the Mount of Olives, the Dome of the Rock luminous above the Kidron Valley. One of Bauernfeind's last major canvases, painted after he made Jerusalem his home, it trades street bustle for an almost devotional topographic stillness.

title: Notre-Dame
description: The cathedral seen from Matisse's quai Saint-Michel apartment late on a winter afternoon, its towers reduced to slate-blue silhouettes over the Seine. Painted on paper during a precarious year before Fauvism, the picture already pushes structural colour ahead of topographical accuracy.

title: The Moon
description: From the pair The Moon and the Stars, a pale-skinned figure leans against a glowing lunar disc among night clouds. The cool silvers and indigos show Mucha working in a more nocturnal, dream-laden register than his daylight posters.

title: Waterloo-Bridge
description: From Monet's London series of 1899-1904, painted from his rooms at the Savoy Hotel overlooking the Thames. Waterloo Bridge dissolves into the famous "effet de brouillard", its iron arches reduced to violet silhouettes against a sulphurous sky thickened by coal smoke.

title: William M. Chase
description: The painter William Merritt Chase is shown three-quarter length in studio dress, brush poised. Sargent's fluid, summarising touch reduces background and costume to broad sweeps, throwing the sitter's alert, assessing face into relief.
credit: Metropolitan Museum of Art, online collection (The Met object ID 12471)

title: Ceremonial Sitting of the State Council on 7 May 1901 Marking the Centenary of its Foundation
description: An immense official commission recording a centenary session of the Russian State Council in the Mariinsky Palace. Repin marshals more than eighty individual portraits into a coherent ceremonial space, his late brushwork looser and more atmospheric than the tightly modelled realism of his earlier work.
credit: uAFzvVW61WKsPg at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Houses of Parliament
description: The Palace of Westminster looms through Thames fog at a moment of breaking sun. Monet painted some nineteen Parliament views from a balcony at St Thomas's Hospital between 1900 and 1904, returning to his hotel each evening to refine the series from memory and notes.
credit: ogHX89fG0cGJxQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Souvenir of Achères
description: A late landscape recalling the village of Achères near the forest of Saint-Germain, painted in the final year of Gérôme's life. The academic precision of his Orientalist canvases is here loosened into a quieter, more atmospheric record of the Île-de-France countryside.

title: The Brooch. Eva Mudocci
description: The English violinist Eva Mudocci, with whom Munch had a brief affair, gazes from a cloud of dark hair in this lithograph. Drawn directly onto stone in Berlin and printed in soft black, it is among Munch's most tender portraits, sometimes called his Mona Lisa.

title: The Houes of Parliament
description: Westminster's Gothic silhouette dissolves into the Thames mist while gulls wheel through a violet, sulphur-tinted sky. Begun on the terrace of St Thomas's Hospital during Monet's 1899-1901 London campaigns and completed in Giverny, the canvas belongs to a series tracking the same view across hours and weather.

title: The Smoker
description: A robed man draws contemplatively on a long-stemmed pipe in a sunlit Cairo courtyard, costume and tilework rendered with watchmaker's precision. By the early twentieth century Deutsch's Orientalist tableaux had become almost hyperreal, vivid set pieces of an imagined East.

title: Assortment of Hepaticae from Kunstformen der Natur
description: Plate 82 arranges liverworts (Hepaticae) into a symmetrical mosaic showcasing the bilateral and radial geometries of Marchantia, Asterella and related thalloid genera. Lithograph from Kunstformen der Natur, where Haeckel's bilateral plate design was meant to advertise nature as an artist following the same compositional rules as architects.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 82: Hepaticae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Acanthometra
description: Lithographic plate gathering Acantharian protozoa whose star-shaped strontium-sulfate skeletons radiate twenty spicules from a central point. Haeckel arranged the microscopic single-celled marine organisms in a cathedral-like symmetry, advancing his argument that morphological law unites the smallest plankton with the greatest works of design.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 21: Acanthometra (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Acanthophracta
description: Plate of Acanthophractan radiolarians whose silica lattices form polyhedral and bell-shaped tests under the microscope. Drawn from specimens collected on the HMS Challenger expedition, the lithograph distils Haeckel's belief that the geometry of marine plankton offered a kind of natural Jugendstil ornament.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 41: Acanthophracta (see here and here )

title: Haeckel Acephala
description: Plate of headless bivalve molluscs arrayed for symmetry: Venus clams, cockles, and related shells shown in opening, valve, and hinge views. Haeckel exploits the radiating ribs and growth rings to draw the eye outward from each pair, treating malacology as ornamental design.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 55: Bivalvia (see here , here , here and here and Biodiversity Heritage Library )

title: Haeckel Actiniae
description: Sea anemones rendered with their tentacle crowns spread in jewel-toned discs, including Cereus pedunculatus and Aiptasia mutabilis. The plate compresses several species into a single bouquet, using the column striations and radial muscle bands as decorative geometry.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate/planche 49: Actiniae (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Ammonitida
description: Fossil ammonite shells from the Mesozoic, shown in lateral and ventral views to display sutured chambers and ribbed coiling. The composition makes the logarithmic spiral and ornamentation of Cardioceras and Schloenbachia legible at a glance, treating extinct cephalopods as design specimens.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 44: Ammonitida (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Amphoridea
description: Paleozoic carpoid and cystoid echinoderms with their armored plates flattened across the page. Haeckel arranges the polygonal calyx tessellations of Enoploura and Pleurocystites to highlight the irregular, almost crystalline plating of these early stalked relatives of starfish.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 95: Amphoridea (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Anthomedusae
description: Hydromedusae of the Anthomedusae order, shown from above, below and in lateral section to expose the gonads and gastrovascular canals. Trailing tentacles fall in glassy curtains around bell-shaped umbrellas, a study in translucent radial symmetry.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 46: Anthomedusae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Antilopina
description: Comparative heads of antelopes and related bovids: four-horned antelope, gnu, sitatunga, pronghorn, and waterbuck. The plate functions as a horn-shape catalogue, each profile placed to compare spiral, lyrate, and forked configurations.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 100: Antilopina (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Arachnida
description: Mites and amblypygids drawn at high magnification, their dorsal armor and segmented legs picked out against neutral grounds. Haeckel emphasizes the sculpted cuticle of cepheid mites and the flattened, crab-like body of the whip spider Phrynichus.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 66: Arachnida (see here , here and here ) Modifications for Wikipedia Black borders removed with stamp tool Background brightened with mask

title: Haeckel Ascidiae
description: Sea squirts presented as solitary forms, in section, and as colonial aggregates. Boltenia echinata is shown bristling with spicules above a longitudinal cut that reveals the U-shaped gut, while compound ascidians cluster like ripening fruit on the lower register.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 85: Ascidiacea (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Ascomycetes
description: Plate from Kunstformen der Natur showing a symmetrical arrangement of sac fungi, with cup-shaped fruiting bodies and powdery mildews magnified and framed like ornament. Haeckel's lithographs translated microscopic biology into decorative compositions that fed directly into Art Nouveau design.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 73: Ascomycetes (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Aspidonia
description: Horseshoe crabs and their extinct sea-scorpion kin, including Tachypleus gigas in adult and larval form alongside the Silurian eurypterid Eurypterus tetragonophthalmus. The plate stages a deep-time comparison between living and fossil chelicerates.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 47: Aspidonia (see here , here , here and here and Biodiversity Heritage Library )

title: Haeckel Asteridea
description: Developmental sequence of the common starfish Asterias rubens, from bipinnaria and brachiolaria larvae to a juvenile adult. Haeckel charts the metamorphosis from bilateral plankton to pentaradial echinoderm in a single ascending series.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 40: Asteridea (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Basimycetes
description: Stinkhorn fungi arranged like floral specimens: the latticed Clathrus ruber, the veiled Phallus indusiatus, and the starfish-armed Aseroe rubra. Cross-sectioned volvas at the base reveal the gelatinous egg stage from which each fruiting body emerges.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 63: Basimycetes (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Batrachia
description: Tropical tree frogs and marsupial frogs grouped for skin-pattern variety, including Hyla meridionalis, Rhacophorus pardalis, and the egg-brooding Gastrotheca. Splayed toe pads and warted backs are emphasized to register adhesive and camouflage adaptations.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 68: Batrachia (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Blastoidea
description: Extinct Paleozoic blastoids with budlike calyces drawn from above and the side, including Pentremites pyriformis. The five-petaled ambulacral fields are featured prominently, and one specimen appears with its delicate brachioles extended, folded, and removed.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 80: Blastoidea (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Bryozoa
description: Freshwater and marine moss animals centered on Cristatella mucedo, traced through statoblast, juvenile, and mature colony stages. Haeckel draws the gelatinous mat with its lophophore crowns extended in a feathered ring around each zooid.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 23: Bryozoa (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Bryozoa 33
description: Calcified bryozoan skeletons enlarged so that individual zooecia read as tiled honeycomb. Phaeostachys, Cribrilina, and Umbonula appear as porous lacework, with detail shifting between whole colonies and single chambered cells.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 33: Bryozoa (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Calcispongiae
description: Calcareous sponges of the Leucosolenia and Guancha groups, shown as branching tubular colonies anchored by spiculate roots. Haeckel sets habitus drawings beside isolated triradiate spicules to expose the skeletal architecture beneath the soft tissue.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 5: Calcispongiae (see here , here )

title: Haeckel Campanariae
description: Hydroid colonies of the family Campanulariidae, slender stems bearing bell-shaped hydranths and reproductive gonophores. The plate threads the colonies like vines across the page, foregrounding the hydrothecae as cup-on-stalk ornaments.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 45: Campanariae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Chaetopoda
description: Marine bristle worms with their feeding crowns expanded: feather duster Sabellastarte indica, the calcareous-tubed Hydroides, and the spiraled fan of Sabella spallanzanii. Tube architecture and feathered radioles are treated as the principal motif.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 96: Chaetopoda (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Chelonia
description: Comparative tableau of turtles and tortoises, ranging from the leatherback Dermochelys coriacea and hawksbill to the matamata-like Hydromedusa, geometric tortoise, and Aldabra giant. Carapace pattern is the organizing logic, set against contrasting head and limb forms.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 89: Chelonia (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Chiroptera
description: Bat heads in frontal view, from the long-eared Plecotus auritus to the leaf-nosed Megaderma and Chrotopterus. The plate lines up specialized noseleaves and ear funnels to show the morphological diversity behind echolocating sensory equipment.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 67: Chiroptera (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Ciliata
description: Tintinnid ciliates magnified so that their vase-shaped loricae read as miniature urns and bells. Codonella, Dictyocysta, and Tintinnopsis shells are arranged with their cellular occupants extending crowns of cilia, microscopic protozoa rendered as architecture.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 3: Ciliata (see here , here )

title: Haeckel Cirripedia
description: Goose barnacles in habit and dissection: Lepas anatifera intact, then with one valve cut away to reveal the feathered cirri and curled body. Conchoderma colonies are shown encrusting the whale-barnacle Coronula, a study in commensal stacking.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 57: Cirripedia (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Coniferae
description: Conifer cones and foliage from monkey puzzle, Norway spruce, Santa Lucia fir, hinoki cypress, and Japanese thujopsis. Haeckel arranges scale-by-scale dissections beside intact cones to compare the geometry of seed-bearing structures across the group.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 94: Coniferae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Copepoda
description: Planktonic copepods drawn with antennae and setae fanned out to fill the page, including the iridescent Calocalanus pavo with its peacock-tail furca. The plate celebrates ornamental setal arrays that drag through seawater for propulsion and sensing.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 56: Copepoda (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Crinoidea
description: Stalked sea lilies of the genera Endoxocrinus and Saracrinus, drawn full-length with feathery arms unfurled above jointed columns. Detail studies of stem ossicles emphasize the segmented, almost machined construction of these living fossil echinoderms.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 20: Crinoidea (see here and here )

title: Haeckel Ctenophorae
description: Comb jellies suspended in implied current, their eight ciliated bands shown as iridescent ribbons running down translucent bodies. Haeckelia rubra appears from above and side, joined by Hormiphora and the winged Callianira bialata.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 27: Ctenophorae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Cubomedusae
description: Box jellyfish in lateral view, the cuboidal bell of Chirodropus and Carybdea hung over trailing tentacle bundles. The plate isolates the rigid cubic geometry that distinguishes cubozoans from softer bell-shaped medusae.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 78: Cubomedusae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Cyrtoidea
description: Radiolarian shells of the Cyrtoidea group, eleven mineralized lattices arranged in three rows. Helmet, bell, and tower forms display the silica skeletons that fascinated Haeckel from his Challenger expedition reports onward.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 31: Cyrtoidea (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Cystoidea
description: Paleozoic cystoid echinoderms shown from above to display the polygonal calyx plating and pore-rhomb respiratory structures. Staurocystis, Glyptosphaerites, and Cystoblastus are arranged as a comparative key to early stalked echinoderm armor.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 90: Cystoidea (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Decapoda
description: Decapod crustaceans in dorsal view, from the spiky Daldorfia horrida and long-eyed Podophthalmus vigil to the banded coral shrimp Stenopus hispidus. The plate revels in carapace ornament, spines, and elongated chelae across crab and shrimp morphologies.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 86: Decapoda (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Desmidiea
description: A plate of desmid green algae, single-celled freshwater organisms whose cell walls split into mirror-image semicells. Haeckel arranges Staurastrum and related genera to highlight their geometric symmetry, including views of mating cells in which two halves conjugate to form a zygospore.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 24: Desmidiea (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Diatomea
description: An assembly of marine and freshwater diatoms, microscopic algae enclosed in silica frustules. Top and side views display the intricate punctate ornament of genera such as Auliscus, Cymbella and Campyloneis, drawn at a scale that turns optical microscopy into ornamental design.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 84: Diatomeae (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Diatomea 4
description: Triangular and lyre-shaped diatom valves arranged in radial symmetry, including Triceratium and Lyrella species seen in valve view. The plate emphasises the lattice of areolae perforating the silica shell, a feature only resolvable under high magnification.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 4: Diatomeae (see here and here )

title: Haeckel Discoidea
description: Radiolarians of the order Discoidea, single-celled marine plankton with star- and disc-shaped silica skeletons. Most figures derive from Haeckel's own 1887 Challenger Expedition monograph, where he described thousands of species from deep-sea sediment samples.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 11: Discoidea (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Discomedusae 18
description: Studies of small disc jellyfish, with Linantha lunulata shown from above and in side profile alongside developmental stages of Palephyra. The plate documents both the radial gastrovascular canals visible from above and the bell musculature seen during swimming.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 18: Discomedusae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Discomedusae 28
description: Multiple views of rhizostome jellyfish in the Toreuma group, dissecting a single animal into side, oral and aboral perspectives. The arrangement shows mouth-arms, gonad ribbons and the gelatinous bell as a coordinated anatomical study.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 28: Discomedusae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Discomedusae 8
description: Desmonema annasethe, a jellyfish Haeckel named after his late wife Anna Sethe, who died the year before he encountered the species. The trailing oral arms and tentacles, drawn in cascading filaments, are often read as a personal memorial within the scientific atlas.

title: Haeckel Discomedusae 88
description: Large rhizostome medusae including Pilema and Rhopilema shown from side, aboral and oral aspects. The plate captures the eight branched mouth-arms that replace a single central mouth in this group, filtering plankton through countless secondary openings.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 88: Discomedusae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Discomedusae 98
description: Aurelia and related semaeostome jellyfish presented alongside the polyp stage budding off juvenile medusae by strobilation. Pairing adult and larval forms on one sheet illustrates the alternation of generations central to scyphozoan life cycles.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 98: Trachomedusae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Echinidea
description: Sand dollars and sea biscuits including Clypeaster rosaceus and Encope emarginata, drawn with and without spines. Aboral views expose the five-petalled ambulacral pattern of the test, a hallmark of the irregular echinoids adapted to burrowing in sediment.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 30: Echinidea (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Echinidea 60
description: Cidaroid sea urchins shown stripped of their long primary spines so the tubercles and plate sutures of the test are legible. Mouth and periproct regions are isolated to compare the apical and oral systems across Cidaris and Prionocidaris species.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 60: Echinidea (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Filicinae
description: Staghorn ferns of the genus Platycerium, traced through successive frond stages from young plant to mature epiphyte. The shield-like sterile basal fronds and forked fertile blades are drawn to emphasise the dimorphism characteristic of the genus.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 52: Filicinae (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Filicinae 92
description: A composition of tropical ferns including tree ferns of Cyatheaceae, the bird's-nest fern Asplenium nidus and the eusporangiate Angiopteris. Habit drawings juxtapose growth forms ranging from arborescent trunks to ribbon-leaved Monogramma.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 92: Filicinae at the Tjiburrum waterfalls near Tjibodas , Mount Gede , Java (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Flagellata
description: Choanoflagellates and related flagellate protists arranged as colonial branched assemblages, including Anthophysa, Cephalothamnium and Codonocladium. The collared cells on stalked rosettes anticipate the body plan of sponge choanocytes.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 13: Flagellata (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Florideae
description: Red algae of the Florideophyceae, with Chondrus crispus, the Irish moss of north Atlantic shores, given prominence. The plate displays the cartilaginous, repeatedly dichotomous thalli that produce carrageenan and were already commercially harvested in the nineteenth century.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 65: Florideae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Fucoideae
description: Brown seaweeds drawn at habit scale, including the giant Pacific kelp Nereocystis luetkeana, Cutleria and Cystoseira. The selection traces the morphological range from pneumatocyst-bearing kelps to bushy littoral species of the order Fucales.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 15: Fucoideae (see here and here )

title: Haeckel Gamochonia
description: Coleoid cephalopods including Chiroteuthis, Histioteuthis and the common octopus, drawn from below and above. The plate sets pelagic deep-sea squids with hooked arms beside benthic octopuses to compare arm crowns, fins and chromatophore patterning.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 54: Gamochonia (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Gorgonida
description: Gorgonian octocorals with skeletal spicules detailed beside whole polyps and branch fragments. Eunicella and Primnoella illustrate the eight-tentacled polyps and calcareous sclerites that stiffen the proteinaceous gorgonin axis of the colony.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 39: Gorgonida (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Hexacoralla
description: Solitary and colonial stony corals of the Hexacorallia, including the cold-water reef-builder Lophelia prolifera. Skeletal views from above and the side reveal the six-fold symmetry of septa within each calcified corallite.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 26: Hexacoralla (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Hexacoralla 69
description: Reef corals from the genera Turbinaria, Pavona and Hydnophora arranged as a compositional bouquet. Lobate and frond-like growth forms are contrasted with branching morphologies to display the architectural variety produced by colonial scleractinian polyps.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 69: Hexacoralla (see here , here , here and here for details)

title: Haeckel Hexactinellae
description: Glass sponges of the class Hexactinellida, with the Venus's flower basket Euplectella aspergillum at the centre. Whole-body drawings are paired with magnified detail of the six-rayed siliceous spicules that fuse into a rigid lattice.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 35: Hexactinellae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Lacertilia
description: A gathering of lizards including the flying dragon Draco volans, the Texas horned lizard Phrynosoma cornutum and a Trioceros chameleon. The plate compares cranial casques, dermal spines and gliding membranes as variations on a single squamate body plan.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 79: Lacertilia (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Leptomedusae
description: Hydrozoan medusae of the order Leptothecata, including Aequorea and Zygocanna, drawn from above and the side. The radial canals and gonadal ribbons of the bell are emphasised, structures that bioluminesce in living Aequorea through the green fluorescent protein.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 36: Leptomedusae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Lichenes
description: Cup and reindeer lichens of the genus Cladonia, with branched podetia and squamulose primary thalli. The plate documents the symbiotic body in growth habit rather than dissection, recording species that dominate boreal heaths and Antarctic ground cover.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 83: Lichenes (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Melethallia
description: Coenobial green algae of the genus Pediastrum, whose flat plate-shaped colonies of polygonal cells float in fresh water. Arranged in concentric rings, the species illustrate how a fixed cell number per colony produces star-, wheel- and disc-shaped geometries.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 34: Melethallia (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Muscinae
description: A garden of mosses including Polytrichum commune, Sphagnum palustre and the dung moss Splachnum luteum with its inflated apophysis. Capsules, setae and leafy gametophytes are drawn at habit scale to compare bryophyte sporophyte architecture.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 72: Muscinae (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Mycetozoa
description: Slime moulds shown across their full life cycle, from amoeboid swarm cells emerging from spores to mature plasmodia of Badhamia and Trichia. The plate treats Mycetozoa as protists rather than fungi, reflecting Haeckel's broader reorganisation of the kingdoms of life.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 93: Mycetozoa (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Narcomedusae
description: Narcomedusan hydrozoans, deep-water jellyfish whose bell margin is scalloped into discrete lobes. Pegantha, Aeginura and Solmaris are shown from below to display the peripheral tentacles arising directly from the exumbrella, a defining feature of the order.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 16: Narcomedusae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Nepenthaceae
description: The tropical pitcher plant Nepenthes gymnamphora, drawn with its modified leaves forming fluid-filled traps. Carnivorous insectivory had been confirmed only decades earlier, and the plate emphasises the lid, peristome and tendril that suspend each pitcher.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 62: Nepenthaceae (see here , here and here and Biodiversity Heritage Library )

title: Haeckel Nudibranchia
description: Sea slugs of multiple aeolid and dorid lineages, including the orange-tipped Facelina auriculata and the spotted Tritonia hombergii. Cerata, rhinophores and branchial plumes are rendered in colour to record pigmentation that fades from preserved specimens.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 43: Nudibranchia (see here , here and here and Biodiversity Heritage Library )

title: Haeckel Ophiodea
description: Brittle stars arranged in radial symmetry across the plate, their slender, articulated arms fanning outward from small central discs. Haeckel emphasized the spiny ornamentation and pentaradial geometry of these echinoderms, drawing on specimens collected by the Challenger expedition.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 10: Ophiodea (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Ophiodea 70
description: Basket stars and brittle stars displayed from multiple angles, including the branching, fractal-like arms of Astrophyton at center. The composition contrasts dense knotted limbs with the more sparse, whip-like rays of smaller ophiuroids to convey echinoderm diversity.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 70: Ophiodea (see here , here and here , and Biodiversity Heritage Library )

title: Haeckel Orchidae
description: Tropical orchids rendered as a dense bouquet, including Odontoglossum and Oncidium species with their lipped flowers and trailing column structures. The plate stresses the asymmetric, almost zoomorphic petals that fascinated nineteenth-century naturalists studying floral mimicry.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 74: Orchidae (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Ostraciontes
description: Boxfish and cowfish drawn from multiple angles to highlight their rigid carapace of fused scale plates. Haeckel sets the angular, polyhedral bodies against one another to underscore how these tropical reef fish trade flexibility for armored defense.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 42: Ostraciontes (see here , here and here

title: Haeckel Pennatulida
description: Sea pens and sea pansies displayed as feather-like and disc-shaped colonies, with enlargements of individual polyps along the rachis. The plate documents the colonial architecture of these soft corals, showing how a single founding polyp gives rise to a polymorphic colony.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 19: Pennatulida (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Peridinea
description: Marine dinoflagellates magnified to reveal their armored cellulose plates, flagellar grooves, and projecting horns. Species like Ceratium tripos and Ornithocercus magnificus dominate the composition, their delicate sail-like extensions translated into precise lithographic line.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 14: Peridinea (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Peromedusae
description: Crown jellies of the genus Periphylla shown from side and underside, the conical bell ringed by deep grooves and trailing tentacles. The plate captures the bioluminescent deep-sea medusae described from Challenger expedition hauls.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 38: Peromedusae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Phaeodaria 1
description: Microscopic radiolarians with siliceous skeletons constructed in geometric forms, including the icosahedral lattice of Circogonia and the radial spines of Haeckeliana. Haeckel renders these planktonic single-celled organisms as if they were architectural blueprints.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 1: Phaeodaria (see here , here )

title: Haeckel Phaeodaria 61
description: Detailed terminations of radiolarian spicules from the Aulographis group, each tip elaborated into candelabras, asterisks, or forks. The plate isolates these microscopic ornament details to show the structural inventiveness of phaeodarian skeletons.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 61: Phaeodaria (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Platodes
description: Cercaria and trematode larvae presented with their characteristic forked tails and suckers visible. The plate documents parasitic flatworm life stages, drawing on Johannes Müller's and others' microscopic studies of digenean development.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 75: Platodes (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Polycyttaria
description: Colonial radiolarians shown as gelatinous spheres studded with individual cells and symbiotic zooxanthellae. The plate documents social plankton, where dozens of single-celled organisms cohabit in a shared transparent matrix.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 51: Polycyttaria (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Prosobranchia
description: Marine snails arranged as a still-life of shells, including the long-spired Murex pecten, the cone of Conus imperialis, and the harp-ribbed Harpa ventricosa. The plate functions as a survey of gastropod ornament, from spines to glossy enamel.

title: Haeckel Rotatoria
description: Microscopic rotifers shown with their ciliated coronas and transparent bodies, including the trumpet-shaped Stephanoceros and the colonial Lacinularia. The plate freezes the spinning corona that gives these freshwater animals their name.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 32: Rotatoria (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Sertulariae
description: Hydroid colonies branching like miniature ferns, with magnified hydrothecae and reproductive gonangia along each stem. The plate emphasizes how these cnidarians blur the line between plant-like growth form and animal anatomy.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 25: Sertulariae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Siphoneae
description: Caulerpa green algae rendered as climbing tendrils, with grape-cluster, peltate, and feathered varieties of a single giant unicell. The composition emphasizes how one continuous cytoplasm can mimic the form of an entire higher plant.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 64: Siphoneae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Siphonophorae
description: Porpema colonies shown with disc-like floats and trailing sexual polyps, set against magnified gas bladders. The plate documents how a siphonophore is not a single jelly but a coordinated colony of specialized zooids.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 17: Siphonophorae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Siphonophorae 37
description: Physophora hydrostatica colonies arranged with central swimming bells and a skirt of stinging tentacles. Haeckel pairs an overall view with anatomical details of the gas float and individual nectophore to dissect the colonial body plan.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 37: Siphonophorae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Siphonophorae 59
description: A Forskalia-like siphonophore dissected layer by layer, from intact colony to stripped polyp clusters. The sequence reveals the modular construction of a pelagic cnidarian, with each zooid specialized for swimming, feeding, or reproduction.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 59: Siphonophorae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Siphonophorae 7
description: Mature and immature Epibulia and Rhizophysa colonies shown alongside cross-sections of their gas bladders. The plate emphasizes the apical pneumatophore that allows these chains of polyps to drift just below the sea surface.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 7: Siphonophorae (see here , here )

title: Haeckel Siphonophorae 77
description: Praya and Bassia colonies displayed as chains of paired swimming bells with trailing reproductive medusae. Haeckel uses the bilateral nectophores to convey the streamlined hydrodynamics of calycophoran siphonophores.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 77: Siphonophorae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Spirobranchia
description: Brachiopod shells from the inside, with internal spiral lophophore supports visible in fossils such as Spirifer and Cyrtina. The plate doubles as a paleontological reference, juxtaposing Paleozoic forms to show the calcified scaffolding of the feeding organ.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 97: Spirobranchia (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Spumellaria
