description: Spherical and spindle-shaped radiolarian skeletons with concentric latticework and radiating spines. The plate isolates the polycystine forms whose silica architecture inspired Haeckel's broader argument that nature is intrinsically geometric.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 91: Spumellaria (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Spyroidea
description: Two-chambered radiolarian skeletons rendered as miniature cages, each with a sagittal constriction and ornamental feet. The composition catalogs the helmet-like Nassellaria forms drawn from Challenger expedition microfossil samples.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 22: Spyroidea (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Stauromedusae
description: Stalked jellyfish rendered as goblet-shaped medusae anchored by adhesive feet, including Haliclystus auricula clinging to algae. The plate captures these unusual sessile cnidarians from side and base views.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 48: Stauromedusae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Stephoidea
description: Ring-shaped radiolarian skeletons with single, double, or triple silica hoops, presented as crowns and lattices. The plate centers on simple-ringed Nassellaria, showing how minimal skeletal motifs can scaffold a living single cell.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 71: Stephoidea (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Teleostei
description: Bizarrely formed bony fish, including seahorses, the leafy sea dragon Phycodurus eques, and the antennated frogfish Antennarius striatus. The plate emphasizes camouflage and modified fins that disguise these teleosts as drifting weed.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 87: Teleostei (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Tetracoralla
description: Solitary and colonial rugose corals shown as polished cross-sections and side views, including the horn-shaped Omphyma turbinata. The plate is essentially paleontological, displaying the four-fold septal symmetry of an extinct Paleozoic order.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 29: Tetracoralla (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Thalamophora 12
description: Miliolid foraminifera arranged as porcelain-like shells with looping chambers, including Quinqueloculina and Cornuspira. The plate magnifies these microscopic shelled protists to reveal the sequential coiling that defines the order.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 12: Thalamophora (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Thalamophora 81
description: Lagenid foraminifera shown as flask- and bottle-shaped tests with single apertures, ornamented by ribs, spines, and squamose plates. Haeckel groups the variety to highlight how a basic vase form can be elaborated into dozens of species.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 81: Thalamophora (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Thalamphora
description: Elongate foraminifera with stacked or alternating chambers, including Nodosaria, Uvigerina, and Bolivina. The plate concentrates on multilocular benthic forms whose calcareous tests accumulate to build deep-sea sediments.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 2: Thalamophora (see here , here )

title: Haeckel Thoracostraca
description: A symmetrical plate of shrimp-like crustaceans, juxtaposing transparent adult Lucifer and Penaeus species with their nauplius and mastigopus larval stages. Haeckel uses the layout to argue visually for embryological recapitulation, treating the developmental sequence as ornamental composition.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 76: Thoracostraca (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Thuroidea
description: Sea cucumbers and their larvae arranged on a dark ground, showing Phyllophorus and Synapta alongside successive auricularia stages. The plate pairs adult body plans with their drifting planktonic young to emphasize life-cycle continuity within Holothuroidea.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 50: Thuroidea (see here , here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Tineida
description: A study of plume and leaf-mining moths in which Alucita and Pterophorus species spread their feathered wings into near-radial patterns. Magnified wing-scale details at the margins underscore the geometric microstructure beneath the apparently dusty surfaces.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 58: Tineida (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Trachomedusae
description: Bell-shaped trachymedusae are drawn from above, beside, and beneath, with the long manubrium of Geryonia trailing like a clapper. The transparent tissues let internal gonads and radial canals show through, turning anatomy into a chandelier-like ornament.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 26: Trachomedusae (see here , here and here )

title: Haeckel Trochilidae
description: Twelve hummingbird species, including the Sword-billed and Booted Racket-tail, are arrayed against black to display iridescent gorgets, crests, and tail streamers. Drawn from millinery skins, the postures are decorative rather than observational, foregrounding plumage geometry over flight behavior.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 99: Trochilidae (see here , here and here and Biodiversity Heritage Library )

title: Haeckel Tubulariae
description: Solitary and budding hydromedusae such as Sarsia and Coryne float across the page, several shown in the act of releasing free-swimming medusae from polyp colonies. The plate compresses an entire alternating life cycle into a single ornamental field.
credit: Kunstformen der Natur (1904), plate 6: Tubulariae (see here , here )

title: Houses of Parliament
description: One of nineteen views of the Houses of Parliament that Monet worked on from a balcony at St Thomas' Hospital across the Thames. Westminster's Gothic silhouette is silhouetted against a sun-soaked fog, the river burning with reflected colour as atmospheric haze flattens detail into pure tonal envelope.

title: London
description: Part of Monet's Houses of Parliament series painted from his window at St Thomas's Hospital, the canvas dissolves the Gothic Revival silhouette into mauve and orange vapour. The campaigns of 1899–1904 used the Thames fog as a coloured veil through which Monet tracked the changing hours.
credit: Musée d'Orsay Former version: The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 .

title: Portrait of Painter Peder Severin Kröyer
description: Tuxen depicts his fellow Skagen colonist Krøyer at work, palette in hand, eyes fixed on an unseen sitter. The portrait commemorates a friendship between the two leading Danish naturalist painters, set down a few years before Krøyer's mental collapse.

title: Sharing the News
description: A cluster of Venetian women pause from their chores to read aloud from a letter, faces tilted in shared attention. De Blaas mines the city's working-class courtyards for these intimate everyday vignettes, polished with the slick academic technique that earned him Belle Epoque popularity.

title: The Flirtation
description: Two young Venetians exchange glances across a sunlit doorway, the woman's costume crisp with embroidered linen and a flowered shawl. De Blaas built his career on such genre scenes of working Venice, prized in Edwardian salons for their sparkling finish and gentle theatrics.

title: The Houses of Parliament
description: The Gothic silhouette of Westminster dissolves into a haze of violet and orange across the Thames. Painted from a terrace at St Thomas's Hospital, the canvas belongs to Monet's London series, in which he tracked the same motif under shifting fog, smoke and sun.

title: Water Lilies
description: Pads of green and lavender float across a pond surface that fills the canvas, sky and bank registering only as reflections among the lilies. With this 1903-08 series Monet refined the immersive water-garden vision that would culminate in the Orangerie cycles.
credit: 1. Unknown source Unknown source 2. flickr.com 3. Sotheby's London,19 June 2007, L07007, lot 7

title: Waterloo Bridge
description: Part of Monet's London Thames series, the bridge dissolves into industrial mist warmed by low sun. Smokestacks beyond the river emit plumes that fuse fog, water, and sky into a unified veil of orange and violet.

title: Wounded Amazon
description: A bare-breasted Amazon staggers from the field clutching her side, her body silhouetted against a stormy ground in von Stuck's customary bituminous palette. The classical subject is treated with the muscular Symbolist eroticism he made the signature of the Munich Secession.

title: Yamamoto 1904
description: A fisherman bends to his work in a small skiff, rendered in flat planes of color and direct gouged lines. The print is a foundational image of Japan's sosaku-hanga (creative print) movement, in which the artist designed, carved, and printed the block himself rather than relying on the traditional ukiyo-e workshop division of labor.

title: Eugen von Blaas - Plauderei - 1905
description: Two women lean together over a parapet in animated chatter, the title's German for 'small talk' framing the scene as anecdote rather than narrative. De Blaas relishes the textile detail, from the patterned shawl to the starched apron, in his typical lustrous, salon-ready finish.

title: The Hungry Lion Throws Itself on the Antelope
description: Henri Rousseau — The Hungry Lion Throws Itself on the Antelope

title: The Struggle for Woman
description: Two nude warriors lock in combat over a sprawling female nude, a Symbolist allegory of erotic rivalry typical of von Stuck's Munich Secession output. Hard contour, bronzed flesh and a smouldering ground push the mythic theme toward late nineteenth-century decadence.

title: Water Lilies
description: An early canvas in the great water-lily cycle Monet pursued at his Giverny pond for the last three decades of his life. The horizon has already vanished; only floating pads, reflected sky and submerged depth remain, organised purely by colour.
credit: Museum of Fine Arts, Boston, online database

title: Salome
description: Salome dances before a moonlit sky, the platter bearing the head of the Baptist held up by a grinning attendant. Von Stuck stages the subject with bare midriff, jewelled adornments and the brooding theatricality favoured by fin-de-siècle Munich Symbolism.
credit: Eva Mendgen: Franz von Stuck 1863–1928. Benedikt Taschen Verlag, Köln 1994; ISBN 3-8228-8953-9

title: Salome
description: Franz von Stuck stages Salome in dark, lurid Symbolism: the dancer raised in ecstasy beneath jewelled veils while a servant hoists the Baptist's head into shadow. The picture distils the Munich Secession's fascination with the femme fatale.
credit: zAFMUUYyQJazIg at Google Cultural Institute , zoom level maximum

title: The Man with a Tulip
description: A pre-Cubist portrait of fellow painter Jean Metzinger, holding a single tulip against a vivid mosaic of pure colour. The divisionist touch and saturated complementaries reveal Delaunay's debt to Neo-Impressionism on the eve of his turn to abstraction.

title: Two Human Beings
description: A man and woman stand side by side on a stony shore, facing the sea but never one another. Munch returned to this image of unbridgeable distance for decades in paintings and woodcuts, here rendered in blunt blocks of beach grey, surf white and brooding water.

title: Water Lilies
description: Lily pads float in even rhythms across green-blue water, with a pale band of sky-reflection running along the upper edge. The picture comes from the first mature group of Nymphéas exhibited by Durand-Ruel in 1909.

title: Water Lilies by Monet
description: An overhead view of the Giverny pond, dotted with discs of pink and white lilies. Cool blues and greens shift toward lavender where the surrounding foliage is mirrored in still water, with no sky visible above the bank.

title: Waterlilies
description: A horizontal screen of lily pads hovers over reflected sky on the Giverny pond. By 1906 Monet had abolished the bank entirely, treating the water as a self-contained world the eye could enter at any point.
credit: EQEY9dAnBaXfnQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Bathing Men
description: Three nude men stride from the surf in a frieze-like composition, painted on the German island of Warnemünde. Munch celebrated sun, sea and male vigour with vehement verticals of brushwork, part of a triptych on the ages of man now in Helsinki's Ateneum.

title: Futsuka Zuki by Kawai Gyokudō
description: A slender new moon hangs above a quiet hillside in sumi ink and faint colour on silk, mounted as a hanging scroll. Kawai Gyokudō, a leading figure of the modern Nihonga movement, won first prize at the 1907 Tokyo industry exhibition with this lyrical evocation of the second-night sky.
credit: National Museum of Modern Art, Tokyo

title: Pond with Water Lilies
description: A vertical Giverny canvas in which the lily pond is rotated almost into pure surface pattern. Pinks and violets float over deep greens, the brushwork loose enough that the pads sometimes read as drifting clouds of pigment.
credit: 7wFvMeHQCRDVEw at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: The Chess Game
description: Two friends bend over a board in a sun-shot garden during one of Sargent's Alpine summers in the Simplon. The painter spent these holidays sketching members of his travelling party in shawls, hats and Kashmiri textiles. The watercolour-like oil handling and high-keyed greens are typical of these informal late landscapes.

title: The Snake Charmer
description: A dark silhouetted figure pipes serpents from a moonlit jungle on a riverbank, commissioned by the mother of Robert Delaunay. Rousseau, a self-taught customs officer, never left France; his jungles were composed from the Jardin des Plantes, picture books and dream, layered in his deliberate, flattened naïve manner.
credit: Google Art Project: Home - pic Maximum resolution.

title: The Song
description: A late portrait by the American Impressionist of a young woman absorbed in performance, rendered with the fluid brushwork and rich tonal harmonies Chase admired in Velazquez. The dark, atmospheric ground throws the figure's pale skin and parted lips into intimate relief.

title: Water Lilies
description: From the Giverny series, the Japanese-style pond seen close-up with no shore in sight. Lily pads punctuate broad zones of mauve and green reflection, the painted surface oscillating between water-skin and pure abstraction.

title: Water Lilies
description: Water Lilies, 1907, 19. 170

title: Water Lilies
description: An early canvas from the Water Lilies series begun in earnest after 1903, when Monet shifted from views of the Japanese bridge to the pond's surface alone. Floating pads anchor a shimmering ground in which clouds and willows are fused as reflection.

title: Waterlilies
description: Clusters of lilies drift on a pond shot through with violet and rose reflection, the pads rendered in short, scumbled strokes. The 1907 group is among Monet's most chromatically daring before his cataracts began to dull his vision.
credit: hgEnPzjBK2STHg at Google Cultural Institute , zoom level maximum

provenance.collection: Kawamura Memorial DIC Museum of Art
provenance.location: Kawamura Memorial DIC Museum of Art

title: The Doge's Palace
description: The Doge's Palace rises from the Venetian lagoon in dissolving pinks and aquamarines, painted during Monet's two-month stay in the city. The facade is treated less as architecture than as a screen for refracted light, with broken brushwork knitting stone, water and sky into a single luminous fabric.

title: The Grand Canal
description: Looking down the Grand Canal toward Santa Maria della Salute, Monet renders Venice as drifting reflections of pink and turquoise on water. The view comes from his only visit to the city, in autumn 1908, which yielded a late series exhibited at Bernheim-Jeune in 1912.

title: The Watercarrier
description: The Italian-Austrian painter de Blaas specialised in genteel scenes of Venetian life, here a young woman pausing with her copper water vessel. His glossy academic finish made such picturesque subjects popular with late-nineteenth-century international collectors.

title: Water Lilies
description: Lily blossoms sit like white embers on a deep mauve pond, the surface broken only by their slow geometry. The canvas dates from Monet's last full-sighted summers at Giverny before cataract surgery would alter his colour sense.

title: Water-Lilies
description: A canvas from the second great wave of Nymphéas pictures, painted as Monet exhibited forty-eight pond views at Durand-Ruel's Paris gallery. Pads drift across reflections of cloud and sky, the water surface flattened into a pure field of colour with no horizon to orient the viewer.

title: The Eiffel Tower
description: An early treatment of the iron tower that would obsess Delaunay for decades, viewed from below and toppling against a turbulent sky. The fractured planes and sharp diagonals translate Cubist analysis into a celebration of modern engineering.

title: Vesterhavet i storm. Efter solnedgang. Højen
description: A view of the North Sea at Højen on the northern tip of Jutland, painted near Tuxen's home at Skagen. Associated with the Skagen painters, Tuxen here forsakes the colony's clear summer light for a churning storm twilight, with thick impasto registering wind-driven spray.
credit: ygHVVTXFanFoYw at Google Cultural Institute , zoom level maximum

title: Crow and blossom by Ohara Koson
description: A solitary crow perches on a flowering branch, the bird's blue-black plumage set against pale petals. Koson's shin-hanga woodblock print refines Edo-period kachō-e bird-and-flower conventions for early-twentieth-century export collectors, prizing economical line and atmospheric ground.
credit: Woodblock print circa 1910 by Ohara Koson (1877-1945)

title: Tropical Forest with Monkeys
credit: This file was donated to Wikimedia Commons as part of a project by the National Gallery of Art . Please see the Gallery's Open Access Policy .

title: Water Lilies
description: Held by the Musée Marmottan Monet, which inherited the artist's studio holdings, this Nymphéas frames the pond as an unbroken field of reflected light. Pads cluster as horizontal accents across deep blues and rose, the composition organised by colour weight rather than perspective.

title: Champs de Mars: The Red Tower
description: The Eiffel Tower rises in fractured red and ochre planes above the Champ de Mars. Delaunay's Orphic Cubism dismantles the engineered iron icon into prismatic facets, part of his sustained series treating the tower as a modern motif of Parisian light and movement.

title: Eiffel Tower
description: Painted at the height of Delaunay's Cubist phase, the tower lurches and splinters as if seen simultaneously from multiple vantage points. Surrounding buildings collapse inward, dramatising the giddy modern experience of urban scale.

title: Repose
description: A languid female figure reclines on a sofa, head turned away, wrapped in shimmering ivory satin and gauze. Sargent's niece Rose-Marie Ormond was the model for several such drapery studies in the years before the First World War. The picture is essentially a virtuoso exercise in painting white folds against deep shadow.

title: Street with church in Kandern
description: A village street in the Black Foothills opens toward the parish church, the planes of road, façade, and sky reduced to firm coloured patches. Painted during Macke's Kandern period, the scene shows him absorbing Cézanne and the early Blaue Reiter palette before his shift to fully saturated colour.

title: The Woman
description: Eve panel from a triptych on the Fall, with Adam and the serpent occupying the flanking compartments. Byam Shaw extends the late Pre-Raphaelite manner of Burne-Jones and Rossetti into the Edwardian era, with hothouse colour and stylised vegetation. The figure is set against a tapestry of curling leaves and ripe fruit.

title: Slavs in their Original Homeland: Between the Turanian Whip and the Sword of the Goths
description: The opening canvas of Mucha's Slav Epic shows a couple cowering in a moonlit field while spectral horsemen ride overhead, embodying the early sufferings of the Slavic peoples. Mucha's tempera-and-oil technique on huge canvas gives the symbolist scene a pale, dreamlike pallor distinct from his Art Nouveau posters.

provenance.collection: museum collection of the Prague City Gallery

title: The City of Paris
description: A monumental synthesis combining the three Graces, the Seine and the Eiffel Tower into a single panorama of modern Paris. Delaunay fragments the city into faceted Cubist planes while introducing the prismatic colour rhythms that would define his Orphist style.

title: The Tiger
description: A crouching tiger fractured into prismatic facets of yellow and black, its body locked into the surrounding crystalline landscape. Marc, co-founder of Der Blaue Reiter, treated animals as emblems of an unfallen spiritual world, here borrowing Cubist faceting to fuse predator and habitat into a single restless rhythm.
credit: zAE32SNgqpnj9Q at Google Cultural Institute , zoom level maximum

title: Jealousy
description: A bearded man stares from the foreground in green-tinged pallor while behind him a woman in red plucks fruit beside another suitor. Munch returned to the jealousy motif repeatedly, treating it as a Garden-of-Eden parable charged with autobiographical anxiety.

title: Promenade
description: Strolling figures pass beneath trees on a sunlit avenue, simplified into faceless silhouettes against blocks of pure colour. Macke, a central member of Der Blaue Reiter, used such promenade scenes to translate everyday Rhineland leisure into the prismatic colour planes he absorbed from Delaunay's Orphism.
credit: nwGW-7U9Uhbm9Q at Google Cultural Institute , zoom level maximum

title: The Tightrope Walker
description: A tightrope walker poised above a fairground crowd, the scene built from interlocking translucent diamonds of colour. Painted in the artist's most prolific year, the composition shows Macke fusing his Blaue Reiter sensibility with the prismatic geometry he had absorbed from Robert Delaunay in Paris.

title: Weeping Nude
description: A naked woman sits on the edge of a bed, head bowed into her hands, the room dissolving in nervous strokes around her. Munch left the figure raw and unidealised, channelling psychological collapse through agitated brushwork rather than narrative detail.

title: Clear Morning with Willows
description: Pale lilies float on a luminous pond crossed by the trailing reflections of weeping willows. The panel forms part of the Grandes Décorations cycle Monet painted at Giverny for the oval rooms of the Musée de l'Orangerie in Paris, where pond, sky and bank dissolve into a continuous surface.
credit: 7QHpOuLSwKTH8A at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: In Russia. Soul of the people
description: An expansive procession of Russian people - peasants, monks, soldiers and intelligentsia - moves across a northern landscape in search of spiritual truth. Nesterov treats his pre-revolutionary allegory in luminous, restrained colour, blending Symbolist mood with Orthodox iconographic gravity.

title: Matin
description: Pale dawn light catches the lily pads on the Giverny pond, the water dissolving into broad horizontal sweeps of mauve and cream. The work belongs to the great cycle Monet developed in his late, near-blind years for the Orangerie ovals.
credit: IAHgEdqp703amQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Omphale
description: The Lydian queen Omphale stands draped in the lion-skin of Hercules, who served her in bondage as punishment for murder. Byam Shaw, a late Pre-Raphaelite, treats the mythological role-reversal with jewel-toned costume detail and decorative outline drawn from his Birmingham School training.

title: Reflets d'arbres
description: Inverted trees and clouds shimmer in the pond, lily pads punctuating the surface as the only signal of where water meets reflection. The picture pushes Monet's late style toward near-abstraction.
credit: _QE0pUecImBBMQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Reflets verts
description: Floating pads drift across a mirror of green leaf-shadow, with no sky or bank to anchor the view. The canvas is a study toward the Grandes Décorations Monet eventually donated to the French state for the Orangerie.
credit: -wEwoHEvFukepQ at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: The Clouds
description: Banks of cloud are reflected on the still pond surface, the lilies riding across them like islands. Painted as Monet wrestled with cataracts, the panel forms part of his bequest of mural-scale Décorations to France.
credit: YQEt9_UVgiL-Og at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: The Two Willows
description: Two weeping willows frame the composition like proscenium curtains, their trunks pale against drifts of pink and blue water. The mural-scale study belongs to Monet's late Giverny project conceived as continuous, all-encompassing decoration.
credit: 3AEQUJnQ8YnB_A at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Water Lilies
description: Water Lilies

title: Water Lilies
description: Water Lilies

title: Goyō Hashiguchi
description: A young woman freshly emerged from the bath gathers her loosened hair, skin warm against the cool indigo of her robe. Hashiguchi, a leading shin-hanga designer, revived the bijin-ga tradition of Utamaro with modern psychological intimacy and richly burnished woodblock printing.

title: Kanae Yamamoto
description: A cow rendered in sōsaku-hanga style, its bulk built up from carved planes of color. Yamamoto launched Japan's creative-print movement, insisting that the artist personally design, carve, and print the block, and his rural subjects helped redirect woodblock practice from commercial ukiyo-e toward modernist self-expression.

title: Self Portrait in 4th Avenue Studio
description: Painted in his Fourth Avenue studio late in life, Chase shows himself in a dark suit among the bric-à-brac collection that doubled as his teaching tool. The bravura handling and theatrical self-presentation summarise the persona he cultivated at the Art Students League and Chase School.
credit: 1. Upload Bot (Cobalty), possibly from a Art-CD-Rom 2. Richmond Art Museum, online collection

title: Water Lilies
description: A large-scale water-lily canvas of 1915, painted in the studio Monet built at Giverny to accommodate his ever-bigger formats. The pond has lost its banks entirely; tangled reflections of weeping willow descend through deep blues and violets while pads float as flat islands of green.

title: Water Lilies
description: One of the water-lily compositions Monet painted at his Giverny pond, dissolving the surface into floating pads, reflected sky, and submerged depths. The late series pushed Impressionism toward an immersive, near-abstract vision of light on water.

title: Water Lilies
description: From Monet's late Giverny series, the painting abandons horizon and shoreline for a pure expanse of water surface. Lily pads and agapanthus stalks float among broken reflections of sky and trees, the brushwork loosened toward near-abstraction.

title: Parting Spring by Kawai Gyokudo
description: The left panel of a pair of folding screens by Kawai Gyokudō, designated an Important Cultural Property of Japan. Working in the nihonga tradition, the artist evokes the melancholy of late spring through misty mountains and falling cherry blossoms.

title: Parting Spring by Kawai Gyokudo
description: The right panel of Gyokudō's Parting Spring screens, completing a continuous landscape of waterfalls, drifting petals, and veiled peaks. The nihonga master fuses traditional ink technique with subtle modern atmospheric effect.

title: Taj Mahal' by Charles W. Bartlett
description: Made during Bartlett's Asian travels and printed in Tokyo by the publisher Watanabe Shozaburo, the sheet fuses British watercolour atmosphere with Japanese shin-hanga woodblock craft. The marble mausoleum floats above its reflecting pool in flat, graded washes of dusk colour.

title: Water-Lilies
description: One of the late Giverny pond pictures, painted as Monet was preparing the monumental Grandes Décorations now in the Orangerie. Lily pads drift across a mirrored surface in which sky, willow, and water trade places, the paint laid in long, lapping strokes of green, mauve, and rose.
credit: 1. The Yorck Project (2002) 10.000 Meisterwerke der Malerei (DVD-ROM), distributed by DIRECTMEDIA Publishing GmbH. ISBN : 3936122202 . 2. [1]

title: Philosophers
description: A double portrait of theologians Pavel Florensky and Sergei Bulgakov walking in conversation through a quiet wooded landscape. Nesterov's pared composition and contemplative palette frame the encounter as an icon of Russian religious philosophy on the eve of revolution.

title: Water Lilies
description: The pond surface fills the canvas edge to edge, lilies massing in clusters of rose and ivory among dark vegetal reflections. Painted as Monet's eyesight deteriorated, the picture's looser, more saturated handling anticipates mid-century abstraction.

title: John Singer Sargent
description: A personification of the defeated Synagogue, blindfolded and clutching a broken staff, painted for Sargent's Triumph of Religion mural cycle in the Boston Public Library. The medieval iconographic pairing with a triumphant Church drew sustained protest from Boston's Jewish community. The cycle was left unfinished, partly in the wake of that controversy.

title: Tabi Miyage Dai 1 - Shu
description: From his first Souvenirs of Travel series, Hasui depicts the Tsuta hot spring deep in the mountains of Mutsu, framed by stands of beech and birch. The cool tonal harmonies typify the shin-hanga revival of landscape woodblock printing he led with publisher Watanabe Shozaburo.
credit: Online Collection of Brooklyn Museum ; Photo: Brooklyn Museum, 68.35.5_IMLS_PS3.jpg

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: The Mishima River winding through Mutsu Province in northern Honshū, drawn from sketches Hasui made on his first long tour of the region. Issued by Watanabe in 1919, it stands among the earliest plates of the Souvenirs of Travel series.

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: A still woodblock view of Tsuta Marsh in the northern province of Mutsu, where reflected trees double the autumn forest on glassy water. The print belongs to Hasui's first Souvenirs of Travel series, in which the artist toured rural Japan recording the country's quieter corners.

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: A mountain temple outside Sendai is glimpsed through a screen of trees, the architecture half-absorbed into the hillside. Hasui's compact composition and softly graded skies belong to the shin-hanga revival, which reworked classical ukiyo-e idioms with modern atmospheric effects.

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: The cliff face of Senjomaku rises from the still surface of Lake Towada, its scale exaggerated by Hasui's high horizon. Carved from successive woodblocks, the print exploits the bokashi gradient technique to soften water into mist.

title: The Water Lily Pond
description: From the late Giverny series, the canvas crops the lily pond into a near-abstract field of floating pads and reflected sky. By 1919 Monet's failing eyesight pushed him toward broader, more saturated touches that would culminate in the wraparound Grandes Décorations of the Orangerie.
credit: 1. Private collection institution QS:P195,somevalue,P3831,Q768717 2. Christie's , LotFinder: entry 5100003 (sale 7599, lot 16, london, 24 June 2008)

title: Tōkyō jūnidai
description: Early summer rain falls on the precincts of Hie (Sanno) Shrine, figures sheltering under umbrellas along the wet stone steps. Hasui's woodblock contrasts vertical rain lines against muted greens and greys in the shin-hanga manner.

title: Evening Snow at Terajima Village
description: A lone figure trudges past snow-laden trees and a thatched cottage as dusk settles over the rural outskirts of Tokyo. Hasui's shin-hanga woodblock combines Western perspective and atmosphere with the layered ink and pigment registration of the traditional Japanese print.

title: Odalisque
description: A reclining model in striped pantaloons inhabits the patterned Nice studio Matisse converted into a North African stage set. The early Riviera odalisques mark his return to sensuous, decorative figuration after the austerity of the war years.

title: Reflections of Clouds on the Water-Lily Pond
description: A late, large-scale Giverny panel related to the Grandes Décorations Monet was preparing for the Orangerie. Clouds are present only as inverted reflections, the painting collapsing sky, water and lily into one immersive plane.
credit: Digital image, The Museum of Modern Art, New York

title: Remaining Snow at Inokashira
description: A shin-hanga woodblock print of the wooded park at Inokashira in Tokyo, with patches of late snow lingering beside dark water. Hasui treats the season with the muted palette and atmospheric stillness that defined his landscape practice.

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: An autumn road through the old Koshiji region, recorded in the first of Hasui's Souvenirs of Travel series. The shin-hanga print pairs sketchbook directness with Watanabe Shōzaburō's carefully graded colour blocks.

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: Iwai Beach on the Bōshū coast, fishing boats drawn up under a high cloud bank. Hasui's first travel album returns again and again to such quiet shorelines, the bokashi gradients standing in for shifting maritime weather.

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: Workmen load quarried stone onto a flat-bottomed boat in Awa Province, the labour rendered with the documentary clarity Hasui brought to early Taishō travel. The print belongs to Souvenirs of Travel I, the series that established his collaboration with publisher Watanabe.

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: The Asano River at Kanazawa, its banks lined by the dark eaves of the old castle town. Hasui treats the scene in muted dusk colour, balancing the traditional Edo-period view with a modern, photographically framed viewpoint.

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: A view of the Nagare pleasure quarter in Kanazawa, lanterns warming the wooden façades along a narrow lane. Hasui finds in the licensed district the same hushed melancholy he gave his rural and seaside views.

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: Autumn descends on the hot-spring village of Arayu in Shiobara, the slopes blazing with red maple above wooden bathhouses and a narrow river. The print captures the kind of seasonal regional travel that drove the early shin-hanga market.

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: Yuhi Falls drops between mossy cliffs at Shiobara, rendered with the layered greens and quietly observed water Hasui made his signature. Carved and printed in collaboration with publisher Watanabe Shozaburo, it is part of the first Souvenirs of Travel cycle.

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: Pine-fringed Kude Beach in Wakasa Province curves along the Sea of Japan, the foreground trees framing a low strip of water and sky. Hasui's clean geometry and reduced palette draw on Hiroshige's coastal landscapes while pushing toward modern simplification.

title: Tabi miyage dai isshū
description: Lake Kugushi lies under a still, overcast sky, its shore traced by reeds and a scatter of fishing boats. The composition is typical of Hasui's first travel series, which favored the unspectacular waterways and country roads of provincial Japan.

title: Tōkyō jūnidai
description: Daikon-gashi, the radish wharf along the Nihonbashi River, from the series Twelve Scenes of Tokyo. Hasui combines crisp architectural drawing with soft tonal washes typical of the shin-hanga revival of landscape woodblock printing.

title: Tōkyō jūnidai
description: The Kaminohashi Bridge in Fukagawa, from the series Twelve Scenes of Tokyo. Hasui captures a quiet canal-side moment, using graded inks and reflective water in the shin-hanga tradition that updated ukiyo-e cityscape conventions.

title: Tōkyō jūnidai
description: Evening at the Kiba lumber yards, where stacked timber and floating logs catch the last light along the canal. From Twelve Scenes of Tokyo, the print exemplifies Hasui's shin-hanga blend of working-city subject and lyrical atmosphere.

title: Tōkyō jūnidai
description: A view of fishing boats anchored in the bay off Shinagawa, sails reflected on a tranquil sea. Part of Twelve Scenes of Tokyo, the design relies on flat planes of graded blue and economical silhouettes characteristic of shin-hanga.

title: Tōkyō jūnidai
description: A winter moon rises over the bare expanse of the Toyama plain, with tiny figures crossing a darkened field. From Twelve Scenes of Tokyo, the print reduces the landscape to broad tonal bands of deep blue and silvered sky.

title: Water-Lily pond
description: Water lilies float on the green-blue surface of the Giverny pond, the bank dissolved in a tapestry of brushstrokes that releases the picture from any horizon. Painted during Monet's late, near-abstract Nymphéas campaign and now installed in Tadao Ando's Chichu Art Museum on Naoshima, the canvas is housed in pure natural light.

title: Waterlilies Pond
description: Waterlilies Pond, Green Reflection

title: Woman in Blue Combing Her Hair
description: A woman in a sheer blue summer kimono draws a comb through her loosened hair, her concentration absolute. Hashiguchi was a leading shin-hanga designer who oversaw every stage of his prints; the work uses mica grounds and subtle keyblock embossing to revive the bijin tradition for the Taishō era.
credit: Walters Art Museum : Home page Info about artwork

title: Asahi Bridge in Ojiya
description: A woodblock print from the Souvenirs of Travel II series depicts the Asahi Bridge spanning the river at Ojiya. Hasui's shin-hanga style fuses Western perspective with traditional Japanese block-printing, building atmosphere through layered bokashi gradations.

title: Kawase Ebisu-11
description: A quiet harbour scene from the Souvenirs of Travel II series, capturing a wooden boat at the small port of Ebisu on Sado Island. Kawase, a leading shin-hanga artist, renders the wet stone and overcast sky in muted, atmospheric washes that revive the woodblock tradition for the modern era.

title: Part of the Byōdō-in Temple at Uji
description: A partial view of the Phoenix Hall at Byodo-in in Uji, rendered as a shin-hanga woodblock print. Hasui isolates a fragment of the eleventh-century temple's tiled roofline, using flat colour fields and quiet tonal washes characteristic of the Souvenirs of Travel II series.

title: Sailboats
description: Sailboats lie at anchor in a sheltered bay, their masts and sails answered by wide reflections in glassy water. The composition belongs to Yoshida's celebrated set of six prints in which the same sailboat block was printed in different colorways to render morning, afternoon, evening, mist, and night.
credit: book scan: "Printed to perfection. Twentieth-century Japanese Prints from the Robert O. Muller Collection". Amsterdam: Hotei, 2004, p.46.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Rain falls in long diagonal strokes across the wooden stage of Kiyomizu-dera in Kyoto, its temple roofs darkened against a washed grey sky. Hasui treats the famed pilgrimage site not as a monument but as an atmospheric encounter with weather.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: A quiet stretch of beach in Echigo Province frames moored boats and a low coastline under heavy cloud. Printed for Watanabe Shozaburo, the sheet exemplifies the shin-hanga preference for unremarkable rural views observed at a contemplative distance.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Kosho Temple at Himi nestles among trees on the Etchu coast, its tiled roof anchoring a composition of slope, sea and sky. Hasui's keyblock outlines are softened by carefully overlapping color blocks printed in registered succession.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Bare winter branches stand against the pale Hozu River at Arashiyama outside Kyoto, a district usually depicted in cherry blossom or maple. Hasui takes the inverse season, finding austere geometry where his predecessors found color.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Moonlight silvers the surface of Lake Kamo on Sado Island, the far shore reduced to a dark silhouette. The night scene relies on the bokashi technique, gradient printing across a single block to model the broad sky.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: A rough beach hut at Himi sits low against the Etchu shoreline, its dark timbers contrasted with pale sand and water. Hasui's interest is in working coastal architecture rather than scenic landmarks, a recurring trait of the second Souvenirs cycle.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: A street view in the Shimohonda neighborhood of Kanazawa shows a low row of merchant houses under a wide, flat sky. The print's restrained palette and elongated horizontal proportion are characteristic of Hasui's urban observation prints.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: The Yanagawa district in Kai Province appears as a cluster of farmhouses and rice paddies set against distant mountains. Hasui sketched directly from life on his journeys, then reworked the studies for the carvers and printers of Watanabe's workshop.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Wooden irrigation troughs lift water across a Sado hillside, a practical landscape of rural infrastructure. The print's quiet subject reflects Hasui's commitment to ordinary working scenes over the picturesque set pieces of older ukiyo-e.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Stone lanterns flank the wooded approach to Kasuga Shrine in Nara, a route lined with thousands of votive lights to the Fujiwara tutelary deities. Hasui sets a single human figure within deep vertical trees to dramatize the scale of the precinct.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Nigatsu-do, the hall on the eastern slope of Todai-ji where the Omizutori water-drawing rite is held each spring, is shown perched above its veranda balustrade. Hasui's diagonal viewpoint compresses the wooden gallery into a strong horizontal band.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Morning along Osaka's Dotonbori canal finds the famed entertainment district at its quietest, the theater district's lanterns unlit and its banks emptied of crowds. The composition trades nightlife spectacle for a city caught off-stage.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: The mining town of Aikawa on Sado climbs a hillside in tightly packed wooden houses, each roof outlined by Hasui's keyblock. Once the seat of the Tokugawa silver and gold mines, the settlement appears here as a quiet provincial village.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: The village of Kamo on Sado Island is laid out along the lake shore, low farmhouses set among rice fields. Hasui composes a near-empty middle ground that lets the water and ridge of the island do most of the visual work.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Mano Bay opens broadly on the western coast of Sado, its beach and headlands rendered in Hasui's typical low-saturation greens and blues. The print is part of an extended Sado suite within the second Souvenirs series.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: A steep road climbs the Nishimikawa slope on Sado Island, its gradient cutting between dense pines. Hasui chooses an everyday traveler's vantage rather than a famous-place view, in keeping with the modest tone of the series.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: The harbor of Ogi at the southern tip of Sado Island holds a cluster of fishing boats below sheltering hills. Hasui's composition layers sea, vessel and shore into clearly delimited bands of overlapping flat color.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Takamatsu Castle in Sanuki Province, one of the few sea-fed castles in Japan, is glimpsed across its tide-fed moat. Hasui's view registers the rebuilt keep and surrounding stone embankments under a softly graded shin-hanga sky.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: The fishing town of Miyazu in Tango Province, near the spit of Amanohashidate, opens onto a calm bay edged by mountains. Hasui's restrained palette of slate blues and greens is tuned to the overcast light of the Sea of Japan coast.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Night rain falls heavily on the port of Teradomari, lantern light scattering across wet ground. Hasui was a master of nocturnes, and the sheet uses successive overprintings to build a saturated, almost lacquered darkness.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Dawn snow lies thick over the harbor at Ogi on Sado, with moored boats reduced to silhouettes against pale water. The print pairs a graded pink horizon with cold blue shadows, a daybreak chord Hasui returned to throughout his career.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: Snow blankets the pine spit of Amanohashidate, the narrow sandbar in Tango Bay long counted among the Three Views of Japan. Hasui strips its familiar profile back to a quiet white horizontal between sea and sky.

title: Tabi miyage dai nishū
description: A clear winter day at Miyajima sees fresh snow lining the rooftops of Itsukushima and the shore of the Inland Sea, the famous floating torii absent from this oblique view. Hasui exploits the contrast of warm vermilion architecture against cold white slopes.

title: Tokyo junidai
description: From the series Twelve Scenes of Tokyo, Hasui frames the temple steps of Atagoyama beneath blossoming cherry. A leading figure of the shin-hanga revival, he pairs traditional woodblock craftsmanship with Western perspective and a cool, atmospheric palette of dusk blues.

title: Atlas and the Hesperides
description: A circular ceiling painting for the rotunda of the Boston Museum of Fine Arts, showing the Titan stooping under the celestial sphere while the nymphs of the western garden recline at his feet. Sargent took on the rotunda decoration as a late, demanding mural commission. The composition reads from any vantage on the floor below.

title: Night Shadows - Oct 1922 Shadowland
description: An anonymous pedestrian crosses an empty street under a sharply raking street lamp, watched from a high, vertiginous viewpoint. The etching, published in Shadowland magazine, sets out the lonely urban geometry Hopper would later expand into his great oil paintings.
credit: Shadowland (Sep. 1922 - Feb. 1923) on the Internet Archive

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: An early Hasui woodblock from Selected Views of Japan, showing thatched roofs at Kazusa village in former Hizen Province on the Kyūshū coast. The print already shows the publisher Watanabe's house style: precise architecture, soft skies, and water rendered in layered indigo wash.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: The cliffside Kannon Hall at Abuto, perched above the Inland Sea, drawn from Hasui's Selected Views of Japan. The shin-hanga print exploits a high horizon and bokashi water gradations to set the small temple against a wide expanse of tide.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A coastal view of Goryō on the Amakusa islands of Kyushu, from Hasui's Selected Views of Japan. The shin-hanga design pairs distant headlands with quiet anchorage, demonstrating the careful balance of woodblock colour and reserve that defined Hasui's travel sheets.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A woodblock print from Hasui's Selected Views of Japan, depicting Mount Onsengadake on the Shimabara Peninsula as seen across the water from Amakusa. Hasui captures the silhouetted volcano in the soft tonal washes that defined his shin-hanga landscapes.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: Morning light over the hot-spring town of Beppu, rendered in the muted blues and greys typical of Hasui's shin-hanga manner. Part of the Selected Views of Japan series, the print balances atmospheric mist against the orderly geometry of harbor architecture.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A view of the seaside district of Hakozaki in former Chikuzen Province, near present-day Fukuoka. Hasui places the famous shrine precinct within a low coastal panorama, using flat color planes characteristic of his shin-hanga collaborations with publisher Watanabe Shozaburo.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: Hasui depicts the Kayagafuchi rapids deep in Chomonkyo Gorge, a famous Yamaguchi ravine of sheer rock walls and clear water. The print belongs to Selected Views of Japan and shows his characteristic handling of river surfaces in graded blue.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A quiet hot-spring scene at Tochinoki in former Higo Province, set among Kumamoto's mountain forests. Hasui frames the bathhouse village with overhanging trees, employing the soft graded tones that became his signature within the shin-hanga movement.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: An island view of Kabeshima off the Hizen coast in present-day Saga Prefecture, shown with low cliffs meeting still water. The print is part of Hasui's Selected Views of Japan, his sustained traveler's record of regional scenery.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: The fishing village of Kyodomari in former Hizen Province, set into a sheltered cove of the Kyushu coast. Hasui uses softly registered color blocks and a low horizon line, in keeping with the contemplative pace of the Selected Views of Japan series.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: The Kotsuki River winding through Kagoshima city, with the volcanic outline of Sakurajima implied in the distance. Part of Selected Views of Japan, the print weighs urban riverbank against open water in Hasui's characteristic flat color planes.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A view of Sakurajima, the active volcano rising from the bay opposite Kagoshima, here seen across calm water. From Selected Views of Japan, Hasui simplifies the mountain into a tonal silhouette in the manner of the shin-hanga landscape.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A street scene at Kasuga-machi in Kumamoto, with low wooden buildings receding along a quiet thoroughfare. Issued in Selected Views of Japan, it shows Hasui turning from grand scenery to the modest texture of a provincial town.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: The vermilion Daigokuden, main hall of the Heian Shrine in Kyoto, modeled on the imperial palace of the ancient Heian capital. Hasui sets the bright lacquered architecture against open gravel courtyard and a soft sky in this Selected Views of Japan plate.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: Sofuku-ji, an Obaku Zen temple in Nagasaki founded by Chinese merchants in the seventeenth century, recognizable by its red Ming-style gate. Hasui frames the precinct within Selected Views of Japan, marking Nagasaki's distinctive Sino-Japanese heritage.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A view of Okayama Castle, the sixteenth-century black-walled keep known as the Crow Castle, mirrored in the Asahi River. Hasui treats the silhouette in deep tonal blocks for his Selected Views of Japan.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: The steep slope leading up to Senko-ji Temple in the port town of Onomichi, with rooftops descending toward the Inland Sea. From Selected Views of Japan, the print captures Onomichi's celebrated layered terrain.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: The volcanic Mount Mayuyama rising behind the harbor of Shimabara on Kyushu's Ariake coast. Within Selected Views of Japan, Hasui pairs working harbor and looming peak in a composition typical of his coastal landscapes.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: Tsukumojima, the cluster of small islets scattered through the bay near Shimabara on Kyushu. From Selected Views of Japan, Hasui arranges the pine-tufted islands against still water in graded blue bokashi.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: The Golden Pavilion of Kinkaku-ji in Kyoto under fresh snow, its gilded upper stories set against bare branches and a frozen pond. From Selected Views of Japan, this winter scene is among Hasui's most familiar treatments of a celebrated subject.

title: Starry Night
description: Painted from the terrace of Munch's Ekely estate outside Oslo, this nocturne reduces landscape to broad zones of indigo, snow-white and shadow. A revisitation of his 1893 motif, it trades earlier symbolism for a quieter, late-career meditation on solitude under the winter sky.

title: The Eiffel Tower and the Champ de Mars Gardens
description: A later, lyrical revisiting of the Eiffel Tower set against the green expanse of the Champ de Mars. By the 1920s Delaunay had softened his Cubist fragmentation into rhythmic curves and bright contrasting colour, integrating the tower into a holiday vision of Paris.
credit: Hirshhorn Museum and Sculpture Garden Collection

title: Franz von Stuck
description: A late self-portrait on board, signed and dated 1923, presents the Munich Secession founder in dark dress against a neutral ground. The clipped scale and sober finish reflect von Stuck's habit of treating his own image as part of his public persona as artist-prince.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A view of Uchisange in Okayama, issued in Hasui's series Selected Views of Japan. Working in the shin-hanga revival of ukiyo-e, he uses graded bokashi printing and a quiet, twilight palette to capture a fleeting weather effect over the streetscape.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A coastal view of Iwafune in the former Bingo Province, in which Hasui sets fishing vessels against weathered shoreline rocks. The print belongs to the Selected Views of Japan series and exemplifies his preference for unpeopled, contemplative landscape.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: Cherry trees in bloom at Kakize in former Bungo Province, treated with the soft pinks and grey greens of early spring. Issued within the Selected Views of Japan series, it joins Hasui's broader survey of Japan's regional natural calendar.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A view of Kanahama on the Hizen coast, where Hasui balances rocky foreground shore against a broader open seascape. Issued in the Selected Views of Japan series, it reflects his interest in the quieter shorelines of Kyushu.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: Miyuki Bridge below the towering walls of Kumamoto Castle, with figures crossing in front of the fortress's distinctive curved stonework. The print belongs to Hasui's Selected Views of Japan and pairs civic architecture with everyday passage.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: Lake Ezu on the outskirts of Kumamoto, its still water spreading toward distant mountains. From Selected Views of Japan, the print exemplifies Hasui's preference for water surfaces graded in blue bokashi to suggest depth and weather.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: Evening along the Kamo River in Kyoto, with low riverside buildings silhouetted against a graded dusk sky. Part of Selected Views of Japan, the print pairs Hasui's interest in city water with the long twilight of a Kyoto summer.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: Nonomiya Shrine in the bamboo groves of Sagano, west of Kyoto, traditionally the site where imperial princesses purified themselves before service at Ise. Hasui treats the dim grove and torii in greens and greys for Selected Views of Japan.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A view of Kanaya-machi, a hillside neighborhood overlooking the harbor of Nagasaki. Issued within Selected Views of Japan, the print sets stepped streets and tile roofs against the city's sheltered bay in Hasui's flat shin-hanga palette.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A modest hot-spring village at Anraku in Osumi, southern Kyushu, set among low hills. The print is part of Hasui's Selected Views of Japan and reflects his persistent interest in regional bathing towns rendered in muted tones.

title: After the Battle of Grunwald
description: From the twenty-canvas Slav Epic that Mucha worked on for nearly two decades, this scene shows the aftermath of the 1410 Polish-Lithuanian victory over the Teutonic Knights. The Art Nouveau poster designer here deploys monumental tempera-and-oil composition to advance a pan-Slavic political mythology.

provenance.collection: museum collection of the Prague City Gallery

title: Gion Bridge in Hondo
description: The arched Gion bridge at Hondo on Amakusa, a woman in kimono pausing on the span. Hasui's plate from the Selection of Views of Japan series is a shin-hanga woodblock that fuses ukiyo-e composition with Western atmospheric perspective and softly graded blue skies.

title: Matsue
description: A crescent moon hangs over the still moat and tile roofs of Matsue Castle, the lights of the lakeside town reflected in pale washes of water. The print is from Hasui's 'Souvenirs of Travel' series for publisher Watanabe Shōzaburō, a flagship of the early-twentieth-century shin-hanga revival.
credit: Flickr The Sandiego Museum of Art collection

title: Shironeko by Takahashi Hiroaki
description: A white cat is shown in profile against a plain ground, executed in the shin-hanga revival of woodblock printing. Shōtei's subtle gradations of grey in the fur and quiet, almost domestic mood typify the movement's blend of tradition and Western naturalism.

title: Tabi miyage dai sanshū
description: A panoramic alpine vista showing Asahi Peak as seen from Mt. Hakuba in the Northern Japanese Alps. Hasui's woodblock layers translate distant snow ridges and atmospheric haze into broad fields of muted colour, typical of his shin-hanga landscapes.

title: Tabi miyage dai sanshū
description: A traveller's view along the Nakayama-Shichiri road threading through the mountains of Hida Province. The print belongs to Hasui's Souvenirs of Travel III, in which woodblock bokashi gradations evoke the changing light of remote inland routes.

title: Tabi miyage dai sanshū
description: A nocturnal scene at the Arifuku hot springs in Iwami Province, with lantern-lit inns reflected on damp streets. Hasui exploits the deep, saturated indigos that became a signature of his nocturnes within the shin-hanga movement.

title: Tabi miyage dai sanshū
description: A coastal view of Hinomisaki on the Izumo peninsula, where pine-clad cliffs meet the Sea of Japan. Hasui's woodblock translates wind, wave and weather into a few decisive colour blocks, a characteristic of his Souvenirs of Travel III landscapes.

title: Tabi miyage dai sanshū
description: A hazy moon hangs over the canals of Matsue in Izumo Province, the city's wooden buildings dissolving into a violet dusk. Hasui's bokashi shading delivers the title's oborozuki — the misted moon of classical Japanese poetry.

title: Tabi miyage dai sanshū
description: Fishing boats and tile-roofed houses cluster along the harbour at Mihogaseki on the Shimane peninsula. The shin-hanga print balances closely observed coastal architecture against the broad calm of the bay.

title: Tabi miyage dai sanshū
description: An autumn rainbow arcs above rice fields at Hatta in Kaga Province. Hasui uses overlapping bokashi gradations to render the prismatic band — a meteorological subject that had become a quiet specialty of his shin-hanga landscapes.

title: Tabi miyage dai sanshū
description: The five timber arches of the Kintai Bridge in Suo Province span the Nishiki River, a famed seventeenth-century engineering feat. Hasui presents the structure in shin-hanga woodblock, foregrounding its rhythmic silhouette against riverbank pines.

title: Enbu by Hayami Gyoshu
description: Black-winged moths spiral upward through tongues of flame, set against a ground of deep ink. Hayami fused traditional nihonga pigments with the decorative geometry of Rinpa, producing an emblem of Taishō-era modernism now held in the Yamatane Museum.

title: Irises
description: Identificatie Titel(s): Irissen Objecttype: prent Objectnummer: RP-P-1999-553 Opschriften / Merken: verzamelaarsmerk, verso, gestempeld: Lugt 2228 Omschrijving: Bloeiende irissen tegen een zachte achtergrond in rood, wit en blauw.

title: Kawase Zôjôji
description: View of the Tokugawa mortuary temple Zōjō-ji at Shiba, from the Twenty Views of Tokyo series. A solitary figure shelters under a paper umbrella as snow falls against the vermilion gate, a hallmark of Kawase's quiet, weather-soaked urban landscapes.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: Dawn over the harbor of Mihogaseki in former Izumo Province on the Sea of Japan, rendered in cool grey-blues warming toward the horizon. From Selected Views of Japan, the print typifies Hasui's attention to specific times of day.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: Nezame-no-Toko, the celebrated stretch of pale, water-sculpted rock along the Kiso River in Nagano. Hasui treats the famous gorge with crisp green water and bleached stone, placing the site within his Selected Views of Japan.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A view of Suhara, a post-station village along the old Nakasendo highway in the Kiso valley. Within Selected Views of Japan, Hasui sets clustered roofs against the surrounding mountains in his customary flat planes of color.

title: Siberian Blue Nightingale by a Peony beneath a Snow-laden Sheaf
description: Identificatie Titel(s): Siberische Blauwe Nachtegaal bij een pioenroos onder een besneeuwde schoof Objecttype: prent Objectnummer: RP-P-2001-731 Opschriften / Merken: verzamelaarsmerk, verso, gestempeld: Lugt 2228 Omschrijving: Bloeiende roze pioenroos onder met sneeuw bedekte rieten afscherming, waarop een groen vogeltje; tegen zwarte achtergrond.

title: Tabi miyage dai sanshū
description: A coastal view of Futomi in Awa Province from the Souvenirs of Travel III series. Hasui captures the meeting of village rooftops and sea through carefully calibrated woodblock gradations, a hallmark of the shin-hanga revival of landscape printing.

title: The Grand Canyon
description: The South Rim falls away into vast layered cliffs flushed with desert light, a Japanese sensibility brought to the American Southwest. The print belongs to Yoshida's United States series, made after his sketching tour abroad, and uses successive overprintings to suggest the canyon's atmospheric haze.

title: The Zojoji Shrine at Shiba
description: A woman beneath a paper umbrella crosses a snowy courtyard before the vermilion gate of the Zōjō-ji temple in Shiba. From Hasui's Twenty Views of Tokyo, the print exemplifies the shin-hanga collaboration between artist, carver and printer in capturing weather as atmosphere.

title: Two Cockatoos on a Branch with Plum Blossom
description: Identificatie Titel(s): Twee kaketoes op tak met pruimenbloesem Objecttype: prent Objectnummer: RP-P-2005-472 Catalogusreferentie: Newland 36-3. Opmerking: S Opschriften / Merken: verzamelaarsmerk, verso, gestempeld: Lugt 2228 Vervaardiging Vervaardiger: prentmaker: Ohara Koson (vermeld op object), uitgever: Watanabe Shôzaburô Plaats vervaardiging: Tokyo Datering: 1925 - 1936 Fysieke kenmerken: kleurenhoutsnedelijnblok in zwart met kleurblokken Materiaal: papier Techniek: kleurenhoutsnede Afmetingen: blad: h 137 mm × b 88 mm Verwerving en rechten Credit line: Schenking van de heer J.

title: Apotheosis of the Slavs
description: The closing canvas of the Slav Epic, in which Mucha gathers the history of the Slavic peoples into an allegorical apotheosis culminating in a youthful figure of independence. The cycle of twenty monumental paintings was his self-declared life's work, given to the city of Prague.

provenance.collection: museum collection of the Prague City Gallery

title: Clearing Sky after Snow
description: A plate from Hasui's Twenty Views of Tokyo, depicting the Sensō-ji temple complex at Asakusa under fresh snow. A leading figure of the shin-hanga revival, Hasui rebuilt the older ukiyo-e collaboration of designer, carver, and printer to record a modernising city in soft, atmospheric colour woodblock.

title: dahlias
description: A garden subject from Monet's Argenteuil period, when his suburban plot became a recurring laboratory for floral colour and dappled shade. Thick, comma-shaped strokes register each bloom as a discrete patch of pigment rather than a botanical specimen.

title: Hiroshi Yoshida, Hikaru umi, 1926
description: Two sailing junks lean before the wind on the dazzled water of the Inland Sea. From the Setonaikai series, the print is one of six in which Yoshida had identical blocks printed in different lighting keys, demonstrating his mastery of multi-impression woodblock colour.

title: Hodakayama
description: From Yoshida's Twelve Scenes in the Japan Alps, this view of Hotaka-dake records the artist's own mountaineering observations of the Northern Alps. Yoshida combined ukiyo-e block techniques with Western tonal modeling, often printing the same blocks in different palettes to capture changing light.

title: Ice Floes at Bougival
description: A frigid winter scene of the Seine clogged with drift ice near Bougival, where Monet lived in straitened circumstances. Pale lavenders and chalky greys describe the freeze, his brush dragged dryly to suggest the glassy plates of broken ice.

provenance.collection: Department of Paintings of the Louvre

title: Kanae Yamamoto
description: A view at the foot of Mount Tokkō in oils, painted after Yamamoto's return from Europe where he absorbed Post-Impressionist palette and brushwork. Better remembered for woodblock prints, he pursued painting alongside his advocacy for the children's free-drawing and farmers' art movements in interwar Japan.

title: Kōjaku cavern at Oga Peninsula
description: A sea cave on the Oga Peninsula renders blue-green water against weathered rock, from Hasui's third Souvenirs of Travel series. The artist toured rural Japan compulsively, and these prints document a coastline that motorised tourism was just beginning to reach.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Kumoi-Zakura (Kumoi Cherry Trees)
description: Pale clouds of Kumoi cherry blossom hang over a Japanese garden in the soft tonal gradations Yoshida coaxed from up to thirty separate woodblock impressions. Trained in Western oil painting before turning to shin-hanga, he supervised every stage of carving and printing personally.
credit: 2wHA6qvg4l6i2g at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Monet w1668
description: From the painter's final year, when cataracts and a programme of cataract surgery transformed his colour. The picture belongs to the freer, near-abstract late manner that prefigures mid-century painting and crowns the lifelong project of the Giverny garden.

title: Nihon fūkei senshū
description: A view of Kiyomizu Temple at Yasugi in former Izumo Province, the wooden hall set among tall cedars. Within Selected Views of Japan, Hasui treats the shaded temple precinct with the carefully bokashi-graded skies of shin-hanga printmaking.

title: Ochanomizu
description: From Hasui's Twenty Views of Tokyo, this woodblock print shows wet snow muffling the rooftops along the Kanda River at Ochanomizu. The artist was renowned for his subtle differentiation of snowfall, here rendered as soft cotton-like accumulation.

title: Ryuga Island
description: A cliff-bound view of Ryūga Island off the Oga Peninsula in Akita, from Hasui's third Souvenirs of Travel set. Pine-fringed rocks rise from a deep cobalt sea, printed with the multiple bokashi gradations that gave shin-hanga its photographic depth.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Sailing Boats
description: From the Inland Sea series of woodblock prints, this view shows fishing junks under afternoon light. Yoshida printed the same key block in successive variants from morning to night, a shin-hanga experiment in atmosphere achieved by altering only the inking and color blocks.

title: Sailing Boats
description: Junks ride a glassy sea under a low evening sun in this version of Yoshida's six-state Sailing Boats suite. The warm orange ground and silhouetted sails show how shin-hanga printers could transform a single composition through color alone.

title: Sailing Boats
description: Sailing junks drift in a pale, fog-bound sea, their masts dissolving into the haze. One of six time-of-day variants printed from a single key block, this state demonstrates Yoshida's command of bokashi gradations and his shin-hanga sensitivity to weather and air.

title: Sailing Boats
description: Boats glide on still water under the cool tones of dawn, the mountains of the Seto Inland Sea faint on the horizon. Yoshida printed the work in six time-of-day variations, a virtuoso shin-hanga study in the chromatic rendering of light.

title: Sailing Boats
description: The darkest of Yoshida's six Sailing Boats variants, the scene is steeped in deep indigo with the silhouettes of junks just visible against a moonlit sea. The print shows shin-hanga craftsmen working color and gradation to evoke night without losing the woodblock's clarity.

title: Scops Owl
description: A small owl perches on a flowering cherry branch silhouetted against a full moon, in the kachō-e bird-and-flower tradition. Koson's woodblock combines soft tonal printing with a quiet, almost photographic stillness aimed at early-twentieth-century export markets.

title: Self-Portrait with Palette
description: A late self-portrait in which Munch shows himself frontally before the easel, palette in hand, the face hollowed by age and frank scrutiny. Painting himself was a lifelong project for the artist, charting the shifts of his temperament as openly as any of his symbolic figures.

title: Tabi miyage dai sanshū
description: A view of the Gozanoishi Shrine on the wooded shore of Lake Tazawa, Japan's deepest lake. The print uses Hasui's signature still-water reflections and bokashi-shaded skies to anchor the small red torii within an expansive landscape.

title: The Dinner
description: Camille and the infant Jean sit at the candlelit family table while a guest leans in from the doorway, the room warmed by lamp glow on porcelain and pewter. An early experiment with artificial light and intimate genre, painted at Étretat during a precarious winter for the young household.

title: Tōkyō nijū kei
description: The grounds of the Kanda Myojin shrine, with worshippers passing beneath the vermilion gate. From Twenty Views of Tokyo, the design pairs precise carpentry detail with the soft snow effects that became a Hasui signature.

title: Tōkyō nijū kei
description: The steel girders of the Shin Ohashi bridge over the Sumida River, rendered through slanting rain and a darkening sky. Hasui treats modern Tokyo infrastructure with the lyrical atmospherics of traditional landscape woodblock printing.

title: Tour Eiffel
description: Delaunay returned to the Eiffel Tower repeatedly across his career, fragmenting its iron lattice into prismatic planes of colour. This late oil sets the monument against tilting Parisian rooftops, channelling the Orphic Cubism he developed with Sonia Delaunay into a near-abstract hymn to modern engineering.

title: Vase of Tulips
description: Vase of Tulips
credit: Claude Monet - Vase of Tulips , Irina , 2014-05-29 23:25

title: yellow-irises-1917
description: A late Giverny study of yellow flag irises, painted as Monet's eyesight clouded with cataracts and his palette grew correspondingly hot and saturated. The flowers swim in loose, near-abstract bands of green and gold, anticipating the decorative scale of the Grandes Décorations.

title: Hachirōgata Lagoon
description: A view of Akita's vast Hachirōgata Lagoon from the shin-hanga series Souvenirs of Travel III. Hasui's quiet woodblock composition relies on flat washes of muted color and a low horizon to evoke the stillness of the inland water at dusk.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Kameidô
description: The arched drum bridge of Kameido Tenjin shrine reflects in the still pond below, surrounded by spring wisteria. Yoshida revisits a motif made famous by Hiroshige, but rebuilds it with the soft tonal gradations and calibrated overprinting of the shin-hanga revival.

title: Karasu Marsh
description: Karasu Marsh in Akita rendered as still water and reed beds under a quiet sky, from Hasui's third Souvenirs of Travel set. The reduced palette and emphasis on horizon embody the contemplative restraint that defines his shin-hanga landscapes.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Kawase Tenno-ji-27
description: Plate from the Souvenirs of Travel III series, depicting the precincts of the ancient Shitennō-ji temple in Osaka. Kawase's shin-hanga method preserves Edo-period block-cutting techniques while indulging Western sensibilities for soft light and weather, here a damp, lantern-lit dusk.

title: Morning on the Daikon Wharf
description: Daybreak over the Daikon vegetable wharf on the Sumida, drawn from Hasui's series Twenty Views of Tōkyō. The print is characteristic shin-hanga: traditional ukiyo-e workshop methods adapted to atmospheric, Western-influenced views of a modernising city.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Akashi-chō after Rain
description: A wet street in the Akashi-chō district after rain, lamplight pooling on the pavement. Hasui, working with the publisher Watanabe Shōzaburō, was the master of these reflective night and weather scenes that defined the shin-hanga revival of Japanese woodblock printing.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Dawn at Beppu
description: Morning rises over the hot-spring town of Beppu in Kyushu, with steam and hills pressed into a tight horizon. Hasui's bokashi gradations carry the cool light of dawn, a signature effect of his shin-hanga landscapes.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Hōrai Rock
description: A scene of the towering Hōrai Rock rising from the Kiso River, drawn from Hasui's Souvenirs of Travel III. The print balances the cliff's bulk against soft atmospheric washes, a hallmark of the shin-hanga revival of traditional landscape woodblock.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Kamezaki in Owari Province
description: A coastal view of the fishing port of Kamezaki in former Owari Province. Hasui frames boats and shoreline buildings under wide skies, using restrained palette and bokashi shading to render the still atmosphere of an Aichi inlet.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Kiyomizu-dō Temple in Ueno
description: The Kiyomizu Hall in Ueno Park seen across blossoming trees, from Hasui's Twenty Views of Tōkyō. The shin-hanga design preserves the planar colour fields of the woodblock tradition while introducing softened, plein-air light.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Nishi Park
description: Fukuoka's Nishi Park rendered in the shin-hanga manner, with figures threading through trees and lanterns. Hasui's design uses overlapping woodblock impressions to suggest dappled light and the casual pace of urban leisure in early Shōwa Japan.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Rapids at the Upper Reaches of Tone River
description: White water churns over boulders in a narrow gorge, with overhanging pines describing the steep banks. Yoshida, trained as a Western-style oil painter before turning to woodblock, achieves the river's translucent depth through dozens of hand-pulled impressions in a single block-printed image.

title: Setting Sun
description: Sun setting over the rooftops of Ichinokura at Ikegami, from Hasui's series Twenty Views of Tōkyō. The shin-hanga print uses long horizontal bands of graded orange and indigo to fix a single, melancholic moment of urban dusk.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Starlit Night at Miyajima
description: From the shin-hanga series Souvenirs of Travel III, this nocturne shows the floating torii of Itsukushima beneath a star-pricked sky. Hasui pairs deep indigo bokashi gradations with sparse highlights to register the hush of Miyajima after dark.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Yoshida Komagatake
description: Ridges of the Southern Japanese Alps recede in cool blues from the summit of Komagatake, a vantage informed by the artist's lifelong mountaineering. Yoshida's woodblock layering produces the thinning air and dry granite light that he sought in his alpine series.

title: Evening in Beppu
description: Evening settles over the Beppu coast in this Souvenirs of Travel III sheet. Hasui anchors the composition with darkened silhouettes against a dusky sky, exploiting the shin-hanga technique of bokashi to dissolve land into water.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Kamisuki
description: A bijin-ga showing a young woman drawing a comb through her loosened black hair, her undergarment slipping from one shoulder. Kotondo revives the intimate vanity scenes of Edo-period printmaking within the early Showa shin-hanga movement.
credit: Walters Art Museum : Home page Info about artwork

title: Shinobazu Pond in Rain
description: Rain falls across Shinobazu Pond in Ueno, the lotus leaves and far shore softened to muted greys and greens. Hasui's mastery of bokashi gradation and overprinted rain-lines made him the foremost printmaker of weather in twentieth-century Japan.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Takinogawa River
description: From the series Twenty Views of Tōkyō, this print follows the Takinogawa River as it winds through suburban greenery. Hasui captures a quieter periphery of the modernizing capital, layering subtle woodblock washes for foliage and reflected water.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: The Pond at Benten Shrine in Shiba
description: A shin-hanga woodblock from Hasui's Tokyo Twenty Views, showing the red-railed bridge to the Benten shrine reflected in still water at twilight. Layered bokashi gradations and crisp keyblock lines exemplify the Watanabe workshop's revival of ukiyo-e technique.

title: American Gothic
description: A stern Iowa farmer and his daughter stand before a white frame house with a Carpenter Gothic window, the pitchfork echoing the seams of his overalls. Wood used his sister Nan and his dentist as models, fashioning a dry, hard-edged image that became shorthand for rural America.
credit: 5QEPm0jCc183Aw at Google Cultural Institute maximum zoom level

title: Moon at Magome
description: Part of Twenty Views of Tōkyō, the print shows the moon hanging over the rural fringes of Magome. Hasui builds the night scene from layered indigo blocks and a single luminous disc, demonstrating shin-hanga's debt to classical Edo landscape traditions.
credit: National Diet Library Digital Collections: Persistent ID 2586549

title: Nikkō Kaidō
